# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any three serial communication standards used to connect a PLC to a computer. State one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **RS-232:** A single-ended, point-to-point standard used for short-distance (up to 15 m) connections, typically between a PC and one PLC.
2. **RS-485:** A differential, multi-drop standard used for long-distance (up to 1200 m) connections, supporting up to 32 devices on a single bus.
3. **USB (Universal Serial Bus):** A high-speed, plug-and-play interface used for temporary laptop-to-PLC connections, supporting up to 480 Mbps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Students often confuse RS-232 and RS-485. The key distinguishing features are *single-ended vs differential*, *point-to-point vs multi-drop*, and *short vs long distance*. Always mention all three when comparing.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "forcing" of an I/O point in a PLC? Why is it used during troubleshooting?**

**Model Answer:**

*Forcing* is the act of **manually overriding** an input or output to a fixed state (ON or OFF), irrespective of the actual field signal or the program logic, through the programming software in *online mode*.

**It is used during troubleshooting to:**
- Verify that a field device (sensor or actuator) is functional by bypassing the program.
- Isolate whether a fault is in the *field wiring/device* or in the *PLC I/O module*.
- Test a specific rung of logic without energising the entire machine.

> [!WARNING]
> **Forcing is dangerous in running plants.** It must be used **only by authorised personnel in test mode** with the machine safely isolated. Forcing an output in a running system can cause unexpected machine motion and serious injury.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 1 Internal Choice

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the different methods used to connect a PLC to a personal computer. Compare any two methods in terms of distance, speed, and noise immunity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the three systematic strategies used for PLC troubleshooting. Describe the diagnostic LED indicators typically provided on a PLC CPU module and their meanings. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION

#### (a) Methods of Connecting a PLC to a Computer (7 marks)

The PLC can be connected to a personal computer by the following methods:

**1. Direct Serial Connection (RS-232 / RS-422):**
A single PC is connected directly to the PLC's programming port via a serial cable (e.g., Siemens PPI Multi-Master cable, DB-9 connector). This is the simplest method, used for *programming and small-scale monitoring*.

**2. Multi-Drop RS-485 Network:**
Multiple PLCs are daisy-chained on an RS-485 bus, and the PC is the master. The PC can communicate with any PLC by addressing it. This is used in *SCADA and distributed control* systems.

**3. Industrial Ethernet (Profinet, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP):**
The PLC and PC are connected via a network switch, enabling high-speed, multi-device, plant-wide communication.

**4. USB Connection:**
Used for short-term, plug-and-play connection with a laptop running the programming software. Limited to ~5 m.

**5. Wireless (Wi-Fi or Bluetooth):**
Used where cabling is impractical, e.g., mobile equipment or rotating machinery.

**Comparison Table (any two):**

| Parameter | RS-232 | RS-485 |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Maximum Distance | 15 m | 1200 m |
| Max Speed | 115.2 kbps | 10 Mbps |
| Noise Immunity | Low (single-ended) | Very High (differential) |
| Multi-Drop | No | Yes (up to 32 nodes) |

---

#### (b) Troubleshooting Strategies and LED Indicators (7 marks)

**Three Systematic Strategies:**

1. **Top-Down:** Start at the system level (overall symptom) and progressively narrow to the faulty component. Best for *system-wide* faults.
2. **Bottom-Up:** Start at the field level (sensors, actuators, wiring) and move up to CPU. Best for *suspected field* faults.
3. **Divide-and-Conquer (Half-Split):** Start in the middle of the suspected faulty chain. Check whether the fault is *above* or *below* the midpoint. Best for *communication or I/O* faults.

**Typical CPU Module Diagnostic LEDs:**

| LED | Color | Meaning |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **POWER / PWR** | Green | Power supply healthy |
| **RUN** | Green | CPU is in RUN mode, executing program |
| **STOP** | Yellow/Red | CPU is in STOP mode (program not executing) |
| **SF (System Fault)** | Red | CPU has detected a serious fault |
| **BF (Bus Fault)** | Red | Communication error with a slave I/O or device |
| **BATT / LOW BAT** | Red | Backup battery voltage is low |
| **LINK / ACT** | Green/Flashing | Ethernet link active, data traffic |
| **FORCE** | Yellow | At least one I/O is forced |

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the various categories of faults that can occur in a PLC system. Describe, with examples, how the programming software is used to diagnose and rectify these faults. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is meant by preventive maintenance of a PLC? Prepare a typical preventive maintenance schedule for an industrial PLC installation, listing at least eight activities with their recommended intervals. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION

#### (a) Categories of PLC Faults and Software-Based Diagnosis (7 marks)

**Categories of PLC Faults:**

1. **Power Supply Faults:** Mains failure, blown fuse, 24 V DC rail collapse.
2. **CPU Faults:** Memory checksum error, watchdog timeout, battery failure, processor overload.
3. **I/O Module Faults:** Failed input transistor, welded output relay, channel short circuit.
4. **Communication Faults:** Cable break, baud rate mismatch, station address conflict, EMI.
5. **Program / Software Faults:** Logic error, scan-time overrun, infinite loop, division by zero.
6. **Environmental Faults:** Over-temperature, dust ingress, humidity, vibration, corrosion.

**Use of Programming Software for Diagnosis:**

- **Online Monitoring:** The software connects to the PLC and shows *real-time status* of every input, output, and memory bit, allowing the engineer to verify whether the program is behaving as expected.
- **Status Words and Error Codes:** Most PLCs (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Schneider) expose diagnostic buffers accessible via the software. For example, Siemens Step 7's *Module Information* and *Diagnostic Buffer* show the *time, date, and nature* of every fault event.
- **Forcing of I/O:** As described earlier, this allows the engineer to bypass program logic and verify the I/O module and field device.
- **Cross-Reference / Watch Tables:** The software lists every instance of a memory bit used in the program, helping to identify *spurious* writes.
- **Trace and Trend Tools:** Capture scan-by-scan changes in a variable to identify intermittent faults.

**Example:** *If a motor fails to start*, the engineer uses the online software to:
- Check the input from the start pushbutton → if OFF, the pushbutton or wiring is at fault.
- Check the rung condition → if input is ON but rung is false, a permissive condition is missing.
- Force the output ON → if the motor still does not start, the output module, contactor, or motor is faulty.

---

#### (b) Preventive Maintenance of PLC (7 marks)

**Definition:**
Preventive maintenance (PM) is the **scheduled, routine servicing** of a PLC and its associated equipment to *prevent unexpected breakdowns* and *extend operational life*. It is based on manufacturer recommendations, MTBF data, and operating environment.

**Typical PM Schedule:**

| S.No. | Activity | Interval |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Visual inspection of all LEDs and displays | Daily |
| 2 | Check enclosure temperature and ventilation | Daily |
| 3 | Export and archive diagnostic logs | Weekly |
| 4 | Verify communication link health | Weekly |
| 5 | Clean/replace air filters in cabinet | Monthly |
| 6 | Inspect and tighten terminal connections | Quarterly |
| 7 | Check cable insulation and wear | Quarterly |
| 8 | Replace backup lithium battery | Annually |
| 9 | Insulation resistance test (Megger at 500 V DC) | Annually |
| 10 | Force-test each I/O point against field device | Annually |
| 11 | Backup program, HMI, and documentation to server | After every change |
| 12 | Review firmware and apply validated updates | As released |
| 13 | Measure cabinet ground resistance (must be < 1 Ω) | Annually |
| 14 | Verify scan time and CPU load trends | Monthly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Best Practice:** A small Indian auto-component plant reduced its annual PLC downtime by **42%** after moving from *corrective* to *preventive* maintenance. The investment in PM was recovered in less than 6 months.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RS-232 and RS-485** — students often interchange them. Memorise the key differences: RS-232 = point-to-point, 15 m, single-ended; RS-485 = multi-drop, 1200 m, differential.
> 2. **Forgetting termination resistors** — for RS-485, mention **120 Ω at both ends**. Students often place termination on *every* node — this is wrong and the examiner will deduct marks.
> 3. **In troubleshooting, students describe "trial and error"** — this is *not* an accepted strategy. Always cite one of the *three systematic* methods: top-down, bottom-up, or divide-and-conquer.
> 4. **Confusing "preventive" with "predictive" maintenance** — preventive is *calendar-based*; predictive is *condition-based* (using sensor data and analytics).
> 5. **Battery is for RAM, not for the program** — program is stored in flash/EEPROM. When the battery dies, *retentive data* (counters, timers, recipes) is lost; the *program* is preserved.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention safety** — never troubleshoot a live machine without proper lockout/tagout (LOTO) and authorisation.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PLC ↔ Computer link** requires three matches: **physical layer, protocol, and parameters** (baud rate, parity, station address).
- **RS-232** is for *short, point-to-point* programming; **RS-485** is for *long, multi-drop* industrial networks; **Industrial Ethernet** is for *modern, high-speed* plant-wide communication.
- **Termination resistor** = **120 Ω** at *both ends* of an RS-485 bus; **fail-safe biasing** = **680 Ω** pull-up/pull-down at *one* end (typically the master).
- **Forcing** is a powerful but dangerous troubleshooting tool — use only in *test mode* with proper LOTO.
- **Troubleshooting is systematic**, not random — apply *top-down, bottom-up, or divide-and-conquer*.
- **Diagnostic LEDs** (PWR, RUN, STOP, SF, BF, BATT, LINK, FORCE) are the first line of fault identification.
- **Online monitoring, status words, watch tables, and I/O forcing** are the four key software tools.
- **Preventive maintenance** is *calendar-based* and includes daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual tasks.
- **Battery** backs up *RAM (retentive data)* — not the program, which is in *flash/EEPROM*.
- **Industrial Ethernet segment** is **100 m**; **RS-485** is **1200 m**; **RS-232** is **15 m**; **USB** is **5 m**.
- **Industrial ambient** is **0 to 55 °C** and **5 to 95 % RH** non-condensing.
- Always **back up the program** after every change and **archive the diagnostic logs** weekly.
- **Grounding resistance** of the cabinet must be **< 1 Ω**.
- **Insulation resistance** of field wiring must be **> 1 MΩ** (tested at 500 V DC).
- **Always isolate and lock out** (LOTO) before performing any physical maintenance on a live system.
- **Document everything** — a maintenance log is a legal and operational necessity in any ISO 9001 / 14001 certified plant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming of Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Programming of Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) ladder logic programming, a **Timer** is a non-retentive (or retentive, depending on type) output instruction that controls an output based on a predefined time interval. The timer instruction monitors (counts) the elapsed time its input rung conditions remain true, and then changes the state of its output bits once the **Accumulated (ACC) value** equals the **Preset (PRE) value**.

According to the IEC 61131-3 standard adopted by KTU's PLC curriculum, timers are standard function blocks that operate on time-base logic to provide precise ON-delay, OFF-delay, or pulse-stretching functions in discrete control systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The three mandatory timer types studied in Module 2 are:
> 1. **TON — Timer ON-Delay (Non-Retentive)**
> 2. **TOF — Timer OFF-Delay (Non-Retentive)**
> 3. **RTO — Retentive Timer ON (Requires Reset Instruction RES)**

## 1.2 The Three Sub-Elements of a Timer Instruction

Every Allen-Bradley (AB) / Rockwell-style timer (used as the KTU reference standard) contains the following three registers:

| Sub-Element | Symbolic Notation | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Preset (PRE)** | `.PRE` | Target time value (set by programmer) |
| **Accumulated (ACC)** | `.ACC` | Current elapsed time (auto-incremented) |
| **Control Word / Status Bits** | `.EN`, `.TT`, `.DN` | Three Boolean flags describing the timer's state |

The **Control Word** is a 16-bit word that contains three critical status bits:
- **EN (Enable Bit):** Set to `1` whenever the input rung is TRUE.
- **TT (Timer-Timing Bit):** Set to `1` while the timer is actively counting (ACC < PRE).
- **DN (Done Bit):** Set to `1` when ACC ≥ PRE (timing complete).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Maggi Noodles" Analogy

Imagine you are cooking **Maggi noodles** on a gas stove:

- You set a **kitchen timer for 5 minutes** (this is your **PRESET** = 5).
- As water boils, the timer's seconds count up (this is the **ACCUMULATED** value rising).
- While the timer is counting, a small red LED glows on the timer body (this is the **TT bit**).
- When 5 minutes elapse, the timer beeps loudly (this is the **DN bit** becoming `1`).
- If you switch off the stove at any point, the timer's `ACC` resets to `0` (this is the behavior of a **non-retentive** timer like TON/TOF).
- However, if you use a stopwatch and pause it when the power fails, then resume — that is a **retentive** timer (RTO).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Example:** A conveyor belt in a bottling plant must run for exactly 3 seconds after a bottle passes a photoelectric sensor to allow the cap to be screwed on. A **TON timer with PRE = 3.0s** implements this exact logic. The DN bit energizes the capping solenoid precisely when timing completes.

## 1.4 Time Base Concept (KTU High-Yield Definition)

The **Time Base (TB)** is the smallest unit of time the timer can resolve. The timer's clock ticks at this resolution, and the **ACC** increments by 1 unit per tick.

$$T_{\text{delay}} = T_{\text{base}} \times \text{PRE}$$

For the KTU standard PLC-5 / SLC 500 platform, the three permissible time bases are:

- **0.01 seconds** (1 centi-second) — for sub-second precision
- **0.10 seconds** (1 deci-second) — for medium delays
- **1.00 seconds** — for long delays (up to 32,767 seconds ≈ 9.1 hours)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of TON (Timer ON-Delay)

The **TON** instruction is the most frequently examined timer in KTU papers. Its operational logic is governed by the following structured steps:

**Step 1 — Input Evaluation:** The PLC scans the rung. If the preceding logic is `TRUE`, the timer is **enabled (EN = 1)**.

**Step 2 — Timing Initiation:** With EN = 1, the timer's **TT bit is set to 1** and the **ACC starts incrementing** at the rate of $1/T_{\text{base}}$ per second.

**Step 3 — Completion Detection:** When ACC ≥ PRE, the **DN bit transitions from 0 → 1** and the **TT bit transitions from 1 → 0**.

**Step 4 — Reset on Loss of Input:** The instant the input rung becomes `FALSE` (false), **ACC is automatically cleared to 0**, and all status bits (EN, TT, DN) are reset to `0`. This is why it is called **non-retentive**.

### ASCII Ladder Logic Representation of TON

```
      [XIC Input]       [TON  T4:1       ]
      |                |                |
   ---|  |-------------|                |
                      |   Time Base: 1.0|
                      |   Preset   : 5  |
                      |   Accum    : 0  |
                      ------------------
                              |
                              T4:1/DN ----[OTE Output]
```

## 2.2 Operational Logic of TOF (Timer OFF-Delay)

The **TOF** is the dual opposite of TON — it delays the **turning OFF** of an output:

**Step 1 — Immediate Activation:** The instant the input rung becomes `TRUE`, the **DN bit immediately becomes 1** (no delay on turn-on).

**Step 2 — Timing on Turn-Off:** When the input transitions from `TRUE → FALSE`, the **TT bit becomes 1** and **ACC starts incrementing**.

**Step 3 — Delay Completion:** When ACC ≥ PRE, the **DN bit finally transitions to 0** (turn-off is delayed by PRE seconds).

**Step 4 — Reset Behavior:** Once DN = 0, ACC is reset to 0 and the timer is dormant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Use Case:** Cooling fans in industrial furnaces must run for 60 seconds AFTER the heater is turned off, to dissipate residual heat. A **TOF with PRE = 60s** implements this exact "after-stop" delay.

## 2.3 Operational Logic of RTO (Retentive Timer ON)

The **RTO** behaves identically to a TON during the timing phase, **but with one critical difference**: when the input rung goes false, **ACC retains its last value** (it is not reset).

- The **only** way to reset the RTO is via a separate **RES (Reset) instruction** with the same address.
- This is essential for **totalizing run-time** applications, e.g., tracking total hours a motor has been in service.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Equation | Units | Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Delay Time** | $T_{\text{delay}}$ | $T_{\text{base}} \times \text{PRE}$ | seconds (s) | $0 \leq T_{\text{delay}} \leq 32{,}767$ |
| **Preset (Integer Form)** | $\text{PRE}$ | $T_{\text{delay}} / T_{\text{base}}$ | integer count | $0 \leq \text{PRE} \leq 32{,}767$ |
| **Accumulated Rate** | $dACC/dt$ | $1 / T_{\text{base}}$ | counts/sec | Discrete (digital PLC) |
| **Max Delay (TB=1s)** | $T_{\text{max}}$ | $1 \times 32{,}767$ | seconds | $\approx 9.10$ hours |
| **Max Delay (TB=0.1s)** | $T_{\text{max}}$ | $0.1 \times 32{,}767$ | seconds | $\approx 54.6$ minutes |
| **Max Delay (TB=0.01s)** | $T_{\text{max}}$ | $0.01 \times 32{,}767$ | seconds | $\approx 5.46$ minutes |
| **Time Base Selector** | $T_{\text{base}}$ | $1.0$ or $0.1$ or $0.01$ | seconds | Discrete (3 choices) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Siemens S7 Time Format (KTU Module Note):** Siemens PLCs use a BCD-coded S5T# format, e.g., `S5T#5s` = 5 sec, `S5T#2M30S` = 2 min 30 sec. The base unit is **0.01 s** and the maximum is `S5T#9990s` (2H_46M_30S).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Timers are the workhorse of industrial automation. They are deployed in:
- **Conveyor sequencing** (bottling, packaging, sorting).
- **Star-Delta motor starters** (delay between star and delta contactors).
- **Traffic light controllers** (green → yellow → red transitions).
- **Process safety** (cool-down delays, purge timers, lubrication intervals).
- **Batch process control** (mixing time, reaction time, settling time).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: Preset Calculation for a Given Time Delay

**Problem Statement:** A water tank filling process requires a pump to run for **2 minutes and 30 seconds** after a level switch closes. The PLC timer is configured with a time base of **0.1 s**. Calculate the integer value to be loaded into the **PRESET (PRE)** register.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert the desired delay into the SI unit (seconds).**

$$T_{\text{delay}} = 2\,\text{min} \times 60\,\frac{\text{s}}{\text{min}} + 30\,\text{s}$$

$$T_{\text{delay}} = 120\,\text{s} + 30\,\text{s} = 150\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU timer equation to solve for PRE.**

$$T_{\text{delay}} = T_{\text{base}} \times \text{PRE}$$

Rearranging for PRE:

$$\text{PRE} = \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{base}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known numerical values.**

$$\text{PRE} = \frac{150\,\text{s}}{0.1\,\text{s}} = 1500$$

**Step 4 — Verify the calculation by reverse-computing the delay.**

$$T_{\text{delay,verify}} = 0.1 \times 1500 = 150\,\text{s} = 2\,\text{min}\,30\,\text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** The programmer must enter **PRE = 1500** in the timer instruction.

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: Time Base Selection for Maximum Resolution

**Problem Statement:** A chemical reaction requires a hold time of exactly **8.4 seconds**. Select the optimal time base and compute the PRE value such that the achieved delay is exactly 8.4 s (no rounding error).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Enumerate the three permissible time bases and check divisibility.**

For $T_{\text{delay}} = 8.4\,\text{s}$:

- TB = 1.0 s: PRE = 8.4 → **not an integer** ❌
- TB = 0.1 s: PRE = 84.0 → **integer** ✓
- TB = 0.01 s: PRE = 840.0 → **integer** ✓

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU rule of "optimal time base" — choose the largest TB that yields an integer PRE to minimize scan-time overhead.**

Since TB = 0.1 s already gives PRE = 84 (integer), we select **TB = 0.1 s**.

**Step 3 — Compute the PRE formally.**

$$\text{PRE} = \frac{8.4\,\text{s}}{0.1\,\text{s}} = 84$$

**Step 4 — Validate.**

$$T_{\text{verify}} = 0.1 \times 84 = 8.4\,\text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** Use **TB = 0.1 s** and **PRE = 84**.

## 3.3 Derivative Problem 3: Retentive Timer (RTO) Behavior Analysis

**Problem Statement:** An RTO timer has PRE = 100, TB = 1.0 s. The input rung is TRUE for 40 s, FALSE for 30 s, TRUE for 35 s, FALSE for 20 s, then a RES instruction is executed. Sketch the ACC and DN bit waveforms.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the time-base resolution and target.**

Each clock tick = 1.0 s. DN will go high when ACC first reaches 100.

**Step 2 — Phase 1: First TRUE interval (0 s to 40 s).**

ACC rises from 0 → 40 in 40 s. Since 40 < 100, DN = 0. TT = 1 throughout. EN = 1.

**Step 3 — Phase 2: FALSE interval (40 s to 70 s).**

Per RTO retentive behavior, **ACC freezes at 40** (does NOT reset). DN = 0, TT = 0, EN = 0.

**Step 4 — Phase 3: Second TRUE interval (70 s to 105 s).**

ACC resumes from 40 and rises to 100 exactly at t = 100 s (i.e., 30 s into this phase). At t = 100 s: DN transitions 0 → 1, TT transitions 1 → 0. ACC then continues to climb to 65 (i.e., 40 + 35) by t = 105 s.

**Step 5 — Phase 4: Second FALSE interval (105 s to 125 s).**

RTO retains ACC = 65. DN = 1, EN = 0, TT = 0.

**Step 6 — Phase 5: RES instruction executes at t = 125 s.**

ACC is forced to 0. DN is forced to 0. Timer is fully reset.

**Final Answer (Tabulated):**

| Time (s) | 0–40 | 40–70 | 70–100 | 100–105 | 105–125 | ≥125 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input | TRUE | FALSE | TRUE | TRUE | FALSE | — |
| ACC | 0→40 | 40 (held) | 40→100 | 100→65* | 65 (held) | 0 (after RES) |
| EN | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| TT | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| DN | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

\*Note: ACC for an RTO does NOT stop incrementing once PRE is reached — it continues counting upward. Only DN transitions state.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TON vs TOF vs RTO Comparison

| Feature | TON | TOF | RTO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Timer ON-Delay | Timer OFF-Delay | Retentive Timer ON |
| **Timing Trigger** | Input goes TRUE | Input goes FALSE | Input goes TRUE |
| **Output Turns ON** | After PRE delay | Immediately | After PRE delay |
| **Output Turns OFF** | Immediately when input FALSE | After PRE delay | Only via RES instruction |
| **ACC Reset** | Automatic (on input false) | Automatic (on DN = 0) | **Manual via RES only** |
| **Retentive?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Reset Instruction Needed?** | No | No | **Yes (mandatory)** |
| **Typical Use** | Delayed start | Delayed stop | Totalizing runtime |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timer Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RungInput[Input Rung Condition] --> ENCheck{EN Check}
    ENCheck -->|TRUE| TimeBase[Time Base Clock Oscillator]
    TimeBase --> ACCUp[ACC Increments at 1/TB rate]
    ACCUp --> Compare{Compare ACC vs PRE}
    Compare -->|ACC less than PRE| TTbit[TT bit = 1]
    Compare -->|ACC greater than or equal PRE| DNbit[DN bit = 1]
    ENCheck -->|FALSE| ResetACC[Reset ACC to 0]
    ResetACC --> ClearBits[Clear EN, TT, DN to 0]
    TTbit --> OutputLogic[Output Logic to Ladder]
    DNbit --> OutputLogic
    ClearBits --> OutputLogic
```

## 4.2 TON Timing Waveform Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Input as Input Rung
    participant EN as EN Bit
    participant ACC as ACC Value
    participant TT as TT Bit
    participant DN as DN Bit
    participant Output as Output OTE

    Note over Input,Output: Time axis: t = 0 to 10 seconds, TB = 1s, PRE = 5
    Input->>EN: t=0, Input goes TRUE
    EN->>ACC: EN=1, start counting
    Note over ACC: ACC: 0,1,2,3,4...
    ACC->>TT: TT=1 (timing in progress)
    Note over Output: Output OFF (DN=0)
    Input->>EN: t=5, ACC reaches 5
    EN->>DN: DN=1 (timing complete)
    DN->>TT: TT=0
    DN->>Output: Output turns ON
    Input->>EN: t=7, Input goes FALSE
    EN->>ACC: ACC reset to 0
    EN->>DN: DN=0
    DN->>Output: Output turns OFF
```

## 4.3 RTO Reset Logic Flow (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageA[Stage A - Input Monitoring]
        A1[Photo Sensor Input XIC] --> A2[Rung Logic Evaluator]
    end
    subgraph StageB[Stage B - RTO Timing Core]
        A2 --> B1{RTO T4:5}
        B1 --> B2[Time Base 0.1s]
        B2 --> B3[ACC Register]
        B3 --> B4{PRE Check}
    end
    subgraph StageC[Stage C - Status Flags]
        B4 --> C1[EN Bit 15]
        B4 --> C2[TT Bit 14]
        B4 --> C3[DN Bit 13]
    end
    subgraph StageD[Stage D - Reset Path]
        D1[Manual Pushbutton PB1 XIC] --> D2[RES Instruction T4:5]
        D2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph StageE[Stage E - Output Actuation]
        C3 --> E1[Output OTE Motor_Start]
        C2 --> E2[Indicator Lamp OTE Timing_Active]
    end
```

## 4.4 Star-Delta Starter with TON — Industrial Application Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Pushbutton NO] --> Stop{Stop PB NC}
    Stop --> Contactor[Main Contactor Coil K1]
    Contactor --> StarTimer[TON T1 - 5 second delay]
    StarTimer --> StarContactor[Star Contactor Coil K2]
    StarTimer -->|After 5s| DeltaContactor[Delta Contactor Coil K3]
    StarContactor -.Interlock.-> DeltaContactor
    DeltaContactor -.Interlock.-> StarContactor
    K1 --> Motor[3 Phase Induction Motor]
    K2 --> Motor
    K3 --> Motor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The TON with PRE = 5 s in this star-delta starter ensures the motor first connects in star (low voltage) for 5 seconds, then transitions to delta (full voltage) for normal running. This limits the inrush current to ~1/3 of the direct-on-line value.
