# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the function of the TT (Timer-Timing) bit in a PLC timer instruction. How does it differ from the EN and DN bits?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The TT bit is a status flag within the timer's control word that remains **logic 1 (TRUE)** for the entire duration the timer is actively counting — i.e., from the moment the input rung enables the timer until the Accumulated value equals the Preset value. It is commonly used to indicate "timing in progress" on HMI panels or to drive a blinking indicator lamp.

| Bit | Function |
|---|---|
| **EN (Enable)** | Set when input rung is TRUE; cleared when input is FALSE |
| **TT (Timer-Timing)** | Set while ACC is actively incrementing (ACC < PRE) |
| **DN (Done)** | Set when ACC ≥ PRE; cleared when timer resets |

The TT bit is essentially the "in-progress" signal, while EN indicates the timer's input status and DN indicates completion. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the RES (Reset) instruction mandatory only for RTO timers and not for TON timers?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The RTO (Retentive Timer ON) is designed to **retain its Accumulated value** even when the input rung goes FALSE — this retentive behavior is what makes it useful for totalizing runtime. Without a manual reset, the ACC would remain latched forever, and the DN bit would never clear. Therefore, a separate **RES instruction addressing the same timer file** must be programmed to forcibly zero out the ACC and clear the control bits.

In contrast, the TON is **non-retentive** — its ACC is automatically reset to 0 the instant the input rung goes FALSE. Hence, no external reset is required; the timer's internal logic handles reset automatically. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the three types of timer instructions — TON, TOF, and RTO — used in PLC ladder logic programming. Compare their operational behavior with the help of timing waveform diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical mixing tank requires an agitator motor to run for **2 minutes 15 seconds** after the START pushbutton is pressed. The timer used has a time base of **0.1 second**. Calculate the required Preset (PRE) value. Develop the complete ladder logic program including an emergency STOP and a status indicator lamp that glows during the agitation period. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — TON, TOF, RTO Comparison (7 marks)

**TON (Timer ON-Delay):**
- Input TRUE → timing begins → after PRE seconds → output (DN) goes ON.
- Input FALSE → ACC automatically resets to 0, DN goes OFF immediately.
- **Use:** Delayed starting of equipment (e.g., delayed conveyor start).

**TOF (Timer OFF-Delay):**
- Input TRUE → output (DN) goes ON immediately (no delay on turn-on).
- Input FALSE → timing begins → after PRE seconds → output goes OFF.
- **Use:** Delayed stopping (e.g., cooling fan runs after heater stops).

**RTO (Retentive Timer ON):**
- Input TRUE → ACC increments.
- Input FALSE → ACC **retains** its value (does not reset).
- Reset only via **RES instruction** with same timer address.
- **Use:** Totalized runtime tracking (e.g., total operating hours of a machine).

**Timing Waveform Description (Tabulated):**

| Timer Type | Input TRUE | Input FALSE Transition | Output Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| TON | Delay ON | Immediate OFF | Output ON after delay |
| TOF | Immediate ON | Delay OFF | Output OFF after delay |
| RTO | Delay ON | Retain ACC | Output stays ON, manual reset needed |

**(7 marks awarded for: 3 marks for individual descriptions + 2 marks for waveform/tabulation + 2 marks for comparison & application examples)**

#### Part (b) — Preset Calculation and Ladder Logic (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert the desired delay to seconds.**

$$T_{\text{delay}} = 2 \times 60 + 15 = 120 + 15 = 135\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Preset (PRE) value.**

Given $T_{\text{base}} = 0.1\,\text{s}$:

$$\text{PRE} = \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{base}}} = \frac{135}{0.1} = 1350$$

**Step 3 — Ladder Logic Program:**

```
   Rung 1: Latching the Start Command
   |  [Start_PB]    [Stop_PB]                [Motor_Run]    |
   |  |  XIC   |---|  XIC   |---+---[ ]---+---(OTE)-------|
   |                          |  Motor_Run|                 |
   |                          +----[ ]----+                 |
   |                                Motor_Run (Seal-in)    |
   --------------------------------------------------------

   Rung 2: Starting the Agitation Timer
   |  [Motor_Run]              [TON  T4:1]                 |
   |  |   XIC   |-------------|   TB: 0.1s  |              |
   |                           |   PRE: 1350 |              |
   |                           |   ACC:    0 |              |
   -------------------------------------------------------

   Rung 3: Driving the Agitator Motor (coil interlocked with timer)
   |  [Motor_Run]                                       |
   |  |  XIC  |  [T4:1/EN]  [T4:1/DN]                  |
   |  |       |----[XIC]---+---[XIC]---+---(OTE Agitator)-
   |  |       |     EN     |    DN     |                 |
   ---------------------------------------------------------

   Rung 4: Status Indicator Lamp (glows during agitation)
   |  [T4:1/TT]                                          |
   |  |  XIC  |-------------------------(OTE LAMP)-------
   --------------------------------------------------------
```

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$T_{\text{verify}} = 0.1 \times 1350 = 135\,\text{s} = 2\,\text{min}\,15\,\text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

**(7 marks awarded for: 2 marks calculation + 3 marks ladder diagram + 1 mark verification + 1 mark for indicator lamp rung)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat ladder diagram, explain how a **TON timer** is used to implement an **automatic traffic light controller** for a single road intersection with Red (30 s) → Green (25 s) → Yellow (5 s) cycle. Specify the timer addresses and preset values assuming a 1 s time base. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RTO timer T4:10 is used to track the total ON-time of a motor. The timer has TB = 1.0 s and PRE = 3600. The motor input is TRUE for: 1500 s, then FALSE for 600 s, then TRUE for 2400 s. A RES instruction is executed at the end. Plot the waveform of the DN bit and calculate the total ON-time recorded. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Traffic Light Controller (7 marks)

**Preset values with TB = 1 s:**
- Red light ON duration = 30 s → T4:1 PRE = 30
- Green light ON duration = 25 s → T4:2 PRE = 25
- Yellow light ON duration = 5 s → T4:3 PRE = 5

**Sequential Ladder Logic (Cascaded TONs):**

```
   Rung 1: Master Run
   |  [Start] [Stop]                                 [Master_Run]
   |  |  XIC  |---| XIC |---+--------------------(OTE)----------
   |                       +----[ ]----+                      |
   |                          Master_Run (Seal)               |
   -----------------------------------------------------------------

   Rung 2: Red Light (ON initially, then 30 s)
   |  [Master_Run]    [T4:2/DN OR NOT T4:1/EN]   [T4:1]      [Red_Light]
   |  |   XIC   |----[   ]----------------------|   TON  |---(OTE)-----
   |                                            |  TB:1  |
   |                                            | PRE:30 |
   -----------------------------------------------------------------

   Rung 3: Green Light (after Red DN)
   |  [T4:1/DN]                      [T4:2]                [Green_Light]
   |  |  XIC  |----------------------|   TON  |------------(OTE)---------
   |                                |  TB:1  |
   |                                | PRE:25 |
   -----------------------------------------------------------------

   Rung 4: Yellow Light (after Green DN)
   |  [T4:2/DN]                      [T4:3]                [Yellow_Light]
   |  |  XIC  |----------------------|   TON  |------------(OTE)---------
   |                                |  TB:1  |
   |                                | PRE: 5 |
   -----------------------------------------------------------------

   Rung 5: Reset Cascading (restart cycle)
   |  [T4:3/DN]            [RES T4:1] [RES T4:2] [RES T4:3]
   |  |  XIC  |------------(OTE)------(OTE)------(OTE)-------
   -----------------------------------------------------------------
```

**Operational Sequence:** When Master_Run is TRUE, the Red light turns on immediately and T4:1 starts counting. After 30 s, T4:1/DN goes high, energizing the Green light and starting T4:2. After 25 s, T4:2/DN goes high, energizing Yellow and starting T4:3. After 5 s, T4:3/DN triggers a reset, restarting the cycle. Total cycle = 30 + 25 + 5 = 60 s. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — RTO Waveform Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total ON-time calculation.**

$$T_{\text{ON,total}} = 1500 + 2400 = 3900\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Preset.**

PRE = 3600. Since 3900 > 3600, the DN bit will go high during the second TRUE interval.

**Step 3 — Determine when DN transitions.**

During the second TRUE interval, ACC = 1500 (held from first interval) + time. We need ACC = 3600, so additional time = 3600 − 1500 = 2100 s. The second TRUE interval lasts 2400 s, so DN goes high at **t = 600 + 2100 = 2700 s** (measured from start).

**Step 4 — Waveform tabulation:**

| Time (s) | 0–1500 | 1500–2100 | 2100–2700 | 2700–3900 | 3900–4500 | ≥4500 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input | TRUE | FALSE | TRUE | TRUE | FALSE | — |
| ACC | 0→1500 | 1500 | 1500→3600 | 3600→3900 | 3900 | 0 (RES) |
| DN | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 5 — Total ON-time recorded by RTO** = 3900 s = 1 hour 5 minutes (exceeds the 1-hour preset because RTO does not stop at PRE; only DN toggles). **(7 marks awarded for: 2 marks total ON-time + 2 marks transition calculation + 3 marks waveform tabulation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Timer Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert minutes/hours into seconds before applying $T = T_b \times \text{PRE}$. This single mistake can cost **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Mis-identifying Timer Type:** Confusing TON and TOF behavior. A TOF turns ON immediately and turns OFF after the delay — students often invert this.
> 3. **Forgetting RES for RTO:** A ladder diagram using an RTO without a parallel RES instruction is logically incomplete and will lose **1–2 marks** in the programming section.
> 4. **Bit Naming Convention:** Writing "T4:1/DN" correctly with the address notation is mandatory in KTU; vague references like "the done bit" without address lose marks.
> 5. **Time Base Selection Justification:** When asked to "select the time base," examiners expect justification based on the formula — a random choice without computation loses marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Timer Types:** **TON** (delayed-ON), **TOF** (delayed-OFF), **RTO** (retentive, needs RES).
- **Master Equation:** $T_{\text{delay}} = T_{\text{base}} \times \text{PRE}$ — memorize this in both forms.
- **Three Status Bits:** **EN** (input enabled), **TT** (timing in progress), **DN** (timing complete).
- **Three Time Bases (AB/Rockwell):** **1.0 s**, **0.1 s**, **0.01 s** — choose the largest TB that gives an integer PRE for optimal resolution and minimum scan overhead.
- **Maximum Preset:** 32,767 (16-bit signed integer limit).
- **RTO is Retentive:** It is the ONLY timer that retains its ACC after input goes FALSE; reset only via **RES** instruction.
- **Siemens S5T# Format:** `S5T#2M30S` is a BCD-coded time literal; range is up to `S5T#9990s` (2H_46M_30S).
- **Industrial Application Mnemonic:** **"TON starts, TOF stops, RTO remembers."**
- **Ladder Diagram Components Required for Full Marks:** Timer address (e.g., T4:1), time base selector, preset value, input rung XIC, output rung with DN/EN/TT bit reference, and (for RTO) a parallel RES branch.
- **Always verify** your preset by reverse-computing: $\text{PRE} \times T_{\text{base}} \overset{?}{=} T_{\text{desired}}$.
- **Cascading Timers:** For delays exceeding the maximum range, cascade two timers using the DN bit of the first as the input of the second — this multiplies the effective time range.
- **One timer = one file address:** Each timer instruction occupies 3 words (Control Word + PRE + ACC) in the PLC's data table.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ON Delay, OFF Delay, Retentive Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ON Delay, OFF Delay, Retentive Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)**, a **Timer** is an internal software instruction used to measure and control the duration of an event without the need for an external electromechanical timing relay. According to the **IEC 61131-3 standard** adopted by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, PLC timers are categorized into three primary functional blocks:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61131-3 Timer Classification (KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight)**
> 1. **TON (Timer ON-Delay)** — A non-retentive timer whose output is energized only after its input has been continuously TRUE for a preset time interval.
> 2. **TOF (Timer OFF-Delay)** — A non-retentive timer whose output is energized immediately when the input becomes TRUE and remains TRUE for a preset duration after the input becomes FALSE.
> 3. **RTO (Retentive Timer ON)** — A retentive timer that accumulates time across multiple TRUE input cycles and requires an explicit reset instruction.

**Preset Value (PT):** The user-defined target time (in milliseconds or seconds) that the timer must reach before changing its output state.

**Accumulated Value (ET):** The actual time elapsed (running count) tracked by the timer CPU.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a dark staircase. You press the light switch:

- **ON-Delay Timer (TON):** The light does **NOT** turn on the instant you press the button. Instead, it waits for a few seconds (preset time). If you release the button before the wait time elapses, the light never turns on. This is useful in **motor soft-start** applications, where the contactor must not close until the control circuit stabilizes.

- **OFF-Delay Timer (TOF):** The light turns on **immediately** the moment you press the button. When you release it, the light *stays on* for a few more seconds before turning off. This is used in **exhaust fans in restrooms** — they keep running for a while after you leave, to remove odor and moisture.

- **Retentive Timer (RTO):** Imagine an **hour-glass on a treadmill**. Even if you flip the hour-glass upside down (input becomes FALSE), the sand that already fell does not magically go back into the top bulb. The timer "remembers" all the time it has accumulated. Only when you manually press a "reset" button does the accumulated time return to zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** TON and TOF are **non-retentive** (memory-less; their ET resets to 0 every time input goes FALSE). RTO is **retentive** (memory-preserving; its ET holds the last value until explicitly reset).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ON-Delay Timer (TON) — Operational Logic

**Operational Steps (Breaking Down the TON):**

1. The CPU continuously scans the **Enable Input (IN)** rung condition.
2. The moment the **IN** condition transitions from FALSE → TRUE, the **TON instruction begins counting up** the elapsed time into the **Accumulator (ET)**.
3. The ET value increases at a rate determined by the **Time Base** (e.g., 1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, or 1 s per count).
4. **Comparison Logic:** Internally, the PLC compares `ET ≥ PT` continuously.
   - If `ET < PT` → Output bit **Q = FALSE** (Timer still counting).
   - If `ET ≥ PT` → Output bit **Q = TRUE** (Timer "done").
5. If at any point the **IN** condition becomes FALSE, the **ET immediately resets to 0** and **Q goes FALSE instantly** (no delay on the falling edge — this is non-retentive behavior).

**Timing Equation for TON:**
$$\text{Output State } Q(t) = \begin{cases} \text{FALSE} & \text{if } t < T_{\text{preset}} \\ \text{TRUE} & \text{if } t \geq T_{\text{preset}} \end{cases}$$

### 2.2 OFF-Delay Timer (TOF) — Operational Logic

**Operational Steps (Breaking Down the TOF):**

1. When **IN transitions FALSE → TRUE**, the **ET resets to 0 instantly** and the **Output Q becomes TRUE immediately** (no delay on the rising edge).
2. Q stays TRUE **as long as IN remains TRUE**, regardless of the PT value.
3. When **IN transitions TRUE → FALSE**, the timer begins counting up.
4. During this count, **Q remains TRUE**.
5. Once `ET ≥ PT`, Q finally drops to FALSE.
6. If IN goes TRUE again before ET reaches PT, the timer cancels the count (ET = 0) and Q stays TRUE.

**Timing Equation for TOF:**
$$Q(t) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{while IN = TRUE} \\ \text{TRUE} & \text{for } t \leq T_{\text{preset}} \text{ after IN drops} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{when } t > T_{\text{preset}} \text{ after IN drops} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3 Retentive Timer (RTO) — Operational Logic

**Operational Steps (Breaking Down the RTO):**

1. When **IN = TRUE**, the ET starts counting up from its current value (not necessarily from zero).
2. If IN momentarily goes FALSE, **the ET holds its current value** — it does NOT reset.
3. When IN goes TRUE again, ET **resumes counting from where it left off**.
4. Output Q becomes TRUE once `ET ≥ PT`.
5. A separate **RESET (R)** input coil must be triggered externally to clear ET back to 0 and turn OFF Q.
6. This "latching of accumulated time" is the defining feature of a retentive timer.

**Timing Equation for RTO:**
$$ET_{\text{new}} = ET_{\text{old}} + \Delta t \quad \text{(only while IN = TRUE; holds otherwise until RESET)}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | TON Behavior | TOF Behavior | RTO Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input Enable | $IN$ | Starts count on rising edge | Stops count on falling edge | Starts/holds count |
| Accumulator | $ET$ | Resets on $IN = FALSE$ | Resets on $IN = TRUE$ | Holds on $IN = FALSE$ |
| Output | $Q$ | TRUE only when $ET \geq PT$ | TRUE while $IN = TRUE$ plus delay | TRUE when $ET \geq PT$ |
| Reset Required | $R$ | Not needed (auto-reset) | Not needed (auto-reset) | **Mandatory** external reset |
| Memory Type | — | Non-retentive | Non-retentive | **Retentive** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Base Constants used in KTU problems:**
> Time Base $(T_b)$ is the **resolution** at which the PLC updates the timer counter.
> Common values: **1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, 1 s**.
> The relationship is:
> $$T_{\text{preset}} = \text{PT (counts)} \times T_b \text{ (seconds per count)}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TON:** Compressor start delay, conveyor belt sequence staging, debounce timers for push-buttons.
- **TOF:** Cooling-fan run-on after machine shutdown, staircase lighting, valve closure delay in hydraulics.
- **RTO:** Total running-hour tracking of motors, cumulative process time logging, machine uptime monitoring for preventive maintenance scheduling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Converting Preset Counts to Real Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can convert between **preset count values** and **actual real-world time durations**. The following derivation is mandatory for solving such problems.

**Given:**
- Time Base of the timer: $T_b = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}$
- Desired real time delay: $T_d = 25 \text{ seconds}$

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship between count and real time:**

Each timer count increments the accumulator by exactly one Time Base unit.

$$T_d = \text{PT} \times T_b$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for PT:**

$$\text{PT} = \frac{T_d}{T_b}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\text{PT} = \frac{25 \text{ s}}{0.1 \text{ s/count}}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division:**

$$\text{PT} = 250 \text{ counts}$$

**Verification (reverse substitution):**

$$T_d = 250 \text{ counts} \times 0.1 \text{ s/count} = 25 \text{ s} \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Truth Table for TON (Preset = 5 s)

| Time (s) | IN State | ET (s) | Output Q |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | FALSE | 0.0 | FALSE |
| 1.0 | TRUE | 1.0 | FALSE |
| 2.5 | TRUE | 2.5 | FALSE |
| 5.0 | TRUE | 5.0 | **TRUE** |
| 7.0 | TRUE | 5.0 | TRUE |
| 7.5 | FALSE | 0.0 | FALSE |
| 9.0 | FALSE | 0.0 | FALSE |

**Explanation:** The output Q only energizes at exactly 5 seconds. When IN drops to FALSE at 7.5 s, the ET instantly snaps back to 0.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Truth Table for TOF (Preset = 5 s)

| Time (s) | IN State | ET (s) | Output Q |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | FALSE | 0.0 | FALSE |
| 1.0 | TRUE | 0.0 | **TRUE (instant)** |
| 3.0 | TRUE | 0.0 | TRUE |
| 4.0 | FALSE | 1.0 | TRUE |
| 7.0 | FALSE | 4.0 | TRUE |
| 9.0 | FALSE | 5.0 | **FALSE** |
| 12.0 | TRUE | 0.0 | TRUE |

**Explanation:** Q turns on immediately when IN rises. After IN falls, Q stays TRUE for 5 seconds before going FALSE.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Truth Table for RTO (Preset = 10 s)

| Time (s) | IN State | RESET | ET (s) | Output Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | FALSE | FALSE | 0 | FALSE |
| 3 | TRUE | FALSE | 3 | FALSE |
| 5 | FALSE | FALSE | **3** | FALSE |
| 8 | TRUE | FALSE | **6** | FALSE |
| 11 | FALSE | FALSE | **6** | FALSE |
| 15 | TRUE | FALSE | **10** | **TRUE** |
| 18 | FALSE | TRUE | **0** | FALSE |

**Explanation:** Notice how ET holds at 3 s between t = 5 and t = 8, then resumes counting. Only an explicit RESET clears it.

### 3.5 Composite Timing Diagram Summary

For all three timers with $PT = T$:

$$Q_{\text{TON}}(t) = \text{IN}(t - T) \cdot u(t - T) \quad \text{(delayed step function)}$$

$$Q_{\text{TOF}}(t) = \text{IN}(t) + [\text{IN}(t^-) \cdot u(T - t_{\text{after drop}})]$$

$$ET_{\text{RTO}}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i \quad \text{where each } \Delta t_i \text{ is a TRUE segment}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TON Ladder Logic Representation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[L+ Power Rail] --> A
    A[X1 - Start Pushbutton NO Contact] --> B
    B[TON T1 - Preset = PT, Time Base = TB] --> C
    C[Timer Bit T1.Done Output] --> D
    D[M - Motor Contactor Coil] --> L2[L- Power Rail]
```

### 4.2 TOF Ladder Logic Representation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[L+ Power Rail] --> A
    A[Limit Switch LS1 - Normally Open] --> B
    B[TOF T2 - Preset = PT, Time Base = TB] --> C
    C[Timer Bit T2.Done Output] --> D
    D[Solenoid Valve Y1 - Cooling Coil] --> L2[L- Power Rail]
```

### 4.3 RTO Ladder Logic Representation with Reset (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[L+ Power Rail] --> A
    A[Sensor Input S1] --> B
    B[RTO T3 - Preset = PT, Time Base = TB] --> C
    C[Timer Bit T3.Done Output] --> E
    E[Alarm Lamp H1] --> L2[L- Power Rail]
    F[Reset Pushbutton PB_R] --> G
    G[RESET Instruction for T3] --> L2
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputCondition[Input Condition - IN Boolean] --> EdgeDetector{Rising Edge Detector}
    EdgeDetector -->|TRUE| Counter[Counter Increment Logic]
    Counter --> Compare{ET greater than or equal to PT?}
    Compare -->|No| Counter
    Compare -->|Yes| OutputEnable[Set Output Q to TRUE]
    InputCondition --> FallDetect{Falling Edge Detector}
    FallDetect -->|TRUE| ResetLogic[Reset Accumulator ET to ZERO]
    ResetLogic --> OutputDisable[Set Output Q to FALSE]
    ResetLogic --> Counter
```

### 4.5 Timing Waveform Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TIMING_DIAGRAM[Timing Waveform Comparison PT equal to T seconds]
        direction LR
        IN_SIG[IN Signal - Square Wave]
        TON_OUT[TON Output - Delayed Rise]
        TOF_OUT[TOF Output - Delayed Fall]
        RTO_ET[RTO Accumulated ET - Staircase Climb]
    end
    IN_SIG --> TON_OUT
    IN_SIG --> TOF_OUT
    IN_SIG --> RTO_ET
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Ladder Logic:** Each rung is scanned **top-to-bottom, left-to-right**. Current flows from the Left Power Rail (L+) through the contacts to the Right Power Rail (L-). A closed (TRUE) contact allows logic continuity, energizing the coil on the right.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an ON-Delay Timer (TON). Mention any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
An ON-Delay Timer (TON) is a non-retentive PLC instruction that delays the activation of its output until the input condition has been continuously TRUE for a preset duration. The output turns ON only after the accumulated time (ET) equals the preset time (PT).

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Compressor start delay** — to allow pressure to stabilize before energizing the motor contactor.
2. **Debounce filter for pushbuttons** — eliminates false triggering due to mechanical contact bounce.

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1 mark) + Application 1 (1 mark) + Application 2 (1 mark).

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Retentive Timer (RTO) and an ON-Delay Timer (TON) in terms of memory behavior.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | TON | RTO |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Behavior | Non-retentive — ET resets to 0 when IN = FALSE | Retentive — ET holds value across IN = FALSE cycles |
| Reset Method | Automatic reset on IN = FALSE | Requires explicit RESET instruction |
| Typical Use | Simple time delays | Cumulative runtime measurement |

**Mark Distribution:** Table or 3 clear differentiating points (3 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat ladder logic diagram and a timing waveform, explain the operation of an **OFF-Delay Timer (TOF)**. Assume a preset value of 8 seconds and a time base of 100 ms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conveyor belt motor must start **5 seconds after** the start pushbutton is pressed and run for **at least 30 seconds** once started, regardless of whether the operator releases the button. Design the ladder logic using TON and TOF timers. Show the complete I/O assignment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TOF Operation with PT = 8 s, TB = 100 ms:**

**Step 1 — Compute the preset count:**
$$\text{PT} = \frac{8 \text{ s}}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 80 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 2 — Behavior description:**
- At $t = 0$: IN = FALSE, Q = FALSE, ET = 0.
- At $t = 2$ s: IN becomes TRUE → Q becomes TRUE **immediately** (no delay on rising edge).
- At $t = 10$ s: IN becomes FALSE → Q **remains TRUE**; ET starts counting from 0.
- At $t = 18$ s: ET reaches 8 s → Q finally becomes FALSE.

**Step 3 — Ladder logic rung:**

```
| LS1     TOF T1              Y1       |
|---[ ]--+-[TOF T1 PT=80]-----( )------|
|        |  TB=100ms          |        |
```

**Step 4 — Timing waveform explanation:**
The Q waveform is a **delayed-fall** of IN. Q tracks IN instantaneously on the rising edge but lags behind by exactly 8 seconds on the falling edge.

**(Mark split: 2 marks for calculation + 2 marks for description + 2 marks for ladder + 1 mark for waveform)**

**(b) Conveyor Belt Start-Delay and Minimum Run-Time:**

**I/O Assignment:**
- Input I0.0 — Start Pushbutton (PB_START)
- Input I0.1 — Stop Pushbutton (PB_STOP)
- Output Q0.0 — Motor Contactor (M1)
- Internal Memory M0.0 — Start Delay Done (Latching bit)
- Timer T1 — TON for 5 s start delay
- Timer T2 — TOF for 30 s minimum run-time

**Ladder Logic Rungs:**

```
Rung 1: Start Delay (5 s)
| PB_START        TON T1            M0.0           |
|---[ ]-----------+-[TON PT=50]-----+-(/)-+         |
|                 |  TB=100ms       |     |         |

Rung 2: Latching the Start Command
| PB_START OR M0.0    M0.0            |
|---[ ]---+---( )----+                |
|---[ M0.0]                           |

Rung 3: Minimum Run-Time TOF (30 s)
| M0.0 AND NOT PB_STOP    TOF T2    T2_Done   |
|---[ ]-------------------+-[TOF PT=300 TB=100ms]-+---( )----|

Rung 4: Motor Output (Q0.0 active while either T1.Done OR T2.Done is true after stop)
| M0.0 OR T2.Done    M1                |
|---[ ]---+---( )----+                  |
|---[ T2.Done]                          |
```

**Explanation:**
1. **TON T1 (5 s):** Delays the activation of M0.0 by 5 seconds after PB_START.
2. **TOF T2 (30 s):** Once M0.0 is TRUE and PB_STOP is pressed, T2 holds the motor running for 30 seconds.
3. The motor M1 runs as long as M0.0 is TRUE OR T2.Done is TRUE.

**Mark split: 2 marks I/O + 3 marks ladder logic + 2 marks timer calculation**

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Retentive Timer (RTO)** with a suitable ladder diagram. How is its accumulated value reset? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical reactor requires cumulative heating. The heater should remain ON until a **total accumulated ON time of 45 minutes** is reached, even if the operator presses START multiple times. Design the ladder logic using an RTO. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RTO Working Principle:**

A Retentive Timer (RTO) is a PLC instruction that **accumulates time** across multiple input cycles. Unlike TON, when the input IN goes FALSE, the accumulated value (ET) is **not reset**. It holds its last value. The output Q becomes TRUE only when ET ≥ PT. To clear the ET, an external **RESET instruction** must be executed (typically via a pushbutton or a conditional contact).

**Ladder Logic Diagram:**

```
| Heater_ON        RTO T5            T5_Done    Heater_Coil |
|---[ ]------------+-[RTO PT=PT]-----+---( )----+---( )----|
|                                                  |      |
| PB_RESET           RESET T5                       |      |
|---[ ]--------------+-(RES)------------------------+      |
```

**Reset Mechanism:** The `RES` (Reset) instruction is energized by pressing PB_RESET. This forces ET = 0 and Q = FALSE in a single scan cycle.

**Mark split: 2 marks definition + 3 marks ladder + 2 marks reset explanation**

**(b) RTO for 45-Minute Cumulative Heating:**

**Step 1 — Convert 45 minutes into seconds:**
$$T_d = 45 \times 60 = 2700 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Choose an appropriate Time Base:** $T_b = 1 \text{ s}$ (best resolution for minutes-scale timing).

**Step 3 — Compute Preset Count:**
$$\text{PT} = \frac{2700}{1} = 2700 \text{ counts}$$

**I/O Assignment:**
- I0.0 — START Pushbutton (latches heater request)
- I0.1 — RESET Pushbutton (manual abort)
- Q0.0 — Heater Contactor (H1)
- T6 — RTO Timer

**Ladder Logic:**

```
Rung 1: Latch Heater Request
| PB_START     Heater_Request_Latch     |
|---[ ]--------+---( S )---+              |
|                            |              |

Rung 2: RTO Timer Accumulation
| Heater_Request_Latch     RTO T6             T6_Done     |
|---[ ]-------------------+-[RTO PT=2700]-----+---( )---+ |
|                                                    |   |
| PB_RESET          RESET T6                         |   |
|---[ ]-------------+--( RES )-----------------------+   |

Rung 3: Heater Output
| Heater_Request_Latch AND NOT T6_Done   H1        |
|---[ ]---+---[/ T6.Done]----------------+---( )----|
```

**Explanation:** The heater turns ON as long as Heater_Request_Latch is TRUE and T6 is not yet done. If the operator releases PB_START mid-way, ET holds. Pressing START again resumes the count. Once 2700 counts accumulate, T6_Done turns off the heater permanently until RESET.

**Mark split: 2 marks conversion + 3 marks ladder + 2 marks working explanation**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Confusing IN rising/falling edge behavior:** Many students wrongly assume TOF delays the *rising* edge. Mark loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to reset RTO explicitly:** Examiners check for the explicit RESET (RES) instruction. A missing reset = **deduct 3 marks**.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert minutes → seconds → counts. Mark loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Drawing ladder logic without I/O assignment:** Always declare inputs and outputs before writing the ladder. A clear I/O table earns **1–2 easy marks**.
> 5. **Using `TON` where `RTO` is required (or vice versa):** Read the question carefully — if it says "cumulative" or "total", it must be **RTO**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **TON** = Delayed ON, auto-reset, non-retentive.
- ✅ **TOF** = Delayed OFF, auto-reset, non-retentive.
- ✅ **RTO** = Delayed ON, manual reset via RES instruction, **retentive**.
- ✅ Time conversion formula: $T_{\text{preset}} = \text{PT} \times T_b$.
- ✅ **Time Base (TB)** is the resolution: 1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, 1 s.
- ✅ Always **declare I/O assignments** before drawing ladder logic (KTU marking scheme reward).
- ✅ TOF output is TRUE the *instant* IN rises — only the *fall* is delayed.
- ✅ RTO accumulated value is **not lost** on power-cycle if the PLC uses a retentive memory area; check your PLC manual.
- ✅ For **cumulative timing**, ALWAYS use RTO + RES, never TON.
- ✅ For **minimum run-time after stop**, use TOF on the motor output contact.
- ✅ Standard ladder symbol: `[TON T1, PT=xxx, TB=xx]` block.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes IEC 61131-3 standard naming — use **TON, TOF, RTO** (not TMR, TMRF).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Timer Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PLC Timer Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLC Timer Function:** A PLC timer is a software-implemented (virtual) counting element of the CPU that monitors elapsed time once an enabling input condition becomes true, and produces a logical output (Boolean 1) after a user-defined **Preset Time (PT)** has expired. Timer instructions are non-retentive (memory resets with input) or retentive (memory retains state across power/scan cycles).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, timer functions are classified as **basic instructions** under the **bit-logic instruction set** of any standard PLC (Allen-Bradley RSLogix 500/5000, Siemens S7-1500, Modicon M340, etc.). They form the backbone of time-sequenced industrial control — from conveyor delay starting to sequential batch process control.

### 1.2 Functional Anatomy of a PLC Timer

A standard PLC timer block has three critical parameters:

| Parameter | Full Name | Data Type | Function |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **IN** | Input Enable | BOOL | Enabling condition that starts the timer when TRUE |
| **PT** | Preset Time | TIME / DINT | Target duration value (e.g., 5 s, 250 ms) |
| **ET** | Elapsed Time | TIME / DINT | Current accumulated time |
| **Q / DN** | Output / Done | BOOL | TRUE when ET ≥ PT |
| **TI / TT** | Timing Bit | BOOL | TRUE while timer is actively accumulating |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Egg Timer 🍳**
> Think of a PLC timer exactly like a kitchen egg-timer. The moment you twist the knob (input `IN` becomes TRUE), the sand inside starts falling (accumulating time in `ET`). The bell rings (output `Q` turns ON) only when ALL the sand has fallen — i.e., when elapsed time equals the preset time. If you stop the timer early (input goes FALSE before completion), the sand falls back to the top — this is a **non-retentive timer**. A **retentive timer** is more like a stopwatch with a pause/resume button: it remembers how much time has elapsed even if you stop and start again.

### 1.4 Time Base (Resolution)

The **time base** is the smallest unit of time the timer can count. Common industrial time bases are:

$$\text{Time Base} \in \{0.01\,\text{s},\ 0.1\,\text{s},\ 1\,\text{s}\}$$

The preset value is usually stored as a **whole number multiple** of the time base. For example, a preset of `50` with a time base of `0.1 s` equals **5 seconds**.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of PLC Timers (KTU Board-Favorite Classification)

PLC timers fall into **four primary functional classes**:

### A. ON-Delay Timer (TON / SD / TP-X)

- **Operation:** Output `Q` becomes TRUE only **after** the input `IN` has remained continuously TRUE for the duration equal to PT.
- **Behavior on Input Drop:** If `IN` drops to FALSE before `ET` reaches `PT`, the timer **resets immediately** (ET → 0, Q → 0).
- **Industrial Use:** Motor star-delta transition delay, lubrication pump run-on, conveyor interlock sequencing.

### B. OFF-Delay Timer (TOF / SF)

- **Operation:** Output `Q` becomes TRUE **immediately** when `IN` becomes TRUE. When `IN` transitions to FALSE, output remains TRUE for `PT` duration, then turns OFF.
- **Industrial Use:** Cooling fan run-on after motor stop, lamp switch-off delay, debounce filtering.

### C. Retentive ON-Delay Timer (RTO / SS)

- **Operation:** Output `Q` becomes TRUE only after the input has been TRUE for a **cumulative** time equal to PT, even if input was interrupted.
- **Behavior on Input Drop:** Does NOT reset; holds elapsed time until explicitly reset by a `RES` instruction.
- **Industrial Use:** Total run-time accumulation, maintenance alarms based on cumulative operating hours.

### D. Pulse Timer (TP / Pulse)

- **Operation:** Generates an output pulse of fixed width `PT` on either rising or falling edge of input. Independent of input duration once triggered.
- **Industrial Use:** Single-shot operations, valve actuation pulses, alarm acknowledgment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61131-3 Standard Timer Types:** The international PLC programming standard (IEC 61131-3) defines timers as function blocks with type-prefixes — `TON` (on-delay), `TOF` (off-delay), `TP` (pulse). Most KTU board questions explicitly reference these IEC-standardized names.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Total Preset Time | $\text{PT}_{\text{real}} = \text{PV}_{\text{raw}} \times \text{TB}$ | seconds |
| Actual Timer (delay) calculation | $t_{\text{delay}} = N \times t_{\text{base}}$ | $N$ = count, $t_{\text{base}}$ = time-base |
| Number of scans for full timing | $n_{\text{scan}} = \dfrac{\text{PT}}{T_{\text{scan}}}$ | dimensionless |
| Timer accuracy (worst case) | $\Delta t = 2 \times T_{\text{scan}} + T_{\text{base}}$ | seconds |
| Cascade total time (two TONs) | $t_{\text{total}} = t_{1} + t_{2}$ | seconds |
| Counter-timer combination | $t_{\text{extended}} = \text{CT}_{\text{PV}} \times \text{TON}_{\text{PT}}$ | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** All ladder-logic timers update their internal `ET` register **once per PLC scan cycle**. Therefore, a timer's accuracy is bounded by the PLC scan time. A typical Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1100 has a scan time of **~1–10 ms**.

## 2.3 Internal CPU Working Principle

PLC timers are NOT physical hardware clocks — they are **memory locations** in the CPU's process image. Each scan cycle, the CPU:

1. Checks if `IN` is TRUE.
2. If TRUE, adds `(T_{\text{scan}} \times \text{time-base-multiplier})` to `ET`.
3. Compares `ET` with `PT`.
4. Sets `Q = TRUE` when `ET \geq PT`; otherwise `Q = FALSE`.

This explains why **fast scans → high timer accuracy**, and **slow scans → cumulative drift**.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Preset Value to Real Time

**Problem:** A Siemens S7-1500 TON timer has a preset value of `PV = 250` with a time-base of `0.1 s`. Calculate the actual on-delay.

**Step 1:** Identify the governing equation.

$$t_{\text{delay}} = \text{PV} \times t_{\text{base}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$t_{\text{delay}} = 250 \times 0.1\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$t_{\text{delay}} = 25\,\text{s}$$

**Interpretation:** The timer output will turn ON exactly 25 seconds after continuous TRUE input.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Preset Value for a Given Delay

**Problem:** In an Allen-Bradley RSLogix 500 TON with a time-base of `0.01 s`, what integer preset value will give a delay of 7.5 seconds?

**Step 1:** Rearrange the governing equation.

$$\text{PV} = \frac{t_{\text{delay}}}{t_{\text{base}}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\text{PV} = \frac{7.5\,\text{s}}{0.01\,\text{s}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$\text{PV} = 750$$

**Answer:** Preset value `750`, time base `0.01 s` → delay of **7.5 seconds** exactly.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Timer Type vs. Behavior

The following truth-table style matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam question pattern**:

Let `IN` transition sequence be: `FALSE → TRUE → TRUE (5 s) → FALSE → FALSE (3 s) → TRUE`

For PT = 10 s:

| Timer Type | Output Q Timeline | Final Q State |
|---|---|---|
| **TON** | FALSE for 5 s (input true but not yet 10 s), FALSE when input drops; never reaches Q=1 in this trace | Q = FALSE |
| **TOF** | FALSE → TRUE (immediately on first TRUE) → TRUE (held) → FALSE (after input drop) → FALSE → FALSE (will not retrigger mid-cycle) | Q = FALSE |
| **RTO** | Accumulates ET=5 s, holds when input drops, accumulates to 10 s on second TRUE → Q=TRUE permanently until RES | Q = TRUE |
| **TP** | Generates a 10 s pulse on the rising edge of IN, regardless of input duration | Q = FALSE (after pulse) |

## 3.4 Cascade Timer Derivation

**Problem:** Two TON timers are cascaded. Timer 1 has `PT1 = 30 s`, Timer 2 has `PT2 = 45 s`. Find the total delay from start of T1 to activation of T2's output.

**Step 1:** T1 starts timing at $t = 0$.

$$t_{Q1 = \text{TRUE}} = 30\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** T2 is enabled by Q1 (T2's `IN` becomes TRUE only when Q1 = TRUE).

$$t_{Q2 = \text{TRUE}} = t_{Q1} + t_{PT2} = 30 + 45 = 75\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** General cascade rule for N cascaded TON timers:

$$t_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{PT}_i$$

For N=2:

$$t_{\text{total}} = \text{PT}_1 + \text{PT}_2 = 30 + 45 = 75\,\text{s}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Timer–Counter Combination for Extended Timing

A single PLC timer typically maxes out at 999 or 32767 counts (depending on memory). To achieve **long delays** (e.g., 1 hour), designers cascade a **timer with a counter**:

**Problem:** Design a delay of 1 hour using a TON timer of `PT = 60 s` and an up-counter `CTU`.

**Step 1:** Timer pulses its `Q` output every 60 s — that pulse is fed to the counter's `CU` input.

$$\text{Pulses per hour} = \frac{3600\,\text{s}}{60\,\text{s}} = 60$$

**Step 2:** Counter preset value.

$$\text{CT}_{\text{PV}} = 60$$

**Step 3:** Total delay formula.

$$t_{\text{extended}} = \text{CT}_{\text{PV}} \times \text{TON}_{\text{PT}} = 60 \times 60 = 3600\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{hour}$$

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PLC Timer Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Condition IN becomes TRUE] --> B[CPU Scan Cycle Detects IN TRUE]
    B --> C[Timer ET register increments by Scan Time]
    C --> D{Is ET greater than or equal to PT}
    D -- NO --> E[Set Q FALSE, TT TRUE, continue counting]
    D -- YES --> F[Set Q TRUE, latch output bit]
    E --> G[Next Scan Cycle]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Output Q TRUE drives downstream load]
    H --> I{Check Input IN}
    I -- STILL TRUE --> J[Retentive behavior depends on type]
    I -- FALSE --> K[Reset or Hold ET based on timer type]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Ladder Logic — ON-Delay Timer (TON)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[XIC Start_PB] --> T1[TON Timer T4:1 PT 50]
    T1 --> Y1[OTE Motor_Run]
    T1 -. ET register .-> DB1[Data Table Word T4:1.PRE]
    T1 -. Done Bit DN .-> X1
```

**Ladder Representation (text-based for clarity):**

```
   |    Start_PB         T4:1              |
   |------|/|------------(TON)---------------|
   |                  PRE: 50               |
   |                  ACC: (ET)             |
   |                                       |
   |    T4:1.DN           Motor_Run        |
   |------| |------------(OTE)---------------|
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Cascaded Timers

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IN as Start_Input
    participant T1 as TON_T1 PT=30s
    participant T2 as TON_T2 PT=45s
    participant OUT as Final_Output
    IN->>T1: TRUE at t=0
    Note over T1: Accumulating 0 to 30s
    T1->>T2: T1.DN becomes TRUE at t=30s
    Note over T2: Accumulating 30s to 75s
    T2->>OUT: T2.DN becomes TRUE at t=75s
    OUT->>OUT: Final Output Energized
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selecting Timer Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need delayed ON?}
    Q1 -- YES --> A[Use TON On-Delay]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Need delayed OFF?}
    Q2 -- YES --> B[Use TOF Off-Delay]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Need cumulative time memory?}
    Q3 -- YES --> C[Use RTO Retentive]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Need fixed-width pulse?}
    Q4 -- YES --> D[Use TP Pulse Timer]
    Q4 -- NO --> E[Re-examine control logic]
```

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between TON and TOF timer instructions with timing diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) |
|---|---|---|
| Output turns ON | After PT delay from IN TRUE | Immediately on IN TRUE |
| Output turns OFF | Immediately when IN FALSE | After PT delay from IN FALSE |
| ET reset behavior | Reset on IN FALSE | Holds ET while IN TRUE; resets after PT on IN FALSE |
| Use case | Conveyor start delay | Cooling fan run-on after motor stop |

```
TON:    IN: ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
        Q:  _________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___

TOF:    IN: ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
        Q:  _____|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
```

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is a retentive timer? Why is an explicit reset (RES) instruction required for it?**

**Model Answer:**
A retentive timer (RTO/SS) accumulates elapsed time **even when the enabling input goes FALSE**, holding the ET value in memory. Unlike a TON which resets its ET to zero on input drop, RTO retains ET across multiple input cycles until the accumulated time reaches PT. Because of this memory-holding property, the output `Q` cannot automatically return to FALSE — it must be explicitly cleared using a **RES (Reset)** instruction. Without RES, the timer would remain latched indefinitely, which is dangerous in applications like motor overload alarms. Typical applications include cumulative run-time tracking and maintenance scheduling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the functional block diagram and operating principle of an ON-Delay Timer (TON) instruction in a PLC. Show its ladder logic implementation and timing diagram with PT = 10 s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conveyor system requires a sequential start: Conveyor A starts, after 8 seconds Conveyor B starts, and after another 6 seconds Conveyor C starts. After Conveyor C is ON for 10 seconds, all conveyors stop together. Design the PLC ladder logic using TON timers. Show full addressing and explain operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A(a):

**Operating Principle:**
A TON timer has three terminals — Input (`IN`), Preset Time (`PT`), and Elapsed Time (`ET`). When `IN` becomes TRUE, the timer starts incrementing its `ET` register by the scan time each cycle. The output `Q` (Done bit) becomes TRUE only when `ET ≥ PT`. If `IN` goes FALSE before completion, `ET` resets to zero and `Q` goes FALSE.

**Ladder Logic:**

```
   |  Start_PB          T4:1                |
   |------| |------------(TON)---------------|
   |                  PRE: 100              |
   |                  ACC: (auto)           |
   |                  TB : 0.1 s            |
   |                                        |
   |  T4:1.DN            Conveyor_Motor     |
   |------| |------------(OTE)---------------|
```

**Timing Diagram (PT = 10 s, TB = 0.1 s, PV = 100):**

```
IN:  ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
ET:  ___/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾___ 
     0    2s  4s  6s  8s  10s   0s
Q:   ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
     0                        10s  0
```

#### Model Solution to Part A(b):

**Design Analysis:**
Three cascaded TON timers, with each timer's `DN` bit enabling the next conveyor. A fourth TON creates the run-duration after Conveyor C starts. All conveyors use the same `Master_Stop` contact for safety cut-off.

**Ladder Logic:**

```
Rung 1: Conveyor A control (immediate start)
   |  Master_Start    Master_Stop       Conveyor_A |
   |------| |------------|/|--------------(OTE)------|

Rung 2: Timer T1 (delay before Conveyor B)
   |  Conveyor_A         T4:1                    |
   |------| |------------(TON)--------------------|
   |                  PRE: 80                    |
   |                  TB: 0.1 s (= 8 s)          |

Rung 3: Conveyor B (start when T1 done)
   |  T4:1.DN            Conveyor_B              |
   |------| |------------(OTE)--------------------|

Rung 4: Timer T2 (delay before Conveyor C)
   |  Conveyor_B          T4:2                   |
   |------| |------------(TON)--------------------|
   |                  PRE: 60                    |
   |                  TB: 0.1 s (= 6 s)          |

Rung 5: Conveyor C (start when T2 done)
   |  T4:2.DN            Conveyor_C              |
   |------| |------------(OTE)--------------------|

Rung 6: Timer T3 (run-time of 10 s after C starts)
   |  Conveyor_C          T4:3                   |
   |------| |------------(TON)--------------------|
   |                  PRE: 100                   |
   |                  TB: 0.1 s (= 10 s)         |

Rung 7: Master stop latch reset
   |  T4:3.DN            Master_Stop_Latch       |
   |------| |------------(OTE)--------------------|
   |  Master_Stop_Latch  Conveyor_A             |
   |------|/|------------(OTE)--------------------|
   |  Master_Stop_Latch  Conveyor_B             |
   |------|/|------------(OTE)--------------------|
   |  Master_Stop_Latch  Conveyor_C             |
   |------|/|------------(OTE)--------------------|
```

**Total time from Master_Start to all-stop:**
$$t_{\text{total}} = 8 + 6 + 10 = 24\,\text{s}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the OFF-Delay Timer (TOF) and Retentive On-Delay Timer (RTO) with timing diagrams. State two industrial applications for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using a TON timer and an up-counter (CTU), design a PLC ladder logic to generate a delay of exactly **30 minutes**. The TON timer has a maximum preset of 60 seconds. Show complete addressing, counter preset calculation, and explain how the circuit achieves 1800-second delay. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B(a):

**OFF-Delay Timer (TOF):**
- Output `Q` becomes TRUE **immediately** when `IN` transitions to TRUE.
- When `IN` goes FALSE, `Q` remains TRUE for the duration of `PT`, then turns OFF.
- Used for: (1) Cooling fan run-on after main motor stop, (2) Emergency light delay-off after power restoration.

**Timing Diagram (TOF):**

```
IN:  ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
ET:  ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
        ↑                              ↑
       PT expires                  ET resets
Q:   ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
       ON immediately         ON until PT
                            after IN FALSE
```

**Retentive Timer (RTO):**
- Accumulates ET even when IN goes FALSE.
- Holds ET until reset by RES instruction.
- Used for: (1) Maintenance-due alarms (cumulative run-time tracking), (2) Filter replacement reminders.

**Timing Diagram (RTO):**

```
IN:  ___|‾‾‾___|‾‾‾‾‾___|___
ET:  ___/‾‾‾‾‾\__/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ (accumulates)
            ↓                      
          RES triggers only when manually issued
Q:   _____________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
            Q turns ON only when cumulative ET >= PT
```

#### Model Solution to Part B(b):

**Step 1 — Required counter preset calculation:**

$$t_{\text{required}} = 30\,\text{min} = 1800\,\text{s}$$

$$t_{\text{TON}} = 60\,\text{s}$$

$$\text{CT}_{\text{PV}} = \frac{t_{\text{required}}}{t_{\text{TON}}} = \frac{1800}{60} = 30$$

**Step 2 — Ladder Logic Design:**

```
Rung 1: Generate one pulse every 60 s using a self-resetting TON

   |  Start_PB        T4:1 (TON, PRE=600, TB=0.1s = 60s)  |
   |------| |--------------------(TON)-------------------------|
   |                       PRE: 600                         |

Rung 2: Reset timer T4:1 with its own DN bit to create oscillation

   |  T4:1.DN        T4:1.RES                                |
   |------| |------------(RES)---------------------------------|

Rung 3: Feed every T4:1 pulse into the counter

   |  T4:1.DN        C5:0 (CTU, PRE=30)                     |
   |------| |------------(CTU)---------------------------------|

Rung 4: When counter reaches 30, activate final output

   |  C5:0.DN        Final_Output_30min                      |
   |------| |------------(OTE)---------------------------------|

Rung 5: Reset the counter on Stop or on completion

   |  Stop_PB        C5:0.RES                                |
   |------| |------------(RES)---------------------------------|
   |  C5:0.DN        C5:0.RES                                |
   |------| |------------(RES)---------------------------------|
```

**Explanation:** The self-resetting TON generates a 1-scan pulse every 60 seconds. The CTU counter counts these pulses; after 30 pulses, `C5:0.DN` becomes TRUE, achieving the **1800-second (30-minute)** delay.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget that TON resets ET to 0 the moment `IN` goes FALSE. RTO does NOT do this — make sure to clarify this distinction when answering.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** When the question asks for the **preset value**, give the integer count, NOT the time in seconds (e.g., write `250` not `25 s`).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Always mention the **time base** in your answer — a TON with `PV=100` means different things at TB=0.01 s vs TB=1 s. Examiners award marks for this precision.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** For RTO, students forget to draw the **RES instruction**. Without it, RTO output latches permanently — examiners specifically check for this.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In cascaded-timer problems, students sometimes parallel-wire instead of cascade. Verify that timer N+1's IN is wired to timer N's DN bit, NOT to the same start input.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for PLC Timer Functions:**

- ⏱️ **Four canonical timer types** per IEC 61131-3: **TON, TOF, TP, RTO**.
- ⏱️ **TON** = delay on energize; resets on de-energize. (Most common in KTU problems.)
- ⏱️ **TOF** = delay on de-energize; output follows input during ON state.
- ⏱️ **RTO** = cumulative timer; **requires explicit RES instruction**.
- ⏱️ **TP** = fixed-width pulse on input edge.
- ⏱️ **Time-base** is the resolution (0.01 s, 0.1 s, 1 s). Always specify it.
- ⏱️ Real delay: $t = \text{PV} \times t_{\text{base}}$.
- ⏱️ Cascading N TON timers: total delay $= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{PT}_i$.
- ⏱️ Timer + Counter combo: $t_{\text{extended}} = \text{CT}_{\text{PV}} \times \text{TON}_{\text{PT}}$.
- ⏱️ Timers update once per scan — accuracy $\Delta t \approx 2 \times T_{\text{scan}}$.
- ⏱️ Address conventions: AB uses `T4:1`; Siemens uses `DB1.TON_T1`; Modicon uses `T1`.
- ⏱️ Industrial applications: star-delta starters, traffic lights, lubrication systems, alarm delays, sequential batch control, motor soft-start.
- ⏱️ Examiners LOVE asking the difference between TON and TOF — always draw timing diagrams side-by-side.
- ⏱️ Examiners LOVE cascaded timer problems — practice full ladder-logic solutions with proper addressing.
- ⏱️ Always include **scan-time consideration** in your answer for full marks.
- ⏱️ Retentive vs. non-retentive is the single most-tested concept in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Timer Function Industrial Application, Programming Counters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Examples of Timer Function Industrial Application, Programming Counters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Timer Functions — Formal Definition

A **PLC Timer** is a non-physical, software-implemented instruction within the CPU scan cycle that measures elapsed time against a preset value (PV) and produces a discrete Boolean output based on whether the elapsed time (ET — Elapsed Time) has reached, exceeded, or fallen below the programmed threshold. The KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes three primary IEC 61131-3 timer function blocks:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61131-3 Standard Timer Types (KTU Syllabus Mandate)**
> - **TON** — Timer ON-Delay (Input-driven delay-on energization)
> -TOF** — Timer OFF-Delay (Delay-on de-energization)
> - **TP** — Timer Pulse (Fixed-width pulse generator)

Each timer block operates on the principle of counting internal clock pulses (typically **1 ms**, **10 ms**, **100 ms**, or **1 s** resolution) generated by the PLC's hardware real-time clock oscillator.

## 1.2 Counters — Formal Definition

A **PLC Counter** is a function block that tallies the number of rising-edge transitions arriving at its count input and produces a Boolean output when the accumulated value (CV — Current Value) reaches a preset value (PV). The KTU 2024 Scheme identifies three counter primitives:

> [!NOTE]
> **IEC 61131-3 Standard Counter Types**
> - **CTU** — Count Up Counter
> - **CTD** — Count Down Counter
> - **CTUD** — Count Up/Down Counter (Bidirectional)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🎯 Timer Analogy — "The Egg Boiler"

Imagine boiling an egg for exactly **5 minutes**:

- **TON (ON-Delay)**: The moment you press the *Start* button (input ON), an internal clock begins. Only after the 5 minutes elapse does the buzzer ring (output ON). The buzzer cannot ring before the timer finishes — the **delay is applied to the ON-transition**. If you release the button early, the buzzer never rings and the clock resets.

- **TOF (OFF-Delay)**: The buzzer rings **immediately** when you press Start. When you release the button, the buzzer keeps ringing for 5 more minutes, then stops. The **delay is applied to the OFF-transition** — useful for cooling-fan rundown after a motor is turned off.

- **TP (Pulse Timer)**: The instant you press Start, the buzzer rings for exactly 5 minutes regardless of how long you hold the button. Pressing it again during the ring does nothing — it is **non-retriggerable** during its pulse width.

### 🎯 Counter Analogy — "The Stadium Turnstile"

A turnstile at a stadium door counts the number of people who pass through. Each person produces one **rising-edge event** as they push past. The CTU counter increments by 1 for every entry. When the count reaches the stadium's maximum capacity (**PV = 50,000**), the output turns ON, which can be wired to illuminate a "FULL" sign and lock the entrance gate. The CTD is the reverse: starting from PV and decrementing with each exit. CTUD does both — tracking net occupancy in real time.

---

### 1.4 Standard Preset Constants and Units

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time Base Resolution | $T_b$ | **milliseconds (ms)** | 1 ms – 1 s |
| Preset Value (Timer) | $PV$ | **milliseconds / seconds** | 0 – 9999 (4-digit BCD) |
| Preset Value (Counter) | $PV$ | **integer count** | 0 – 9999 |
| Current Value | $CV$ / $ET$ | same unit as PV | 0 – 9999 |
| Scan Cycle Time | $T_{scan}$ | **milliseconds** | 1 – 20 ms |
| Clock Pulse Period | $T_{clk}$ | **milliseconds** | 1, 10, 100, 1000 ms |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TON (Timer ON-Delay) — Operating Logic

The TON is the most frequently used timer in KTU exam problems. Its operational phases are:

**Phase 1 — Idle (Input OFF):**
- When input `IN = 0`, the elapsed time `ET = 0` and the output `Q = 0`.
- The timer is in a **reset** condition.

**Phase 2 — Accumulation (Input ON):**
- The instant `IN` transitions from $0 \to 1$ (rising edge), the internal clock begins counting.
- On every scan cycle, `ET` increases by $T_b$ (the time base).
- $Q$ remains at $0$ during this phase.

**Phase 3 — Done (Threshold Reached):**
- When $ET \geq PV$, the output $Q$ becomes $1$ and **latches** (stays ON) as long as $IN$ remains 1.
- If $IN$ drops to $0$ at any point, $ET$ resets to $0$ and $Q$ returns to $0$.

### Operational Truth Table for TON

| Input `IN` | Condition | Elapsed Time `ET` | Output `Q` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Reset | $ET = 0$ | $Q = 0$ |
| 1 (rising edge) | Start counting | $ET$ increasing | $Q = 0$ |
| 1 (steady) | Counting | $0 < ET < PV$ | $Q = 0$ |
| 1 (done) | Done | $ET \geq PV$ | $Q = 1$ |
| 0 (anytime) | Reset | $ET \leftarrow 0$ | $Q = 0$ |

## 2.2 TOF (Timer OFF-Delay) — Operating Logic

The TOF is commonly used in motor-cooling, indicator-lamp, and lamp-warm-up applications.

**Phase 1 — Input ON:** $Q = 1$ immediately (no delay), $ET = 0$.

**Phase 2 — Input OFF (Rising Edge of OFF):** $ET$ begins incrementing from 0.

**Phase 3 — Threshold Reached:** When $ET \geq PV$, $Q$ becomes 0. If $IN$ returns to 1 before $ET = PV$, the timer is **cancelled** and $Q$ remains 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** TON delays the *turning-ON* of an output. TOF delays the *turning-OFF* of an output. Mixing these up is a common 2-mark deduction.

## 2.3 TP (Pulse Timer) — Operating Logic

The TP generates a **fixed-width pulse** of duration equal to $PV$, regardless of how long the input stays ON. It is non-retriggerable during its pulse window — a new input rising edge is ignored until the pulse completes.

## 2.4 Counter Operating Logic

### 2.4.1 CTU (Count Up Counter)

The CTU maintains an internal accumulator `CV` that increments by 1 on every **rising edge** of the `CU` (Count Up) input. When $CV \geq PV$, the output $Q$ becomes 1. A separate `R` (Reset) input forces $CV \leftarrow 0$ and $Q \leftarrow 0$.

### 2.4.2 CTD (Count Down Counter)

The CTD decrements `CV` on every rising edge of `CD`. The initial value is loaded via the `LD` (Load) input. When $CV \leq 0$, the output $Q$ becomes 1.

### 2.4.3 CTUD (Count Up/Down Counter)

The CTUD combines both functions. It has a single accumulator, a single $Q$ output (with separate QU and QD for up-direction and down-direction done flags), and a `R` reset input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CTU Output Equation (Master Formula):**
> 
> $$Q_{CTU} = 1 \iff CV \geq PV$$
> 
> The accumulator updates as:
> 
> $$CV(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \delta(t - t_k) \cdot 1$$
> 
> where $\delta(t - t_k)$ is the discrete unit impulse representing the $k$-th rising edge event at time $t_k$.

## 2.5 Industrial Application Examples (KTU High-Yield)

### Application 1 — Conveyor Belt Sequential Start

In a 3-stage conveyor system, motor 1 must run for **5 seconds** alone, then motor 2 starts and runs for **3 seconds** alongside motor 1, then motor 3 joins. This requires **cascaded TON timers** with the output of T1 enabling T2's start condition, and so on.

### Application 2 — Traffic Light Controller

A traffic light uses a master oscillator (clock-pulse generator) with **3 TON timers** (red, yellow, green), each preset to a specific duration. The done-bit of timer 1 resets it and starts timer 2, creating a state machine.

### Application 3 — Product Counting and Box Packing

A photoelectric sensor pulses the `CU` input of a CTU counter. When 12 products are counted, the output activates a solenoid that pushes the box onto the next conveyor. A reset pulse returns the counter to 0 for the next cycle.

### Application 4 — Motor Star-Delta Starter

Two TON timers (T1 = 5 s star-time, T2 = 100 ms transition delay) coordinate the contactor switching. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ET = N_{pulses} \times T_b$ | Elapsed time = number of clock pulses × time base |
| 2 | $PV = T_{delay} / T_b$ | Preset value = desired delay / time base (rounded up) |
| 3 | $T_{total} = T_{scan} \times N_{cycles}$ | Total elapsed wall-clock time = scan time × number of scan cycles |
| 4 | $Q_{CTU} = 1 \iff CV \geq PV$ | Counter output assertion condition |
| 5 | $CV_{CTUD}(k) = CV(k-1) + \Delta_{CU}(k) - \Delta_{CD}(k)$ | CTUD accumulator recursion |
| 6 | $T_{cascade} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3$ | Total time of cascaded TON timers |
| 7 | $f_{max, counter} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot T_{scan}}$ | Maximum countable input frequency (Nyquist for scan-based counters) |
| 8 | $N_{cascaded, total} = PV_1 \times PV_2$ | Cascaded counter multiplication (e.g., two 9999 counters → 99,980,001 counts) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** Timer-counter combinations are the backbone of **SCADA-linked batching systems** in pharmaceutical, bottling, and packaging industries. Understanding them is essential for PEERT861 lab work and for KTU's Module 2 case-study questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting a Desired Delay to Preset Value (PV)

**Problem Context (KTU Exam Standard):** A TON timer is required to produce a delay of **7.5 seconds**. The available time base selections are **10 ms, 100 ms, and 1 s**. Determine the optimal time base and compute the corresponding PV.

### Step 1 — Analyze the Required Delay

We are given the target delay:
$$T_{delay} = 7.5 \text{ s} = 7500 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2 — Test Each Available Time Base

**Test 1: $T_b = 10$ ms**
$$PV = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_b} = \frac{7500 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} = 750 \text{ counts}$$

This is feasible (within 0 – 9999 range), but coarse resolution. The actual delay would be $750 \times 10 \text{ ms} = 7500 \text{ ms} = 7.5 \text{ s}$ — **exact match**.

**Test 2: $T_b = 100$ ms**
$$PV = \frac{7500 \text{ ms}}{100 \text{ ms}} = 75 \text{ counts}$$

Also feasible. Actual delay: $75 \times 100 = 7500$ ms. **Exact match**.

**Test 3: $T_b = 1$ s = 1000 ms**
$$PV = \frac{7500 \text{ ms}}{1000 \text{ ms}} = 7.5 \text{ counts}$$

This is a **non-integer** and thus **invalid** for a digital counter. The PLC would either round up to 8 (giving 8 s, 6.67% error) or reject the value.

### Step 3 — Choose the Optimal Time Base

The best choice is **$T_b = 100$ ms** with **$PV = 75$** because it gives an exact delay while using a moderate number of counts (better noise immunity than very long counts, and finer resolution than 1 s base).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule:** Always pick the **largest time base** that yields an **integer PV** and stays within the counter's word-size range (typically 0 – 9999 or 0 – 65535 in modern PLCs).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cascaded TON Timer Timing Analysis

**Problem Context:** Three TON timers T1, T2, T3 are connected such that:
- T1 starts when input `I0.0 = 1`
- T2 starts when T1's done bit $Q_1$ becomes 1
- T3 starts when T2's done bit $Q_2$ becomes 1

Presets: $PV_1 = 5$ s, $PV_2 = 3$ s, $PV_3 = 2$ s. All using $T_b = 100$ ms. Sketch the timing diagram and determine when $Q_3$ becomes 1.

### Step 1 — Convert Presets to Counts

$$PV_1 = \frac{5 \text{ s}}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 50 \text{ counts}$$

$$PV_2 = \frac{3 \text{ s}}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 30 \text{ counts}$$

$$PV_3 = \frac{2 \text{ s}}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 20 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 2 — Determine the Start Time of Each Timer

| Timer | Start Condition | Start Time $t_{start}$ |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | $I0.0 = 1$ (rising edge) | $t = 0$ s |
| T2 | $Q_1 = 1$ | $t = 5$ s (T1 done) |
| T3 | $Q_2 = 1$ | $t = 5 + 3 = 8$ s |

### Step 3 — Compute the Done Time of Each Timer

$$t_{done,T1} = t_{start,T1} + PV_1 = 0 + 5 = 5 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{done,T2} = t_{start,T2} + PV_2 = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{done,T3} = t_{start,T3} + PV_3 = 8 + 2 = 10 \text{ s}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Cascade Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= PV_1 + PV_2 + PV_3 \\
&= 5 + 3 + 2 \\
&= 10 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Generate the Timing Matrix

| Time (s) | I0.0 | T1.ET | T1.Q | T2.ET | T2.Q | T3.ET | T3.Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 1→ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2.5 | 1 | 2.5 s | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 5.0 | 1 | 5.0 s | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 6.5 | 1 | 5.0 s | 1 | 1.5 s | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 8.0 | 1 | 5.0 s | 1 | 3.0 s | **1** | 0 | 0 |
| 9.0 | 1 | 5.0 s | 1 | 3.0 s | 1 | 1.0 s | 0 |
| 10.0 | 1 | 5.0 s | 1 | 3.0 s | 1 | 2.0 s | **1** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Counter Cascading for High-Count Applications

**Problem Context:** A bottling plant must count up to **50,000 bottles** per shift. The CTU counter has a maximum PV of **9999**. Show how to cascade two CTU counters to achieve the required range.

### Step 1 — Determine Multiplication Factor

Each CTU can hold 0 – 9999 counts. Two cascaded counters multiply the range:

$$N_{max,total} = PV_1 \times PV_2 = 9999 \times 9999 = 99{,}980{,}001 \text{ counts}$$

Since 50,000 is well within this range, the cascade is feasible.

### Step 2 — Choose PVs

For 50,000 bottles, choose $PV_1 = 100$ and $PV_2 = 500$. The logic: Counter 1 counts 0–99 (the "ones" group of 100), and every time Counter 1 reaches 100, its done bit pulses the `CU` input of Counter 2 (which counts the "hundreds"). The final output triggers at:

$$N_{final} = PV_1 \times PV_2 = 100 \times 500 = 50{,}000 \text{ bottles}$$

### Step 3 — Verify with Recursion Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
CV_1(k) &= k \mod PV_1 \\
CV_2(k) &= \left\lfloor \frac{k}{PV_1} \right\rfloor \\
Q_{total} &= 1 \iff (CV_1 = PV_1 - 1 \text{ AND } CV_2 = PV_2 - 1)
\end{aligned}$$

At $k = 50{,}000$:
$$CV_1 = 50{,}000 \mod 100 = 0 \text{ (just rolled over)}$$
$$CV_2 = \lfloor 50{,}000 / 100 \rfloor = 500 = PV_2 \rightarrow Q = 1$$

The output asserts at the 50,000th bottle, triggering the end-of-shift alarm. ✔

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Countable Input Frequency

A counter can only reliably count pulses that are **slower than twice the scan time** (Nyquist criterion applied to scan-based edge detection). Derive the maximum safe input frequency.

### Step 1 — State the Nyquist Constraint

For a digital sampling system, the input must change at less than half the sampling rate to be unambiguously detected:

$$f_{max,input} \leq \frac{f_{sample}}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute PLC Scan Time

The PLC scans (samples) the input at frequency $f_{sample} = 1 / T_{scan}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{max,input} &\leq \frac{1}{2 \cdot T_{scan}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \times 10 \text{ ms}} \quad \text{(assuming } T_{scan} = 10 \text{ ms)} \\
&= 50 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

For a typical industrial sensor with pulse rate of 100 Hz, the scan time must satisfy:
$$T_{scan} \leq \frac{1}{2 \times 100} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Pitfall:** Many students assume the counter can count at any frequency. In reality, **missed counts** occur if the input pulse rate exceeds the Nyquist limit. High-speed counters (HSC) with hardware-based edge detection are required above ~1 kHz.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timer Function Block — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the internal signal flow of a generic TON timer function block, mapping every input, output, and internal register.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IN Input Boolean] --> B[Edge Detector Rising]
    B --> C[Enable Accumulator]
    D[Time Base T_b 10 ms or 100 ms] --> E[Internal Clock Oscillator]
    E --> C
    C --> F[ET Elapsed Time Register]
    F --> G[Comparator ET vs PV]
    H[PV Preset Value User Set] --> G
    G --> I{ET Greater or Equal PV}
    I -->|Yes| J[Q Output Set to 1]
    I -->|No| K[Q Output Held at 0]
    A --> L[Reset Logic]
    L --> F
    A --> M[Output Buffer]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    M --> N[Q Output Boolean to Ladder Logic]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The IN input enables a clock-driven accumulator. Each clock pulse (period = $T_b$) increments ET. A comparator continuously checks if ET has reached PV. When the condition is met, Q is set. If IN drops to 0 at any moment, the reset logic zeros out ET.

---

## 4.2 CTU Counter — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CU Count Up Input] --> B[Rising Edge Detector]
    B --> C[Adder Block]
    D[1 Constant] --> C
    C --> E[CV Current Value Register]
    E --> F[Comparator CV vs PV]
    G[PV Preset Value] --> F
    F --> H{CV Greater or Equal PV}
    H -->|Yes| I[Q Output 1]
    H -->|No| J[Q Output 0]
    K[R Reset Input] --> L[Reset Logic]
    L --> E
    L --> I
    L --> J
    E --> M[Display CV to HMI]
```

---

## 4.3 Conveyor Belt Sequential Start — Ladder Logic Schematic

This is the **classic 3-stage conveyor application** commonly asked in KTU exams. The diagram shows the rung-by-rung ladder layout and the electrical wiring.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Rung1[Rung 1 - Master Start]
        A1[PB_START Normally Open] --> A2[T1 TON PV 5s]
        A2 --> A3[M1 Conveyor Motor 1 Contactor]
    end
    subgraph Rung2[Rung 2 - Stage 2 Enable]
        B1[T1.Q Done Bit] --> B2[T2 TON PV 3s]
        B2 --> B3[M2 Conveyor Motor 2 Contactor]
    end
    subgraph Rung3[Rung 3 - Stage 3 Enable]
        C1[T2.Q Done Bit] --> C2[T3 TON PV 2s]
        C2 --> C3[M3 Conveyor Motor 3 Contactor]
    end
    subgraph Rung4[Rung 4 - Emergency Stop]
        D1[PB_STOP Normally Closed] --> D2[Master Seal In Coil M1 M2 M3]
    end
```

**Operating Sequence:**
- $t = 0$ s: PB_START pressed → T1 starts counting, M1 energizes immediately (if wired as TON-of-output-style) or after 5 s (if wired with T1.Q in series).
- $t = 5$ s: T1 done → T2 starts, M2 energizes.
- $t = 8$ s: T2 done → T3 starts, M3 energizes.
- $t = 10$ s: T3 done → "System Ready" indicator.

---

## 4.4 Product Counter with Box Packing — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Photoelectric Sensor at Conveyor] -->|Pulse per Bottle| B[CU Input of CTU1]
    B --> C{CTU1 CV 12}
    C -->|Yes| D[Q Output 1]
    C -->|No| B
    D --> E[Solenoid Actuator Push Box]
    D --> F[Generate Reset Pulse 100ms One-Shot]
    F --> G[R Input of CTU1]
    G --> B
    D --> H[HMI Display Total Boxes]
```

**Interpretation:** Each bottle passing the sensor pulses CTU1's CU input. When 12 bottles have passed, the box is full. The solenoid pushes the box, and a 100 ms one-shot timer generates a reset pulse to return the counter to 0 for the next box.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between TON and TOF timer instructions in a PLC. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | TON (ON-Delay) | TOF (OFF-Delay) |
|---|---|---|
| Output ON transition | Delayed by PV | Immediate |
| Output OFF transition | Immediate | Delayed by PV |
| ET reset condition | When IN = 0 | When IN = 1 again |
| Latch of Q | Yes, while IN = 1 | Yes, while ET < PV after IN drops |

**TON Application (1 mark):** Star-delta motor starter — the motor runs in star configuration for 5 s before switching to delta.

**TOF Application (1 mark):** Cooling-fan run-on after a furnace heater turns off — fan continues for 30 s to dissipate residual heat.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the function of a CTUD (Count Up/Down Counter) with a suitable industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A CTUD counter has two count inputs — `CU` (Count Up) and `CD` (Count Down) — and a single accumulator `CV`. On every rising edge of CU, CV increments by 1; on every rising edge of CD, CV decrements by 1. The counter has two output bits:
- $QU = 1$ when $CV \geq PV$ (upper threshold reached)
- $QD = 1$ when $CV \leq 0$ (lower threshold reached)

**Industrial Example (2 marks):** **Parking-lot occupancy counter.** Entry barriers pulse CU, exit barriers pulse CD. PV = 500 (max capacity). When $QU = 1$, the entry barrier locks and the "FULL" sign illuminates. When a car exits, $CD$ decrements and reopens entry. The HMI displays live $CV$ as the current occupancy count.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Draw and explain the ladder logic diagram for a **motor star-delta starter** using two TON timers. Assume star-time = 5 s and transition delay = 100 ms. Show all rungs, contactor labels, and timer presets clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conveyor system has three belts M1, M2, M3. M1 must start first, M2 must start **4 seconds after M1**, and M3 must start **6 seconds after M1**. M2 and M3 must stop simultaneously when a STOP button is pressed. Design the ladder program using TON timers and explain its operation with a timing diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Star-Delta Starter (7 Marks)

**Principle (1 mark):** A star-delta starter initially connects the motor windings in star (low-voltage) configuration to limit starting current. After a preset time (typically 3–10 seconds), the connection switches to delta (full-voltage) for normal running.

**Component List (1 mark):**
- $K_M$ — Main contactor (connects line to motor terminals)
- $K_S$ — Star contactor (shorts terminal ends together)
- $K_D$ — Delta contactor (connects terminals in delta)
- $T_1$ — TON, PV = 5 s, $T_b = 100$ ms → preset = 50 counts
- $T_2$ — TON, PV = 100 ms, $T_b = 100$ ms → preset = 1 count (acts as transition debounce)
- $PB_{start}$, $PB_{stop}$, $OL$ (overload relay)

**Ladder Rungs (3 marks):**

```
Rung 1 (Master Seal-In):
 |--[ PB_start ]--[/ PB_stop ]--[/ OL ]--+--[ K_M ]--( K_M )--|
                                         |                     |
                                         +---------------------+

Rung 2 (Star Contactor — Active for first 5 s):
 |--[ K_M ]--[/ T1.Q ]--[/ T2.Q ]---------( K_S )--|

Rung 3 (Star Timer T1 — Starts when K_M energizes):
 |--[ K_M ]----------------[ TON T1, PV=50 (5s) ]--|

Rung 4 (Transition Debounce T2 — Starts when T1 done):
 |--[ T1.Q ]---------------[ TON T2, PV=1 (100ms) ]--|

Rung 5 (Delta Contactor — Active after 5.1 s, interlocked with K_S):
 |--[ K_M ]--[ T2.Q ]--[/ K_S ]---------( K_D )--|
```

**Operating Sequence (1 mark):**
- $t = 0$: PB_start pressed → $K_M$ seals in. With $T_1.Q = 0$ and $T_2.Q = 0$, $K_S$ energizes → motor runs in star.
- $t = 5$ s: $T_1$ done → $T_1.Q = 1$ → $K_S$ de-energizes; $T_2$ starts.
- $t = 5.1$ s: $T_2$ done → $T_2.Q = 1$ → $K_D$ energizes (interlocked with $/K_S$ to prevent short circuit).
- PB_stop or OL trip → $K_M$ drops → $K_D$ drops → motor stops.
