KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]** #### **Q1. Differentiate between an analog input module and an analog output module in a PLC. (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Analog Input (AI) | Analog Output (AO) | |---|---|---| | **Direction** | Field sensor → PLC | PLC → field actuator | | **Conversion** | A/D (analog → digital) | D/A (digital → analog) | | **Memory table** | `AIW0`, `AIW2` (input) | `AQW0`, `AQW2` (output) | | **Signal range** | 0–10 V, 4–20 mA, RTD, TC | 0–10 V, 4–20 mA | | **Example** | Reading pressure transmitter | Driving control valve positioner | **One-line distinction:** An AI module *listens* to a continuous signal and digitises it; an AO module *speaks* a continuous signal driven by a digital word. --- #### **Q2. Why is 4–20 mA preferred over 0–20 mA in industrial analog signal transmission? (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The 4–20 mA current loop is preferred over 0–20 mA for three industrial reasons: 1. **Live-zero (broken-wire detection):** The 4 mA lower bound means that if the loop current falls to 0 mA, the PLC can detect that the wire is broken or the sensor has failed. With 0–20 mA, a reading of 0 mA could either mean "true zero" or "broken wire," causing ambiguity. 2. **Two-wire transmitter power:** The 4 mA baseline supplies sufficient current to power the remote transmitter's electronics through the same two wires that carry the signal — eliminating the need for separate power cables. 3. **Noise immunity and long-distance fidelity:** Current signals are immune to voltage drops along long cable runs, contact resistance, and EMI, unlike voltage signals that suffer from $V = I R$ losses. --- ### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — 14-Mark Pattern, Internal Choice Pattern]** --- #### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the functional block diagram of a typical PLC analog input module. Describe each stage in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 12-bit unipolar ADC is used in a PLC analog input module. The input voltage range is 0–10 V. Determine: **(7 marks)** (i) The resolution (LSB) of the ADC in mV. (ii) The digital output code in hex when $V_{in} = 6.25$ V. (iii) The maximum quantization error. --- #### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the Successive Approximation Register (SAR) type A/D converter used in PLC analog input modules. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A level transmitter with 4–20 mA output corresponds to a tank level of 0–8 m. The current is measured by passing it through a 250 Ω sense resistor and feeding it to a 12-bit ADC. (i) Determine the digital code when the level is 5 m. **(3 marks)** (ii) Determine the level in metres when the digital code is 3072. **(4 marks)** --- ### 5.3 Model Solutions to Question A #### **Solution to Q-A (a) — Functional block diagram of PLC AI module (7 marks)** | Stage | Function | Key Element | |---|---|---| | 1. Protection | Clamp transients, limit overvoltage | TVS diode, MOV, fuse | | 2. Signal Conditioning | Amplify weak sensor signal; reject noise | Instrumentation amplifier, RC low-pass filter | | 3. Multiplexing | Share one ADC across N channels | 8:1 or 16:1 analog MUX | | 4. Sample and Hold | Freeze value during conversion | MOSFET switch + hold capacitor | | 5. A/D Conversion | Convert held voltage to digital word | SAR / Σ-Δ / Flash ADC | | 6. Isolation | Galvanic separation field side ↔ logic | Optocoupler / capacitive isolator (1500 V RMS) | | 7. CPU Interface | Transfer binary word to memory table | Backplane serial/parallel bus | **Marks distribution (typical KTU 7-mark scheme):** - Block diagram — 3 marks - Description of any 4 stages — 4 marks (1 mark each) #### **Solution to Q-A (b) — 12-bit ADC, 0–10 V (7 marks)** **Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $V_{in} = 6.25$ V. **(i) Resolution in mV (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} LSB &= \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 0.002441406\,\text{V} \\ &\approx 2.44\,\text{mV} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Digital code for 6.25 V (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{out} &= \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{LSB}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{6.25}{0.002441406}\right) \\ &= \text{round}(2560) = 2560_{10} \end{aligned} $$ Convert to hex: $2560 \div 16 = 160$ rem $0$; $160 \div 16 = 10$ rem $0$; $10 = A_{16}$. So $2560_{10} = \mathbf{A00_{16}}$. **(iii) Maximum quantization error (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_q &= \pm\frac{LSB}{2} = \pm\frac{2.44\,\text{mV}}{2} = \pm 1.22\,\text{mV} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 5.4 Model Solutions to Question B #### **Solution to Q-B (a) — SAR ADC Operation (7 marks)** The **Successive Approximation Register ADC** is a closed-loop converter that uses a binary search algorithm. It performs one bit-test per clock cycle, completing the conversion in exactly **n clock cycles** for an n-bit ADC. **Step-by-step operation:** 1. The SAR is cleared to all zeros. 2. The MSB (bit 11 for a 12-bit ADC) is set to 1; all other bits remain 0. 3. The binary word in the SAR is fed to an internal DAC which produces $V_{DAC} = V_{ref}/2$. 4. The comparator compares $V_{in}$ with $V_{DAC}$: - If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$ → MSB is **kept at 1**. - If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$ → MSB is **reset to 0**. 5. The next bit is set to 1 and the process repeats. 6. After n iterations, the SAR holds the final digital code. **Advantages:** Fixed conversion time, medium speed, low power, good resolution (12–18 bits), low cost. These properties make it the most common ADC in industrial PLC AI modules. **Marks distribution:** - Block diagram of SAR — 3 marks - Step-by-step operation — 3 marks - Advantages — 1 mark #### **Solution to Q-B (b) — 4–20 mA Level Transmitter (7 marks)** **Given:** - Range: 0–8 m ↔ 4–20 mA - $R_{sense} = 250\,\Omega$ - $n = 12$ bits Span calculations: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta I &= 20 - 4 = 16\,\text{mA} \\ \Delta V &= \Delta I \times R_{sense} = 16 \times 0.25 = 4\,\text{V} \\ V_{offset} &= 4 \times 0.25 = 1\,\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Resolution (1 V to 5 V ADC range):** $$ \begin{aligned} LSB = \frac{\Delta V}{2^n} = \frac{4}{4096} = 0.9766\,\text{mV} \end{aligned} $$ **(i) Digital code for level = 5 m (3 marks):** Voltage at ADC: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{in} &= V_{offset} + \Delta V \cdot \frac{5}{8} = 1 + 4 \cdot 0.625 = 3.5\,\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_{out} &= \text{round}\!\left(\frac{3.5 - 1.0}{0.0009766}\right) \\ &= \text{round}(2559.875) \\ &= 2560_{10} = 0A00_{16} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Level for digital code = 3072 (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{in} &= 1.0 + 3072 \times 0.0009766 = 1.0 + 3.0010 = 4.0010\,\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ Current: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= \frac{V_{in}}{250} = \frac{4.0010}{250} = 16.004\,\text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ Level: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Level} &= \left(\frac{I - 4}{16}\right) \times 8 = \left(\frac{16.004 - 4}{16}\right) \times 8 = \frac{12.004}{16} \times 8 \\ &= 6.002\,\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** Level ≈ **6.0 m**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the 4 mA offset** — students use 0 mA as zero when scaling a 4–20 mA loop, losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing 0–10 V with 1–5 V ADC range** — when a sense resistor is used, the ADC sees 1–5 V, not 0–5 V. The LSB is `4 V / 4096`, not `5 V / 4096`. > 3. **Using $2^n - 1 = 4095$ in the LSB formula** — the standard convention is `LSB = V_FS / 2^n`, using 4096, not 4095. Markers may or may not deduct for 4095; play safe with 4096. > 4. **Not converting decimal to hexadecimal** — KTU often asks for "the digital code in hex" — students forget to convert and lose 1 mark. > 5. **Mixing up sampling rate and resolution** — these are *independent* parameters. Resolution is bit-depth; sampling rate is time-domain. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** - **Definition recall:** Analog module = translator between continuous real-world signal and PLC binary logic. - **Classification (AI / AO / AIO):** Know the *direction* of each. - **Standard ranges:** **0–10 V**, **±10 V**, **4–20 mA** — know the 4 mA "live zero" advantage. - **Resolution formula:** $LSB = V_{FS}/2^n$. Memorize. - **Scaling formula (4–20 mA → EU):** $EU = \left(\frac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}}\right) (EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min}$. Always subtract 4 mA offset. - **ADC types:** SAR (general purpose, 12-bit), Σ-Δ (precision, RTD/strain), Flash (fast, 8-bit), Dual-slope (slow, 16-bit). Know which is used where. - **Nyquist theorem:** $f_{sample} \geq 2 f_{max}$. Always pair ADC with anti-alias filter at $f_{cutoff} = f_{sample}/2$. - **Block diagram of AI module:** 7 stages — Protection → Conditioning → MUX → S/H → ADC → Isolation → CPU. - **Block diagram of AO module:** Reverse pipeline — CPU → DAC → Buffer → V/I converter → Isolation → Actuator. - **Specialty modules:** TC (CJC, linearization), RTD (3/4-wire, excitation current), Strain gauge (bridge, mV/V). - **Resolution ladder:** 8-bit (256) → 12-bit (4096) → 16-bit (65 536) → 24-bit (Σ-Δ). - **SNR formula:** $SNR_{dB} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ — useful for cross-checking module selection. - **Isolation rating:** Typically **1500 V RMS** between field and logic for industrial modules. - **Quantization error:** $E_q = \pm LSB/2$. - **Two-wire vs four-wire transmitters:** 4–20 mA can be 2-wire (sensor powered by loop), 3-wire (separate ground), or 4-wire (separately powered). - **Most exam-favoured ADC:** SAR — be thorough on its binary-search algorithm and block diagram. - **Anti-alias filter:** RC low-pass with cutoff at the Nyquist frequency; use over-sampling factor of 5× for safety. - **Channel count vs scan time:** $T_{scan} = N_{channels} \times T_{ADC}$ — multiplexing trades speed for cost. - **PLC memory addressing:** AI data in `IW` (Siemens), `%IW` (Schneider), or `I:4.x` (Allen-Bradley); AO data in `QW`/`%QW`/`O:4.x`. --- > **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — PEERT861**

topics.title: Case Study of Tank Level Control System, Bottle Filling System, and Sequential Switching of Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study of Tank Level Control System, Bottle Filling System, and Sequential Switching of Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEERT861 – Module 3):**
> A *Case Study* in PLC applications refers to the systematic design, simulation, and implementation of Ladder Logic Programs (LLP) for **real-world industrial automation problems**. The three canonical case studies mandated by KTU are — *(a) Tank Level Control, (b) Bottle Filling, and (c) Sequential Switching of Motors*. Each case study validates the PLC's ability to interface **Discrete Inputs (DI), Discrete Outputs (DO), Analog Inputs (AI)**, and **Analog Outputs (AO)** with mechanical actuators and field sensors.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a PLC as the "digital brain" of a factory.** Just as your brain tells your hand to lift a glass when you are thirsty (without you consciously calculating muscle force), a PLC tells a pump motor to switch ON when a tank's water falls below a threshold. The three case studies are simply **three different "reflex actions"** programmed into that digital brain.

| Case Study | Real-World Analogy | Human Reflex Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Tank Level Control | Overhead water tank with auto-cut pump | Feeling thirsty and drinking water |
| Bottle Filling | Soft-drink plant conveyor line | Filling a glass of juice to a fixed height |
| Sequential Motor Switching | 3-phase industrial startup of fans/pumps | Clapping thrice with 1-second gap between each clap |

### Core Subsystems Involved

- **Tank Level Control** : Field sensors (float switches, capacitive probes, ultrasonic), analog signal conditioning (4–20 mA / 0–10 V), PID controller block, relay/contactor outputs.
- **Bottle Filling System** : Photo-electric / inductive proximity sensor, conveyor motor, solenoid valve, timer (TON) and counter (CTU) instructions.
- **Sequential Motor Switching** : Three DOL starters, MPCB (Motor Protection Circuit Breaker), thermal overload relay, contactors, and software-based interlocks.

> [!TIP]
> All three systems share a common KTU exam keyword — **"I/O Assignment List / Address Allocation Table"**. Always prepare this table first; it is the highest-yield step in any case study answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Tank Level Control System

A tank-level control loop is fundamentally a **closed-loop feedback system**. The PLC reads the instantaneous liquid height via a level transmitter, compares it to a *set-point (SP)*, computes an error $e(t)$, and actuates the inlet valve or pump.

The control architecture has three modes:

1. **On/Off (Two-Position) Control** — simplest, uses two float switches (HIGH and LOW).
2. **Proportional Control** — analog input, modulating valve.
3. **PID Control** — eliminates steady-state error and overshoot.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The *On/Off* mode is the most commonly asked variant because it integrates a **latch (SET/RESET)** instruction — a guaranteed 14-mark question in any KTU ESE.

#### Latch-Based Operating Logic
- **Start PB** pressed → Pump Motor latches **ON** (SET instruction).
- Water reaches **HIGH level** switch → Pump Motor unlatches (RESET instruction).
- **LOW level** switch also serves as a permissive interlock.
- A **High-High (HH)** alarm triggers an emergency shut-down.

**PID Equation (standard form):**

$$
m(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

where $K_c$ is the proportional gain, $T_i$ is the integral time, and $T_d$ is the derivative time. The PLC implements this in *discrete* form using the *PID* instruction block.

### 2.2 Bottle Filling System

A sequential batch process with **four discrete steps** typically executed by a single PLC scan cycle using **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** or **state-bit logic**:

1. **Step 1** — Bottle detected by photo-sensor → Conveyor advances.
2. **Step 2** — Bottle in position → Filling solenoid energised.
3. **Step 3** — Timer (TON) elapses (or flow-meter preset reached) → Solenoid de-energised.
4. **Step 4** — Capping cylinder actuates → Bottle discharges to next station.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter Instruction (CTU)** is the KTU-favourite instruction here. One bottle = one count. The **preset value (PV)** defines the batch size (e.g., 24 bottles per crate).

### 2.3 Sequential Switching of Motors

Three (or more) motors must start **one after the other with a fixed time gap** to:
- Reduce **inrush current** on the power bus.
- Allow pressure/flow to stabilise in cascade pumping stations.
- Implement safety-critical process startup sequences.

The PLC uses **three TON timers** cascaded in series:

$$
\text{Motor}_1 \xrightarrow{t_1} \text{Motor}_2 \xrightarrow{t_2} \text{Motor}_3
$$

Each timer triggers the contactor of the next motor and also *enables* the next timer's start condition.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Instruction | Definition | Typical Range / Value | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{AI}$ | Analog Input voltage from level transmitter | **0 – 10 V** or **4 – 20 mA** | Convert to engineering units using scaling |
| $h_{\text{tank}}$ | Actual tank level (cm) | 0 – 200 cm | Computed from $V_{AI}$ |
| $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Error signal | Engineering units | Sign convention: +ve = below set-point |
| $K_c$ | Proportional gain | 0.1 – 10 | Too high → oscillation |
| $T_i$ | Integral time (reset) | 1 – 100 s | Eliminates steady-state error |
| $T_d$ | Derivative time (rate) | 0.1 – 10 s | Acts on rate of change |
| $N_{\text{batch}}$ | Bottle count per batch | 12 / 24 / 48 | Set as CTU preset |
| $t_{\text{seq}}$ | Inter-motor delay | **10 s, 20 s, 30 s** | Standard KTU values |
| Scan Time $T_s$ | PLC cycle time | **10 – 20 ms** | Timer accuracy depends on this |
| $\Delta P_{\text{pump}}$ | Pump differential pressure | 1 – 5 bar | Used for overload alarm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Engine Notice:** All absolute values in formulas above are rendered using LaTeX `\vert` / `\mid` constructs (NOT raw pipe characters), to preserve KTU formula sheet table integrity.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tank Level Control** → Municipal water distribution, chemical reactors, pharmaceutical mixing vessels, nuclear coolant reservoirs.
- **Bottle Filling** → Beverage industry, pharmaceutical syrup lines, cosmetics, dairy plants.
- **Sequential Motor Switching** → Cement kilns, HVAC chiller sequencing, paper mill drives, oil-rig mud pumps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Tank Level — Analog Scaling Derivation

A level transmitter outputs **4 – 20 mA** corresponding to **0 – 100 %** tank level. The PLC's analog input module converts this to a raw count of **0 – 32000** (for a 16-bit ADC).

**Derivation of scaling equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw count} &= \frac{(I_{\text{in}} - 4\,\text{mA})}{16\,\text{mA}} \times 32000 \\[4pt]
\text{Level (in \%)} &= \frac{\text{Raw count}}{32000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\text{Level (in cm)} &= \frac{\text{Raw count}}{32000} \times H_{\max}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $H_{\max}$ is the maximum tank height. For example, if $H_{\max} = 200\,\text{cm}$ and the raw count is **16000**, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level} &= \frac{16000}{32000} \times 200\,\text{cm} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 200 \\[4pt]
&= 100\,\text{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means the tank is exactly half-full, and the PLC will decide whether to start or stop the pump based on the **set-point (e.g., 150 cm)**.

### 3.2 Bottle Filling — Timer Preset Calculation

Suppose the nozzle flow rate is $Q = 500\,\text{mL/s}$ and the desired fill volume per bottle is $V = 250\,\text{mL}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{fill}} &= \frac{V}{Q} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{250\,\text{mL}}{500\,\text{mL/s}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This 0.5 s is the **TON timer preset value** in the ladder logic. For a batch of 24 bottles, the **CTU counter preset** is $PV = 24$.

### 3.3 Sequential Switching — Total Startup Time

If three motors start sequentially with delays of 10 s, 20 s, and 30 s, the **total plant startup time** $T_{\text{total}}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 \\[4pt]
&= 10\,\text{s} + 20\,\text{s} + 30\,\text{s} \\[4pt]
&= 60\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

In-rush current drawn is now *only that of the largest motor* at any instant, instead of the *sum of all three* simultaneously — a **66 % reduction** in peak demand.

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Tank Level | Bottle Filling | Motor Sequencing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Sensor | Float / Ultrasonic | Photo-electric / Proximity | Contactors / OL relay |
| Analog / Digital | AI + DI + DO | DI + DO + Counter | DI + DO + Timers |
| Key PLC Instruction | PID, MOV, CMP | CTU, TON, SFC | TON, RTO, Interlocks |
| Cycle Time | Seconds – minutes | 1 – 5 s / bottle | Hours (continuous) |
| Critical Failure | Tank overflow | Mis-fill / spillage | In-rush trip |
| Typical KTU Marks Weight | 5 / 14 | 4 / 14 | 5 / 14 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tank Level Control — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Tank Vessel] --> B[Level Transmitter 4-20mA]
    B --> C[Analog Input Module AI]
    C --> D[PLC CPU Scan Cycle]
    D --> E{Compare Level vs SP}
    E -->|Level is LOW| F[SET Pump Motor Coil]
    E -->|Level is HIGH| G[RESET Pump Motor Coil]
    F --> H[Contactor K1 Energised]
    H --> I[Three Phase Pump Motor ON]
    G --> J[Contactor K1 De-energised]
    J --> K[Pump Motor OFF]
    D --> L[HH Alarm Logic]
    L --> M[Emergency Shutdown Relay]
```

### 4.2 Bottle Filling — Sequential Function Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Start PB] --> S2[Conveyor Motor ON]
    S2 --> S3{Bottle Detected by PS1}
    S3 -->|No| S2
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Stop Conveyor at Fill Station]
    S4 --> S5[Energise Fill Solenoid Y1]
    S5 --> S6[TON Timer Starts t equals 0.5s]
    S6 --> S7{Timer Done}
    S7 -->|No| S6
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[De-energise Y1 Reset Timer]
    S8 --> S9[CTU Counter increments]
    S9 --> S10{Count equals 24}
    S10 -->|No| S11[Move bottle to capping]
    S11 --> S2
    S10 -->|Yes| S12[Batch Complete Lamp ON]
    S12 --> S13[Reset Counter to Zero]
```

### 4.3 Sequential Motor Switching — Timer Cascade

```mermaid
graph TD
    M0[Master Start PB] --> T1[TON Timer T1 Preset 10s]
    T1 --> M1A[Motor 1 Contactor K1 Energised]
    T1 --> T2[TON Timer T2 Preset 20s]
    T2 --> M2A[Motor 2 Contactor K2 Energised]
    T2 --> T3[TON Timer T3 Preset 30s]
    T3 --> M3A[Motor 3 Contactor K3 Energised]
    M1A --> M2A
    M2A --> M3A
    M3A --> N1[System Ready Lamp ON]
    M0 --> STOP[Master Stop PB]
    STOP --> RST[RESET all three contactors]
```

### 4.4 Unified I/O Address Allocation Table

| Address | Tag Name | Device Type | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START_PB | NO Push-button | Master start |
| I0.1 | STOP_PB | NC Push-button | Master stop |
| I0.2 | LVL_LOW | Float switch (DI) | Low-level sensor |
| I0.3 | LVL_HIGH | Float switch (DI) | High-level sensor |
| I0.4 | BOTTLE_PS | Proximity sensor (DI) | Bottle detected |
| AIW0 | LVL_AI | Ultrasonic 4-20 mA | Continuous level |
| Q0.0 | PUMP_MTR | Contactor coil | Pump motor |
| Q0.1 | CONVEYOR | Contactor coil | Conveyor motor |
| Q0.2 | FILL_SOL | Solenoid valve | Fill nozzle |
| Q0.3 | CAP_CYL | Cylinder solenoid | Capping actuator |
| Q0.4 | M1_RUN | Contactor K1 | Motor 1 |
| Q0.5 | M2_RUN | Contactor K2 | Motor 2 |
| Q0.6 | M3_RUN | Contactor K3 | Motor 3 |
| Q0.7 | ALARM_HH | Buzzer + Lamp | High-High alarm |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. List any three industrial applications of PLC-based tank level control systems.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Municipal overhead water tanks** — automatic pump ON/OFF based on level feedback.
2. **Chemical mixing reactors** — maintaining precise liquid level during exothermic reactions.
3. **Pharmaceutical storage vessels** — ensuring sterile fluid inventory with HH alarms.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the purpose of using timers in sequential motor switching?**

**Model Answer:**
Timers introduce a **predefined time delay** between the start of successive motors. This **reduces peak inrush current**, prevents mains voltage dip, allows process stabilisation, and implements software interlocks for safe cascade startup. Typical preset values are 10 s, 20 s, 30 s for a three-motor system.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In case-study answers, students *commonly lose marks* by:
> 1. **Skipping the I/O Address Table** — it is a mandatory pre-ladder step; missing it costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Not labelling every rung with comments** — KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for unreadable logic.
> 3. **Forgetting the emergency stop (ESTOP) rung** — considered a safety violation, attracts **1 mark** penalty.
> 4. **Writing only ladder logic without process description** — the *Process Flow Diagram / Mermaid chart* carries **2 marks** independently.

#### Question A — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) Design a PLC-based Tank Level Control System for an industrial water reservoir. The system must use TWO float switches (LOW and HIGH), a master start push-button, and a pump motor. Draw the I/O address table and the complete ladder logic diagram. Also explain the role of latch and unlatch instructions.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Modify the above system to incorporate an EMERGENCY HIGH-HIGH (HH) level alarm using an additional float switch. Draw the modified ladder logic and explain how the alarm latches even if the level subsequently falls.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The I/O allocation is:

| Address | Tag | Device |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START | NO PB |
| I0.1 | LOW | Float switch (NC) |
| I0.2 | HIGH | Float switch (NC) |
| I0.3 | STOP | NC PB |
| Q0.0 | PUMP | Motor contactor |
| Q0.1 | RUN_LAMP | Indicator |

**Ladder Rungs:**
- **Rung 1:** `START` OR `PUMP` → `PUMP` (SET latch).
- **Rung 2:** `STOP` OR `HIGH` → `PUMP` (RESET unlatch).
- **Rung 3:** `LOW` → `RUN_LAMP` (system healthy indicator).
- **Rung 4:** `PUMP` → `RUN_LAMP` (running feedback).

**SET (Latch)** keeps the pump ON after the START button is released. **RESET (Unlatch)** turns the pump OFF when HIGH level is reached. This implements *memory retentive behaviour* — essential for any PLC automation.

**(b)** A third float switch `HH` at address **I0.4** is added.

- **Rung 5:** `HH` → `ALARM_LATCH` (SET).
- **Rung 6:** `ALARM_ACK_PB` (I0.5) → `ALARM_LATCH` (RESET).
- **Rung 7:** `ALARM_LATCH` → `BUZZER` (Q0.2).
- **Rung 8:** `ALARM_LATCH` → `PUMP` (RESET) — forces pump OFF.

Once `HH` is triggered, the alarm latches via the SET instruction and remains ON **even if water level drops**, until an operator presses the acknowledge push-button. This is the classic *fail-safe alarm philosophy* required by **ISA-18.2 / IEC 61511** standards.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice Option 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) Design a PLC ladder logic for an Automatic Bottle Filling System.** The conveyor runs until a bottle is detected by a proximity sensor; then the filling solenoid energises for 2 seconds; after which the bottle is counted and the cycle repeats for a batch of 12 bottles. Draw the I/O table and ladder logic. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Design a Sequential Motor Switching system for THREE motors using PLC timers.** Motor 1 starts immediately, Motor 2 after 10 s, and Motor 3 after 25 s. A single STOP push-button must switch OFF all motors. Draw the I/O table, ladder logic, and explain the role of the enable contact in each rung. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** I/O table:

| Address | Tag | Device |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START | NO PB |
| I0.1 | STOP | NC PB |
| I0.2 | BOTTLE_PS | Proximity sensor |
| Q0.0 | CONVEYOR | Motor contactor |
| Q0.1 | FILL_SOL | Solenoid valve |
| Q0.2 | BATCH_DONE | Indicator lamp |
| C0 | COUNT | CTU counter, PV = 12 |
| T37 | FILL_TMR | TON timer, preset 2 s |

**Rungs:**
1. `START` AND `NOT STOP` AND `NOT BATCH_DONE` → `CONVEYOR` (latch).
2. `BOTTLE_PS` → `CONVEYOR` (unlatch) AND `FILL_SOL` (latch) AND `T37` (start).
3. `T37.DN` → `FILL_SOL` (unlatch) AND `CONVEYOR` (re-latch) AND `C0` (count up).
4. `C0.DN` → `BATCH_DONE` (latch).
5. `BATCH_DONE` → `CONVEYOR` (unlatch) AND `BATCH_DONE` lamp.

The **timer T37** with preset 2 s controls fill duration; **counter C0** tracks batch completion.

**(b)** I/O table:

| Address | Tag | Device |
|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | START | NO PB |
| I0.1 | STOP | NC PB |
| Q0.1 | M1 | Motor 1 contactor |
| Q0.2 | M2 | Motor 2 contactor |
| Q0.3 | M3 | Motor 3 contactor |
| T37 | T1 | TON, preset 10 s |
| T38 | T2 | TON, preset 15 s (additional delay = 25-10) |

**Rungs:**
1. `START` AND `NOT STOP` AND `T37.DN` → `M1` (latch).
2. `START` AND `NOT STOP` → `T37` (start).
3. `T37.DN` AND `NOT STOP` AND `T38.DN` → `M2` (latch).
4. `T37.DN` AND `NOT STOP` → `T38` (start).
5. `T38.DN` AND `NOT STOP` → `M3` (latch).
6. `STOP` OR overload → `M1`, `M2`, `M3` (unlatch all).

The **enable contact `T37.DN`** ensures Motor 2 only starts after 10 s have elapsed, while `T38.DN` allows Motor 3 to start at 25 s total. The `NOT STOP` contact in every rung guarantees **centralised emergency shutdown**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Always **draw the I/O Address Table first** — it is worth 2 marks independently.
- ✅ Use **SET (Latch) / RESET (Unlatch)** instructions for retentive outputs like pumps and alarms.
- ✅ **On/Off level control** = two float switches + latch logic (most asked KTU variant).
- ✅ **Bottle filling** combines **TON timer + CTU counter** for time-based and quantity-based control.
- ✅ **Sequential motor switching** uses **cascaded TON timers** to stagger starts and reduce inrush.
- ✅ Include an **Emergency Stop (ESTOP) rung** in every ladder — fail-safe philosophy is mandatory.
- ✅ **PID control** uses the discrete form of $m(t) = K_c \left[ e + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt + T_d \frac{de}{dt} \right]$.
- ✅ **Scaling equation** for 4–20 mA → 0–100 %: $\text{Level (\%)} = \frac{\text{Raw count}}{32000} \times 100$.
- ✅ For a 250 mL fill at 500 mL/s, the **timer preset** is **0.5 s** (always compute $t = V/Q$).
- ✅ Standard KTU **sequential delay** values are 10 s, 20 s, 30 s for three motors.
- ✅ **Latching alarm** logic must remain ON until operator acknowledgement — even if the triggering condition clears.
- ✅ Practice **at least three full ladder diagrams** before the exam — KTU ESE allocates 4–6 marks purely for the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sampling Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Sampling Theorem** (also known as the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**) is the foundational pillar of digital signal processing and Data Acquisition Systems (DAS). It states that:

> A continuous-time, **band-limited** analog signal $x(t)$ whose highest frequency component does not exceed a maximum frequency $f_m$ (in Hertz) can be completely and uniquely reconstructed from its discrete samples $x(nT_s)$, provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ is greater than or equal to **twice** the highest frequency present in the signal.

Mathematically, the condition is expressed as:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

The minimum sampling rate required to satisfy this condition is called the **Nyquist Rate** ($f_N$):

$$f_N = 2 f_m \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

The corresponding maximum allowable sampling interval (time gap between two consecutive samples) is the **Nyquist Interval** ($T_N$):

$$T_N = \frac{1}{f_N} = \frac{1}{2 f_m} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Sampling Theorem is the gateway concept for understanding Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC), Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC), and the necessity of anti-aliasing filters in any PLC-based or industrial Data Acquisition System.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stroboscopic Wheel" Intuition

Imagine a spinning bicycle wheel with a single white spoke. If you look at the wheel continuously, you see a smooth blur — the wheel is rotating. But what if you take photographs of it at fixed time intervals?

- **If you take photos very fast** (e.g., 100 photos per second), each photo shows the spoke at a slightly different angle, and you can correctly reconstruct the rotational motion.
- **If you take photos too slowly** (e.g., 1 photo per second), the spoke appears to be *stationary*, or even rotating *backwards*. This is an illusion — a misrepresentation of reality.

This illusion is precisely what we call **aliasing** in signal processing. The "too slow" camera is sampling at a rate below the **Nyquist Rate**, and the true high-frequency motion is masquerading as a false low-frequency motion.

### Real-World Engineering Analogy
- **Cinema Films (24 FPS):** A movie camera samples the real world 24 times every second. The human eye can only perceive motion smoothly up to about 12 Hz, so 24 FPS is safely above the Nyquist rate for visual perception. If the film were shot at 1 frame per second, characters would appear to *jump* in unnatural, jerky motions — aliasing in real life.
- **Slow-Motion Replay:** To capture a fast-moving cricket ball, broadcasters use cameras sampling at **300 to 1000 FPS**, well above the Nyquist rate of the ball's spinning motion.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|:------:|---------|
| Highest Signal Frequency | $f_m$ | The maximum frequency component in the bandlimited signal |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | Number of samples taken per second from the analog signal |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_N$ | Minimum permissible $f_s = 2f_m$ |
| Nyquist Interval | $T_N$ | Maximum permissible sampling period $T_s = 1/f_N$ |
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | Time gap between two consecutive samples |
| Aliasing | — | False low-frequency components appearing when $f_s < 2f_m$ |
| Anti-Aliasing Filter | — | A Low-Pass Filter (LPF) placed *before* the sampler to limit $f_m$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Sampling Theorem so important for PLCs and DAS?**
> Industrial sensors (RTDs, thermocouples, flow meters, pressure transducers) produce **continuous analog voltages or currents**. A PLC or DAS cannot process these directly — it requires **discrete digital numbers**. The Sampling Theorem guarantees that we can convert the analog signal into numbers *without losing information*, provided we obey $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "How" and "Why"

The Sampling Theorem operates on three pillars:

### Pillar 1: The Analog Input Must Be Band-Limited
- An ideal analog signal has infinite frequency content. A **band-limited signal** has zero energy above some finite frequency $f_m$.
- In real engineering, no signal is perfectly band-limited. Hence, we *force* it to be band-limited by passing it through a sharp **anti-aliasing Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** before sampling.

### Pillar 2: The Sampling Process (Impulse Train Multiplication)
- Mathematically, sampling is achieved by multiplying the continuous signal $x(t)$ by a periodic **unit impulse train** $p(t)$ of period $T_s$.
- In the **frequency domain**, this multiplication becomes **convolution** between $X(f)$ and $P(f)$, producing infinite replicas of the spectrum, centered at multiples of $f_s$.

### Pillar 3: The Reconstruction (Low-Pass Filtering)
- To recover $x(t)$ from its samples, we apply an **ideal Low-Pass Filter** with cutoff frequency $f_c = f_m$ to the sampled spectrum.
- This filter passes the **baseband spectrum** (centered at $f = 0$) and rejects all higher-order replicas (centered at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$).
- Reconstruction is only possible if these spectral replicas **do not overlap**, which requires $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

## 2.2 The Two Failure Modes

| Condition | Result | Consequence |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| $f_s > 2f_m$ | **Oversampling** | Safe reconstruction. Spectral replicas are well-separated. |
| $f_s = 2f_m$ | **Critical Sampling** | Theoretically valid, but practically impossible (requires ideal LPF). |
| $f_s < 2f_m$ | **Undersampling → Aliasing** | Spectral replicas overlap. Information is permanently lost. Reconstruction is impossible. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The exact boundary condition $f_s = 2f_m$ is rarely used in practice. Industrial DAS systems typically operate at $f_s = 2.5 f_m$ to $f_s = 10 f_m$ to provide a **safety margin** against non-ideal filter roll-off and sensor noise.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|:-----:|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Fundamental Sampling Theorem inequality | Hz |
| 2 | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | Nyquist Rate (minimum sampling frequency) | Hz |
| 3 | $T_N = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$ | Nyquist Interval (maximum sampling period) | s |
| 4 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Sampling frequency from sampling period | Hz |
| 5 | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ | Sampling frequency in rad/s | rad/s |
| 6 | $\omega_m = 2\pi f_m$ | Maximum signal frequency in rad/s | rad/s |
| 7 | $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$ | Sampling in time domain (multiplication) | V |
| 8 | $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - n f_s)$ | Sampled spectrum (periodic replication) | V/Hz |
| 9 | $f_a = \vert f_s - f_m \vert$ | Alias frequency (false frequency produced) | Hz |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{f_s}{2 f_m}$ | Samples per cycle (a measure of oversampling) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall:** When calculating alias frequencies, always reduce $f_m$ to its **principal range** $[0, f_s/2]$ by repeatedly subtracting $f_s$ until the result falls within the Nyquist band. A common mistake is to report a "negative" alias frequency.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in PLC and DAS

| Application | Typical $f_m$ | Required $f_s$ | Industrial Context |
|-------------|:-------------:|:--------------:|--------------------|
| Temperature monitoring | 0.1 Hz | 0.2–1 Hz | Slow thermal process in a furnace |
| Pressure transducer | 100 Hz | 250 Hz | Hydraulic press control |
| Vibration analysis | 5 kHz | 12.5 kHz | Motor bearing health monitoring |
| Audio (CD quality) | 20 kHz | 44.1 kHz | Standard PCM audio (slight oversampling above 40 kHz) |
| Power line monitoring | 50 Hz | 2.5–10 kHz | Harmonic analysis up to the 50th harmonic |
| ECG (medical) | 150 Hz | 360–500 Hz | Patient cardiac monitoring in hospitals |

> [!NOTE]
> The compact disc (CD) audio standard of **44.1 kHz** was chosen because the human ear's maximum audible frequency is approximately **20 kHz**, and 44.1 kHz is just above the Nyquist rate of 40 kHz — a textbook example of the Sampling Theorem in consumer electronics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Mathematical Statement of Sampling

**Given:**
A continuous-time band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ (in Hz), i.e., $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$.

**Sampling mechanism:** Multiplying $x(t)$ by a periodic unit impulse train $p(t)$:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

where $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period.

**Step 1 — Sampled Signal in Time Domain**

The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ is the product:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = x(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 2 — Exploiting the Sifting Property**

Using the sifting property of the Dirac delta function $x(t) \delta(t - t_0) = x(t_0) \delta(t - t_0)$:

$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \delta(t - n T_s)$$

This shows that $x_s(t)$ is a weighted impulse train, with each impulse at $t = nT_s$ having a strength equal to the sample value $x(nT_s)$.

**Step 3 — Fourier Transform of the Impulse Train**

The Fourier Transform of $p(t)$ is another impulse train in the frequency domain:

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Frequency Domain of the Sampled Signal**

By the **Multiplication Theorem** of Fourier Transforms, multiplication in time = convolution in frequency:

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f)$$

Substituting the expression for $P(f)$:

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * \left[ f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Convolving with an Impulse**

Convolution with $\delta(f - kf_s)$ simply shifts $X(f)$ to be centered at $f = kf_s$:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 6 — The Reconstruction Condition**

If $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the shifted replicas of $X(f)$ (centered at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$) **do not overlap**. A low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ satisfying $f_m \leq f_c \leq f_s - f_m$ recovers $X(f)$ exactly:

$$X(f) = \frac{1}{f_s} X_s(f) \quad \text{for } \vert f \vert \leq f_m \quad \text{(after LPF)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This rigorous derivation is the **proof** of the Sampling Theorem and is a high-value 7-mark derivation question in KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculating the Alias Frequency

**Problem Setup:** A pure sinusoid $x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ is sampled at $f_s < 2 f_m$.

**Step 1 — Express the Signal as Sum of Two Complex Exponentials**

$$x(t) = \frac{A}{2} \left[ e^{j 2\pi f_m t} + e^{-j 2\pi f_m t} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply Sampling ($t = nT_s = n/f_s$)**

$$x(nT_s) = \frac{A}{2} \left[ e^{j 2\pi f_m n / f_s} + e^{-j 2\pi f_m n / f_s} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Apparent Frequency**

After sampling, the signal is interpreted as if it had an *apparent* (alias) frequency $f_a$ given by:

$$f_a = \left\vert f_m - k f_s \right\vert$$

where $k$ is the integer chosen so that the result falls in the range $\left[ 0, \dfrac{f_s}{2} \right]$.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose $f_m = 800 \text{ Hz}$ and $f_s = 1000 \text{ Hz}$.

Check Nyquist condition: $2 f_m = 1600 \text{ Hz} > f_s = 1000 \text{ Hz}$ → **Aliasing will occur.**

Apply the formula with $k = 1$:

$$f_a = \vert 800 - 1 \cdot 1000 \vert = \vert -200 \vert = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

A true 800 Hz signal sampled at 1 kHz will appear to be a **200 Hz signal** to the reconstruction filter. The high-frequency information is permanently corrupted.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Number of Samples for a Pure Tone

If a pure sinusoid of frequency $f_m$ must be uniquely identified from its samples, the minimum number of samples per cycle $N$ is:

$$N = \frac{f_s}{f_m} \geq \frac{2 f_m}{f_m} = 2$$

This confirms the classical result: **at least 2 samples per cycle** are needed. In practice, engineers use 5–20 samples per cycle to get a faithful waveform representation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sampling Theorem Decision Flow

| Step | Question to Ask | Action |
|:----:|-----------------|--------|
| 1 | What is the maximum frequency $f_m$ in the analog signal? | Identify from sensor datasheet or FFT analysis |
| 2 | Choose $f_s = k \cdot 2f_m$ where $k \in [1.2, 10]$ | Safety factor for non-ideal filtering |
| 3 | Is the signal truly bandlimited? | If not, install an **anti-aliasing LPF** with cutoff $f_c = f_m$ |
| 4 | Does the ADC's maximum sample rate support $f_s$? | Verify ADC datasheet specification |
| 5 | After sampling, does the spectrum show overlap? | Check on spectrum analyzer — if yes, increase $f_s$ |
| 6 | For reconstruction, use a low-pass filter with $f_c = f_m$ | Typically a 2nd- to 8th-order Butterworth filter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Complete DAS Sampling Chain

The diagram below maps the **signal flow** through a complete Data Acquisition System, highlighting the role of the Sampling Theorem at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Sensor Signal x t] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler and Hold Circuit]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Processor PLC or Microcontroller]
    E --> F[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    F --> G[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    G --> H[Reconstructed Analog Output x hat t]

    subgraph "Sampling Theorem Enforced Here"
    B
    C
    end

    subgraph "Storage and Control"
    E
    end

    subgraph "Signal Recovery"
    F
    G
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Anti-Aliasing LPF** is the *first line of defense* — it band-limits the signal to $f_m$ as required by the theorem.
- The **Sample-and-Hold** circuit freezes the analog value during the ADC's conversion time, ensuring the sampled value is stable.
- The **ADC** converts each held sample into a binary number with finite resolution (e.g., 12-bit, 16-bit).
- The **DAC** plus **Reconstruction LPF** performs the inverse operation, recovering an analog waveform from the digital stream.

## 4.2 Spectral Representation — The Effect of Sampling Frequency

The diagram below illustrates the *frequency-domain* effect of the Sampling Theorem, showing three cases.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Case1["Case 1: fs greater than 2fm  Safe Oversampling"]
    S1["Original Spectrum X of centered at 0"]
    S2["Replicas centered at plus minus fs plus minus 2fs"]
    S1 --> S2
    end

    subgraph Case2["Case 2: fs equals 2fm  Critical Sampling"]
    T1["Original Spectrum X of centered at 0"]
    T2["Replicas just touching at fs over 2"]
    T1 --> T2
    end

    subgraph Case3["Case 3: fs less than 2fm  Aliasing Disaster"]
    U1["Original Spectrum X of centered at 0"]
    U2["Replicas overlap with original  information destroyed"]
    U1 --> U2
    end
```

## 4.3 Sampling Process — Time Domain View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Continuous Analog Signal x t] --> P2[Impulse Train Sampler p t]
    P2 --> P3[Sampled Signal xs t weighted impulses]
    P3 --> P4[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    P4 --> P5[Reconstructed Signal x hat t approx equal to x t]

    P2 -. fs .-> P3
    P4 -. fc equals fm .-> P5
```

## 4.4 Anti-Aliasing Filter Placement — Why "Before" the Sampler Matters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Sensor Output with Noise] --> Q2[Anti Aliasing LPF with cutoff fm]
    Q2 --> Q3[Sampler with rate fs greater than 2fm]
    Q3 --> Q4[Clean Digital Samples]

    R1[Wrong Sensor output] --> R2[No filter applied]
    R2 --> R3[Sampler at rate fs]
    R3 --> R4[Corrupted samples containing alias frequencies]

    Q4 --> Safe[Sampling Theorem Satisfied]
    R4 --> Wrong[Aliasing Occurs Information Loss]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** Whenever you draw a DAS block diagram in an exam, always show the anti-aliasing LPF *before* the sampler. Examiners explicitly award marks for correct signal-chain ordering because it demonstrates understanding of the Sampling Theorem's pre-condition.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Sampling Theorem. Define Nyquist Rate and Nyquist Interval.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Sampling Theorem:** A continuous-time, band-limited signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its discrete samples, provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
>
> **Nyquist Rate** ($f_N$): The minimum sampling rate that satisfies the Sampling Theorem, given by $f_N = 2 f_m$ Hz.
>
> **Nyquist Interval** ($T_N$): The maximum allowable time gap between two successive samples, given by $T_N = 1/f_N = 1/(2f_m)$ seconds.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for statement + 1 for Nyquist Rate + 1 for Nyquist Interval.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is aliasing? How is it prevented in a Data Acquisition System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal appear as false low-frequency components in the sampled output, occurring when the sampling rate is below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2f_m$).
>
> **Prevention:** An **anti-aliasing Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** with cutoff frequency $f_c = f_m$ is placed *before* the sampler to band-limit the input signal. Additionally, the sampling rate is selected with a safety margin (typically $f_s = 2.5 f_m$ to $5 f_m$).

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for prevention methods.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024):** The student must attempt **exactly one** of the two alternatives. Each alternative is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the Sampling Theorem mathematically. State and explain the Nyquist criterion. (7 marks)

**(b)** A music signal contains frequencies from 50 Hz to 15 kHz. Determine:
- (i) The Nyquist rate
- (ii) The Nyquist interval
- (iii) The minimum sampling frequency to be used
- (iv) The number of samples collected in 1 second
- (v) If $f_s = 32$ kHz is used, is aliasing avoided? Justify.

(7 marks)

---

### Solution to Question B-A

#### Part (a) — Mathematical Derivation of the Sampling Theorem (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Let $x(t)$ be a band-limited signal with $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$.

**Step 2:** Sampling is achieved by multiplying $x(t)$ with an impulse train:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 3:** The sampled signal is:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 4:** Taking the Fourier Transform of $p(t)$:

$$P(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 5:** Using the convolution theorem, the spectrum of the sampled signal is:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 6:** If $f_s \geq 2f_m$, the replicas do not overlap. A low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ in the range $[f_m, f_s - f_m]$ extracts the original spectrum $X(f)$ exactly.

**Nyquist Criterion:** The sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency in the signal: $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

*(Marks: 1 for each setup step, 1 for the convolution result, 1 for the recovery condition, 1 for the Nyquist statement.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $f_{min} = 50$ Hz, $f_{max} = f_m = 15$ kHz = 15,000 Hz.

**(i) Nyquist Rate:**

$$f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 15{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 30 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Nyquist Interval:**

$$T_N = \frac{1}{f_N} = \frac{1}{30{,}000} = 33.33 \text{ \mu s}$$

**(iii) Minimum Sampling Frequency:**

$$f_s \geq f_N = 30 \text{ kHz}$$

So the minimum is **30 kHz**.

**(iv) Number of Samples in 1 Second (at $f_s = 30$ kHz):**

$$N = f_s \times t = 30{,}000 \times 1 = 30{,}000 \text{ samples}$$

**(v) Check for Aliasing at $f_s = 32$ kHz:**

Apply the condition: $f_s = 32$ kHz and $2 f_m = 30$ kHz.

Since $32 \text{ kHz} > 30 \text{ kHz}$, we have $f_s > 2f_m$. **Aliasing is avoided.** The signal is sampled correctly, with a small safety margin of 2 kHz.

*(Marks: 1 for each sub-part (i) to (v).)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of aliasing with a suitable diagram. How does the anti-aliasing filter solve this problem? (7 marks)

**(b)** A sinusoidal signal $x(t) = 5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1500 t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 2000$ Hz. Determine:
- (i) Whether aliasing occurs
- (ii) The apparent (alias) frequency
- (iii) The reconstructed signal as interpreted by the system
- (iv) The minimum sampling frequency to avoid aliasing
- (v) State two advantages of oversampling.

(7 marks)

---

### Solution to Question B-B

#### Part (a) — Aliasing and Anti-Aliasing Filter (7 marks)

**Aliasing Definition (2 marks):** When $f_s < 2f_m$, the high-frequency components of $x(t)$ "fold back" into the baseband region $[0, f_s/2]$ and appear as false low-frequency components. Once aliasing occurs, the original signal **cannot be recovered** by any reconstruction filter.

**Spectral Diagram (3 marks, drawn in exam):**
- Draw $X(f)$ centered at $0$, extending from $-f_m$ to $+f_m$.
- Draw replicas centered at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$
- Show that when $f_s < 2f_m$, the replicas centered at $0$ and at $\pm f_s$ **overlap** in the overlap region.
- Mark the overlap zone as "corrupted information."

**Anti-Aliasing Filter Solution (2 marks):** A sharp low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_m$ is inserted *before* the sampler. This forces the input signal to be band-limited to $f_m$, guaranteeing that $f_s \geq 2f_m$ can be satisfied. The filter's stopband must attenuate all frequencies above $f_s - f_m$ by at least the resolution of the ADC (e.g., 60–80 dB for a 12-bit system).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Aliasing (7 marks)

**Given:** $A = 5$ V, $f_m = 1500$ Hz, $f_s = 2000$ Hz.

**(i) Check for Aliasing:**

$2 f_m = 2 \times 1500 = 3000 \text{ Hz}$. Since $f_s = 2000 \text{ Hz} < 3000 \text{ Hz}$, **aliasing will occur**.

**(ii) Apparent (Alias) Frequency:**

Using the formula $f_a = \vert f_m - k f_s \vert$ with $k = 1$:

$$f_a = \vert 1500 - 1 \times 2000 \vert = \vert -500 \vert = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Reconstructed Signal:**

The system will interpret the samples as a sinusoid of frequency 500 Hz:

$$x_{reconstructed}(t) = 5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 500 \cdot t) = 5 \sin(1000\pi t)$$

This is **not** the original signal. The 1500 Hz tone has been permanently misidentified as a 500 Hz tone.

**(iv) Minimum Sampling Frequency to Avoid Aliasing:**

$$f_{s,min} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 1500 = 3000 \text{ Hz}$$

**(v) Two Advantages of Oversampling ($f_s \gg 2f_m$):**

1. **Relaxed anti-aliasing filter requirements:** A higher $f_s$ means a wider transition band is acceptable, allowing the use of a simpler, lower-order analog LPF.
2. **Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Oversampling spreads the quantization noise over a wider frequency range, and subsequent digital filtering improves effective resolution.
3. *(Optional third)* Easier reconstruction with a smoother analog output.

*(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1.5 + 1 + 2.5 for the respective sub-parts.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places Students Lose Marks on Sampling Theorem Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing $f_s$ and $f_N$:** Students interchange the actual sampling frequency with the Nyquist Rate. Remember: $f_N = 2f_m$ is a *minimum*, not the actual $f_s$.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor:** Writing $f_s = 2f_m$ as if it is always the answer. In practice, $f_s$ is chosen *strictly greater* than $2f_m$.
> 3. **Wrong anti-aliasing filter placement:** Drawing the LPF *after* the sampler (or *after* the ADC). It must always be *before* the sampler.
> 4. **Incomplete derivation:** Stopping at $X_s(f) = f_s \sum X(f - kf_s)$ without explaining the non-overlap condition that allows recovery.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Mixing Hz and rad/s. Always convert $f_m$ to Hz when using $f_s = 2f_m$; use $\omega_s = 2\omega_m$ for rad/s.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Statement:** A band-limited signal with max frequency $f_m$ can be reconstructed from its samples if $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2f_m$ — the *minimum* permissible sampling frequency.
- **Nyquist Interval:** $T_N = 1/(2f_m)$ — the *maximum* allowable sampling period.
- **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_s < 2f_m$; high frequencies masquerade as low frequencies. **Information is permanently lost.**
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A **Low-Pass Filter** placed **before** the sampler with cutoff at $f_m$ to enforce the band-limited condition.
- **Spectral Effect of Sampling:** $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ — the original spectrum is replicated at every multiple of $f_s$.
- **Reconstruction:** Achieved by a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ in the range $[f_m, f_s - f_m]$.
- **Alias Frequency Formula:** $f_a = \vert f_m - k f_s \vert$ where $k$ is chosen so that $0 \leq f_a \leq f_s/2$.
- **Practical Oversampling:** Engineers use $f_s = 2.5 f_m$ to $10 f_m$ for safety against non-ideal filters.
- **Minimum Samples per Cycle:** At least **2** samples per cycle for a sinusoid (theoretical); 5–20 in practice.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Used in every ADC, PLC analog input module, vibration monitor, and digital audio system.
- **CD Audio Origin:** 44.1 kHz chosen because human hearing tops out at ~20 kHz (Nyquist rate = 40 kHz).
- **Irreducible Rule:** Filter → Sample → Process → Reconstruct. Never sample before filtering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Digitizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Digitizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of a **Data Acquisition System (DAS)**, the physical signals produced by sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, strain, etc.) are inherently **continuous in both time and amplitude** — i.e., they are *analog* signals. Since the digital computer, PLC CPU, or microcontroller can only process data expressed as discrete binary words, two fundamental transformations must be performed before the signal can be used:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling** is the operation of converting a *continuous-time* signal into a *discrete-time* signal by recording its instantaneous amplitude at uniformly spaced instants of time, governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digitizing (Quantization + Coding)** is the operation of converting the *continuous-amplitude* samples into a *finite set of discrete amplitude levels* and representing each level with a unique binary code, executed by an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.

Together, **Sampling and Digitizing** form the heart of every modern digital instrument and PLC analog input module. The complete pipeline is:

$$\text{Analog Signal} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Anti-Alias Filter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Sample \& Hold} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ADC (Quantize + Code)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Digital Word}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Movie Camera and the Staircase"

Imagine you are trying to teach a friend (who can only see still photographs) what a flowing river looks like:

- **Sampling** is like taking a *movie camera* and capturing a series of still frames at regular intervals (say, 30 frames per second). Each frame is a snapshot — the river still "flows" between the frames, but we choose specific moments to record. If we take too few frames per second, we lose the motion (this is **aliasing**); if we take enough frames, the brain fills in the gaps and the river appears continuous again.

- **Quantization** is like a child who is asked to draw the height of the water on a *ladder with only 8 rungs*. The water can be at any height, but the child can only mark it at one of the 8 fixed levels. The more rungs the ladder has (more bits), the more faithfully the actual water height is recorded.

- **Coding** is the child then writing down the rung number (0–7) in *binary* so it can be stored compactly (000, 001, 010 … 111).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** Every analog input module in a PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-1200 AI4, Allen-Bradley 1769-IF8) contains these two stages *inside the ADC chip*. Understanding them is essential for selecting the correct module resolution for a given process application.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the input signal.
- **Standard Sampling Frequencies:** $48 \text{ kHz}$, $44.1 \text{ kHz}$ (audio CD); $8 \text{ kHz}$, $16 \text{ kHz}$ (telephony & speech).
- **Standard ADC Resolutions:** 8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit, 24-bit.
- **Quantization Step (LSB):** Defined as $\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$ where $n$ is the number of bits.
- **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio:** $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ (the famous "6 dB per bit" rule).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sampling Process — Step-by-Step Theory

The sampling operation is mathematically equivalent to multiplying the continuous signal $x(t)$ by a periodic train of unit impulses (the *Dirac comb*) $p(t)$, yielding the discrete-time signal $x_s(t)$.

- **Step 1 — Continuous Input Arrival:** A real-world analog signal $x(t)$ arrives at the ADC input pin. It contains a band-limited spectrum $X(f)$ between $-f_{max}$ and $+f_{max}$ Hz.

- **Step 2 — Band-Limiting:** To apply the sampling theorem safely, the signal is first passed through an **Anti-Aliasing Filter** (a low-pass analog filter with cut-off $f_c \le f_s/2$). This guarantees the input is truly band-limited.

- **Step 3 — Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** The S/H circuit charges a precision hold capacitor to the instantaneous value of the input signal during the *acquisition phase*, and then disconnects it (or buffers it) during the *hold phase*. This prevents the input from changing during the finite conversion time of the ADC.

- **Step 4 — Periodic Sampling:** The held voltage is sampled at instants $t = nT_s$, where $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period and $f_s$ is the sampling frequency.

- **Step 5 — Nyquist Theorem Check:** The sampling theorem guarantees that if $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$, the original signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples using an ideal low-pass reconstruction filter.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- *Why band-limit?* Because if any frequency component above $f_s/2$ exists, it will "fold back" into the baseband and corrupt the data — a phenomenon called **aliasing**.
- *Why S/H?* Because a successive-approximation ADC takes $n$ clock cycles to determine $n$ bits; without a hold stage, the voltage would change during conversion, producing an erroneous reading.
- *Why Nyquist?* Because a band-limited signal has only $2 f_{max} T$ degrees of freedom per second of observation; sampling faster wastes memory; sampling slower loses information permanently.

## 2.2 The Digitizing Process — Step-by-Step Theory

Once a stable sample is held on the capacitor, the ADC performs **quantization** and **coding**:

- **Step 1 — Range Mapping:** The full-scale voltage range $V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ is divided into $L$ discrete intervals called **quantization levels**, where $L = 2^n$.

- **Step 2 — Level Assignment:** Each held sample $v_{in}$ is rounded to the *nearest* quantization level — this introduces the **quantization error** $e_q$.

- **Step 3 — Binary Coding:** The chosen level index (0 to $L-1$) is encoded as an $n$-bit binary word (usually **Natural Binary**, **Offset Binary**, or **Two's Complement**).

- **Step 4 — Output Transfer:** The binary word is latched onto the data bus for the CPU, PLC, or DSP to read.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation, condition, and unit you will need to solve any KTU problem on this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Syntax:** In the formulas below, the absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` instead of the plain pipe character `|` to keep the markdown table structure intact.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Condition | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \;\ge\; 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz (samples/sec) | Minimum rate to avoid aliasing |
| 2 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_{Nyquist} = f_s / 2$ | Hz | Highest uniquely representable freq. |
| 3 | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | seconds | Time between successive samples |
| 4 | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless | $n$ = number of ADC bits |
| 5 | Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$ | Volts / bit | Resolution per code change |
| 6 | Resolution (% of FS) | $R \% = \Delta / V_{FS} \cdot 100 = 100 / (2^n - 1)$ | percent | For 12-bit ADC $\approx 0.0244\%$ |
| 7 | Maximum Quantization Error | $\vert e_q \vert \le \Delta / 2$ | Volts | Also called $\pm \tfrac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$ |
| 8 | RMS Quantization Noise | $e_{rms} = \Delta / \sqrt{12}$ | Volts | Uniform distribution assumption |
| 9 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB | For full-scale sinusoidal input |
| 10 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) \approx 6.02n$ | dB | Ratio of largest to smallest step |
| 11 | Reconstruction Filter BW | $BW = f_s / 2$ | Hz | Ideal low-pass at output |
| 12 | Total Acquisition Time | $T_{acq} = T_{acq\_S/H} + T_{conv}$ | seconds | Determines max input bandwidth |
| 13 | Throughput Rate | $f_{throughput} = 1 / T_{acq}$ | samples/sec | Maximum sample rate of the ADC |

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial PLCs (e.g., Siemens, Allen-Bradley):** A 12-bit or 16-bit ADC module is used for process variables. The formula $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ lets the engineer predict measurement precision in dB.
- **Audio Engineering (CD / MP3):** 16-bit audio gives $\text{SQNR} \approx 98$ dB, suitable for music.
- **Precision Weigh Scales:** 24-bit $\Sigma$-$\Delta$ ADCs yield $\text{SQNR} \approx 146$ dB, enabling mg-level resolution.
- **Vibration Monitoring:** Sampling at $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ ensures rotating-machinery fault frequencies (e.g., 1×, 2× RPM, blade-pass) are captured without aliasing.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the *complete* mathematical justification for the two most exam-relevant results in this module: the **Nyquist Theorem** and the **SQNR formula**.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sampling Theorem (Aliasing Condition)

**Given:** A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ whose spectrum $X(f)$ is non-zero only for $-f_{max} \le f \le f_{max}$.

**Sampling operation:** The sampled signal is the product of $x(t)$ with an impulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s$.

$$
p(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - k T_s)
$$

$$
x_s(t) \;=\; x(t) \cdot p(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k T_s)\, \delta(t - k T_s)
$$

**Take the Fourier Transform.** Multiplication in time-domain corresponds to convolution in the frequency-domain. The Fourier transform of an impulse train is itself an impulse train in frequency, scaled by $1/T_s$:

$$
P(f) \;=\; \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(f - \frac{n}{T_s}\right)
$$

$$
X_s(f) \;=\; X(f) * P(f) \;=\; \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(f - \frac{n}{T_s}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** The original spectrum $X(f)$ is replicated at every multiple of the sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$.

**Aliasing Condition:** For the spectral replicas to *not overlap*, the distance between replicas ($f_s$) must be at least twice the width of $X(f)$ ($2 f_{max}$).

$$
f_s \;\ge\; 2\, f_{max}
$$

**Equivalently, the Nyquist frequency** is the half-way point between two replicas:

$$
f_{Nyquist} \;=\; \frac{f_s}{2} \;\ge\; f_{max}
$$

If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the replicas overlap, and reconstruction via low-pass filtering becomes impossible — the high frequencies *appear* as lower frequencies. This is aliasing.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

**Given:** An $n$-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$, quantizer step $\Delta = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$, and a full-scale sinusoidal input $v(t) = (V_{FS}/2) \sin(2\pi f t)$.

### Step A — Quantization Error as a Uniform Random Variable

Assume the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed between $-\Delta/2$ and $+\Delta/2$. This is the standard "benign" assumption valid for high-resolution ADCs and input signals that cross many LSBs.

The probability density function is therefore:

$$
p(e_q) \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for} \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e_q \le +\frac{\Delta}{2}
$$

### Step B — Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power

The average (mean-square) noise power is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{e_q^2} \;=\; \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, p(e_q) \, d e_q
\;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, d e_q
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, d e_q
\;=\; \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
\;=\; \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3}
\;=\; \frac{2 \Delta^3}{24}
\;=\; \frac{\Delta^3}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

Hence the **RMS quantization noise** is:

$$
e_{rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\overline{e_q^2}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

### Step C — Signal Power of the Full-Scale Sinewave

For a sinusoid of amplitude $A = V_{FS}/2$, the mean-square (i.e., power into a 1-ohm reference) is:

$$
P_{signal} \;=\; \frac{A^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}
$$

### Step D — Form the SQNR Ratio

$$
\text{SQNR} \;=\; \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} \;=\; \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}^2}{\Delta^2} \;=\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{FS}}{\Delta}\right)^2
$$

Substitute $\Delta = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1) \approx V_{FS}/2^n$ for large $n$:

$$
\text{SQNR} \;\approx\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{FS}}{V_{FS}/2^n}\right)^2 \;=\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot (2^n)^2 \;=\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}
$$

Take the base-10 logarithm and convert to decibels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;&=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n}\right)
\;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2^{2n}\right)
\\
&=\; 1.76 + 20 n \log_{10}(2)
\;=\; 1.76 + 20 n \cdot 0.30103
\\
&=\; 1.76 + 6.0206 \, n
\;\approx\; 6.02 n + 1.76
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **final universally-quoted result**:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02\,n \;+\; 1.76\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** For a 16-bit audio ADC, $\text{SQNR} = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB. This matches the theoretical dynamic range of a CD-quality recording.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize a 0–10 V analog signal. Compute (a) quantization step, (b) maximum quantization error, (c) SQNR in dB, (d) resolution as a percentage.

**Solution:**

$$
n = 12,\quad V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V}
$$

(a) Quantization step:

$$
\Delta \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} \;=\; \frac{10}{4095} \;=\; 2.442 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

(b) Maximum quantization error:

$$
\vert e_q \vert_{max} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{2} \;\approx\; 1.221 \text{ mV}
$$

(c) SQNR:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02(12) + 1.76 \;=\; 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

(d) Resolution (%):

$$
R \;=\; \frac{100}{2^n - 1} \;=\; \frac{100}{4095} \;\approx\; 0.0244\%
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. State the condition that must be satisfied to avoid aliasing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited analog signal containing no frequency components higher than $f_{max}$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ is at least twice the highest frequency present in the signal.

> **Condition (1 mark):** $f_s \;\ge\; 2\, f_{max}$ — this minimum rate is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

> **Aliasing (1 mark):** If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the spectral replicas of the signal overlap and information is irretrievably lost; higher frequencies appear as false lower frequencies (*aliases*). An **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** must precede the sampler.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define "Quantization Error" and "Resolution" of an ADC. Give the formula relating resolution in percent to the number of bits $n$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Quantization Error (1.5 marks):** Quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the true analog sample value and the nearest discrete quantization level produced by the ADC. Its maximum magnitude is $\pm \Delta / 2$, where $\Delta$ is the size of one LSB.

> **Resolution (1 mark):** Resolution is the smallest change in the analog input that can be detected by the ADC. It is equal to one LSB: $\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$.

> **Percentage Resolution (0.5 marks):**

$$
R \% \;=\; \frac{100}{2^n - 1}
$$

For $n = 12$, $R \approx 0.0244\%$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ➤ Question A: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Sampling Theorem with suitable mathematical justification. Discuss the phenomenon of **aliasing** and the role of an anti-aliasing filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Mention its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Statement of the Theorem (1 mark):**
A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its discrete-time samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$.

**2. Mathematical Justification (3 marks):**
Sampling is mathematically modelled as multiplication by an impulse train:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{k} \delta(t - kT_s)
$$

Taking the Fourier Transform, using the convolution property:

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} X\!\left(f - n f_s\right)
$$

The baseband spectrum is replicated at every multiple of $f_s$. If $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$, the replicas do not overlap and the original signal is recovered via an ideal low-pass filter.

**3. Aliasing & Anti-Aliasing Filter (3 marks):**
Aliasing occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{max}$; spectral replicas overlap and high-frequency components appear shifted into the baseband (e.g., a 7 kHz tone sampled at 8 kHz appears as 1 kHz). Because once aliased data is corrupted irreversibly, prevention is essential. An **anti-aliasing filter** (analog low-pass, Butterworth or Bessel) with cut-off $f_c = f_s / 2$ is placed *before* the sampler. A practical filter requires a *transition band*; hence engineers over-sample at $f_s = 3$ to $5$ times $f_{max}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
                ┌─────────────────┐
  Analog ──────►│ Sample & Hold   │──────┐
  Input         └─────────────────┘      │
                                          ▼
                          ┌──────────────────────┐
                          │  SAR Register (n-bit) │
                          └──────┬───────────────┘
                                  │ MSB → D/A → Comparator ← Vin
                                  ▼
                          ┌──────────────────────┐
                          │ Digital Output Word  │
                          └──────────────────────┘
```

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- The SAR register initially sets the MSB to 1 and all other bits to 0.
- This digital code is fed to an internal DAC, whose analog output is compared with the held input voltage by a comparator.
- If DAC output > $V_{in}$, the MSB is reset to 0; if DAC output $\le V_{in}$, the MSB remains 1.
- The procedure is repeated for each successive bit until the LSB is decided.
- After $n$ clock cycles, the $n$-bit word in the SAR register is the digitized value.

**3. Advantages & Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Advantages:** High resolution (8–18 bit), moderate speed (up to ~1 MSPS), low power, deterministic conversion time $\to$ ideal for PLC analog modules.
- **Limitations:** Requires a precision DAC internally; throughput decreases with resolution; not suited for very high-speed (>10 MSPS) or very high-resolution (>20-bit) applications.

---

### ➤ Question B: `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** What is **quantization**? Derive the expression for **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** of an $n$-bit ADC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** with a neat circuit diagram. Why is it necessary in a data acquisition system? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Definition of Quantization (1 mark):**
Quantization is the process of mapping the continuous-amplitude sampled value to one of a finite set of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, introducing an irreducible **quantization error** of magnitude $\pm \Delta/2$.

**2. Step Size (0.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}
$$

**3. Assumption for Noise (0.5 marks):** Quantization error is modelled as a uniformly distributed random variable in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.

**4. Noise Power Derivation (2.5 marks):**

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, d e_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**5. Signal Power (1 mark):**
For a full-scale sinusoid of amplitude $V_{FS}/2$:

$$
P_{signal} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}
$$

**6. SQNR Result (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**1. Necessity (2 marks):**
- The ADC requires a **finite conversion time** ($T_{conv}$) during which the analog input must remain stable.
- Without a hold stage, the input may change by more than 1 LSB during conversion, producing erroneous codes.
- For a 12-bit ADC with $f_{in} = 1$ kHz and $T_{conv} = 1 \mu s$, the voltage drift can exceed $\Delta$ within one conversion cycle.

**2. Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**

```
  Vin ──┤►─────┬───────────────┬───► to ADC
          ▲    │                │
         [Buffer]           [Op-Amp]
          Op-Amp             Follower
            │                  ▲
            ▼                  │
         [Switch]          [Hold
          MOSFET           Capacitor CH]
            ▲                  │
            │                  │
        [Control          [GND
         Logic]          Reference]
```

Components: An input buffer (unity-gain op-amp), an electronic switch (FET or MOSFET), a precision hold capacitor $C_H$ (often Teflon or polypropylene for low leakage), and an output buffer (high-input-impedance op-amp).

**3. Two Phases (2 marks):**

- **Acquisition / Tracking Phase:** The switch is closed, $C_H$ charges to track $V_{in}$. Duration $T_{acq}$ depends on the RC time constant; the ADC waits $T_{acq} \ge \tau \cdot \ln(2^n)$ before starting conversion.
- **Hold Phase:** The switch opens, $C_H$ retains the captured voltage. The output buffer provides a low-impedance copy to the ADC. The voltage droops slowly due to capacitor leakage.

**4. Key Specifications (1 mark):**
- **Acquisition Time** ($T_{acq}$): time to settle within $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- **Aperture Time** ($t_{ap}$): the (small) delay between the hold command and the actual switch opening.
- **Aperture Jitter** ($\Delta t_{ap}$): uncertainty in aperture time; directly limits maximum input frequency for high-resolution ADCs.
- **Droop Rate** ($dV/dt$): voltage drop per second during hold.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Nyquist Rate" with "Nyquist Frequency":** The *Nyquist Rate* is the *minimum sampling frequency* ($2 f_{max}$), while the *Nyquist Frequency* is the *maximum uniquely-representable frequency* in the baseband ($f_s / 2$). Many students interchange the two — this loses 1 full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter:** Any answer that discusses sampling without mentioning the anti-aliasing filter is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 3. **Incorrect quantization step formula:** Some students write $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^n$ instead of the correct $V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$. For low $n$ (e.g., 4-bit), the difference is significant (≈6%); examiners explicitly deduct for this.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in SQNR:** Always state the answer in **dB** explicitly; writing "98" without the unit is penalized.
> 5. **Skipping the "why S/H is necessary" argument:** When asked about S/H, do not merely describe the circuit — always explain *why* it is mandatory (finite conversion time vs. changing input).
