> 6. **Confusing "natural sampling" vs. "flat-top sampling":** In flat-top sampling (used by most S/H circuits), the sample is held constant for the entire sampling interval; in natural (gated) sampling, the sample follows the input for a brief pulse. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Sampling** = time-discretization; **Quantization** = amplitude-discretization; **Coding** = binary representation.
- The **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** mandates $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$; $f_{Nyquist} = f_s/2$.
- **Aliasing** is irreversible — it must be *prevented* by an analog low-pass anti-aliasing filter placed *before* the sampler.
- The **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit captures and freezes the analog value during the ADC's finite conversion window. Its key specs are acquisition time, aperture time, aperture jitter, and droop rate.
- **Number of quantization levels:** $L = 2^n$.
- **Quantization step (LSB):** $\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$.
- **Maximum quantization error:** $\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \Delta / 2 = \pm \tfrac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- **Resolution in percent:** $R \% = 100 / (2^n - 1)$.
- **RMS quantization noise:** $e_{rms} = \Delta / \sqrt{12}$ (uniform distribution assumption).
- **SQNR (dB):** $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ — the famous **"6 dB per bit"** rule, plus a 1.76 dB constant.
- **Dynamic Range:** $\text{DR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) \approx 6.02 n$ dB.
- **ADC Architectures to remember:** Flash (fast, low resolution), SAR (balanced, common in PLCs), Dual-Slope (slow, excellent noise rejection for DMMs), Sigma-Delta (high resolution, audio & precision).
- **Coding schemes:** Natural Binary, Offset Binary, Two's Complement (most common for bipolar signals in PLCs).
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Always over-sample by a factor of **3 to 5** in real systems so that the anti-aliasing filter has a practical (non-ideal) transition band.
- **Quick mental calculation:** For an $n$-bit ADC, the resolution is approximately $V_{FS}$ in $mV$ divided by $1000$ for $n=10$, by $4000$ for $n=12$, by $16000$ for $n=14$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aliasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aliasing** is a phenomenon in signal processing and data acquisition where a continuous-time analog signal, when sampled at an insufficient sampling rate, produces a reconstructed discrete-time signal that appears to be a *different* signal than the original. High-frequency components of the original signal "masquerade" (or take on a false identity) as lower-frequency components in the sampled version, causing irreversible loss of information.

Mathematically, aliasing occurs when the sampling frequency $f_s$ is less than twice the highest frequency component $f_{max}$ present in the input signal. This fundamental condition is governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> Aliasing is the effect that causes different continuous signals to become indistinguishable (aliases of one another) when sampled. It occurs when the sampling rate is below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2 f_{max}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Keywords for KTU Board Exams:**
> *Aliasing, Nyquist Rate, Nyquist Frequency, Sampling Theorem, Anti-Aliasing Filter, Folded Frequency, Spectral Overlap, Signal Reconstruction*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Wheel Effect

Imagine a **wagon wheel spinning in a Western movie**:

1. When the wheel rotates slowly (say, 10 rotations per second), your eyes and a standard film camera (sampling at 24 frames per second) correctly perceive the forward rotation.
2. As the wheel speeds up to about 12 rotations/second, the wheel appears to **slow down** and eventually **stop**.
3. If the wheel rotates even faster (say, 15 rotations/second), it appears to **rotate backwards**.

Why? Because the camera samples (captures frames) at a fixed rate. When the wheel's rotation frequency exceeds half the camera's frame rate, the camera cannot capture the wheel's true motion, and **the high-frequency rotation is misrepresented as a low-frequency (or reverse) rotation**.

This is **exactly what aliasing does to an analog signal in a Data Acquisition System (DAS)**: the ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) acts as the "camera," sampling the input signal at discrete time intervals. If the input signal changes too rapidly between samples, the ADC records false, lower-frequency information.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Manifestation:**
> In a temperature control PLC system, if a temperature sensor outputs a signal with rapid fluctuations (e.g., due to electrical noise at 5 kHz) but the PLC's analog input module samples at only 1 kHz, the noise will appear as a slow, spurious temperature drift — leading to incorrect PID control actions.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

The following terms form the **core vocabulary** examiners expect:

| Term | Symbol | Standard Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | Number of samples per second (**Hz** or **samples/second**) |
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | Time between consecutive samples, $T_s = 1/f_s$ (**seconds**) |
| Maximum Signal Frequency | $f_{max}$ | Highest frequency component in the input signal (**Hz**) |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_N$ | $f_N = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (**Hz**) — *minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing* |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_N/2$ | Half the sampling rate, $f_s/2$ (**Hz**) — *highest uniquely representable frequency* |
| Alias Frequency | $f_a$ | The false frequency that appears after sampling (**Hz**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The terms **Nyquist Rate** and **Nyquist Frequency** are often confused. Remember:
> - **Nyquist Rate** = $2 \cdot f_{max}$ (a *required sampling rate*).
> - **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_s / 2$ (a *frequency limit* imposed by the sampling rate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem — Operational Breakdown

The theorem has **two complementary statements** that KTU examiners frequently test:

### Statement 1 (Forward Direction — Reconstruction Guarantee):
> A band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ containing **no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz** can be **uniquely and perfectly reconstructed** from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies:
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

### Statement 2 (Converse — When Information is Lost):
> If $f_s < 2 \cdot f_{max}$, then the frequency components above $f_s/2$ will "fold back" into the baseband (0 to $f_s/2$ range), producing **irreversible aliasing distortion**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Why Aliasing Occurs

1. **Step 1 — Spectral Replication:** When a continuous signal $x(t)$ is impulse-sampled, its spectrum $X(f)$ is replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$. Mathematically:
$$X_s(f) = f_s \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

2. **Step 2 — Non-Overlapping Condition:** For perfect reconstruction, these spectral replicas must **not overlap**. This requires:
$$f_{max} \leq \frac{f_s}{2}$$

3. **Step 3 — Overlap Catastrophe:** If $f_{max} > f_s/2$, adjacent replicas overlap. The overlapping regions add together, producing **distorted spectral content** that cannot be separated by any filter.

4. **Step 4 — False Frequency Emerges:** A true input frequency $f_{in} > f_s/2$ reappears in the reconstructed signal as a **lower alias frequency**:
$$f_{alias} = \left\vert f_{in} - n \cdot f_s \right\vert$$
where $n$ is the nearest integer such that $f_{alias} \leq f_s/2$.

5. **Step 5 — Loss of Original Information:** Because the spectral overlap is additive, the **original high-frequency content is permanently lost** — there is no DSP algorithm that can recover it post-sampling.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist Sampling Criterion | **Hz** |
| 2 | $f_N = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist Rate (minimum sampling rate) | **Hz** |
| 3 | $f_{Nyquist} = f_s / 2$ | Nyquist Frequency (folding frequency) | **Hz** |
| 4 | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | Sampling Period | **seconds** |
| 5 | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - n \cdot f_s \vert$ | Alias Frequency Computation | **Hz** |
| 6 | $n = \text{round}(f_{in} / f_s)$ | Integer multiplier for alias calculation | *dimensionless* |
| 7 | $\Delta t = T_s / N$ | Oversampling factor relation | **seconds** |
| 8 | $f_{cutoff} = f_s / 2$ | Anti-aliasing filter cutoff frequency | **Hz** |
| 9 | $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02N + 1.76$ dB | ADC Quantization SNR (N-bit ADC) | **dB** |
| 10 | $f_{sig,max} = f_s / (2 \cdot OSR)$ | Max signal freq with oversampling ratio OSR | **Hz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall Warning:** In markdown tables, absolute value bars `|x|` **will break the table syntax**. KTU examiners always write $\vert x \vert$ or use the inline notation $\mid x \mid$ when rendered in LaTeX.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Aliasing Control |
|---|---|
| **PLC Analog Input Modules** | Anti-aliasing filters precede ADCs to band-limit sensor signals |
| **Audio CD (44.1 kHz sampling)** | Designed for human hearing limit of ~20 kHz; $f_N = 40$ kHz |
| **Vibration Monitoring** | Machine vibration frequencies must be sampled at $f_s > 2f_{vib,max}$ |
| **Medical ECG/EEG** | Biological signals have low $f_{max}$, but noise aliasing must be prevented |
| **Oversampling ADCs (Sigma-Delta)** | Sample at very high $f_s$ to relax anti-aliasing filter requirements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **PLC-based Data Acquisition System**, the analog input module typically contains:
> 1. A **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** (analog, hardware).
> 2. A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit**.
> 3. The **ADC** itself.
> 
> The anti-aliasing filter is *always* placed **before** the S/H circuit because once aliasing occurs in the sampled data, no software can fix it.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Nyquist Criterion from Spectral Analysis

We start with a band-limited signal $x(t)$ whose Fourier Transform $X(f)$ satisfies:

$$X(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert > f_{max}$$

When this signal is sampled by an ideal impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$, the sampled signal in the frequency domain is the convolution of $X(f)$ with the spectrum of the impulse train. The impulse train has Fourier series representation:

$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

Taking the Fourier Transform of $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

This shows that the spectrum $X(f)$ is **replicated at every multiple of $f_s$**.

For **non-overlapping replicas**, the right edge of the baseband spectrum at $+f_{max}$ must not touch the left edge of the next replica at $f_s - f_{max}$:

$$f_{max} \leq f_s - f_{max}$$

Solving for $f_s$:

$$2 f_{max} \leq f_s$$

$$\boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_{max}}$$

This is the **Nyquist Criterion**. The minimum sampling rate $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Alias Frequency Calculation

Suppose a signal contains an unknown frequency component $f_{in}$, but the ADC samples at $f_s$. We need to find the apparent (alias) frequency $f_a$.

**Step 1:** Identify the integer $n$ such that the alias frequency falls in the baseband $[0, f_s/2]$:

$$n = \text{round}\left(\frac{f_{in}}{f_s}\right)$$

This selects the spectral replica nearest to $f_{in}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the distance from $f_{in}$ to the nearest multiple of $f_s$:

$$f_a = \left\vert f_{in} - n \cdot f_s \right\vert$$

**Step 3:** If $f_a > f_s/2$, take the mirror value (Nyquist symmetry):

$$f_a = f_s - \left\vert f_{in} - n \cdot f_s \right\vert$$

This $f_a$ is the false frequency that will appear after sampling.

---

### Worked Example (KTU Board Style):

**Problem:** An analog signal of frequency **7.5 kHz** is sampled at **6 kHz**. Determine the alias frequency.

**Solution:**

$$f_{in} = 7.5 \text{ kHz}, \quad f_s = 6 \text{ kHz}$$

Compute $n$:

$$n = \text{round}\left(\frac{7.5}{6}\right) = \text{round}(1.25) = 1$$

Compute the alias frequency:

$$f_a = \vert 7.5 - (1)(6) \vert = \vert 7.5 - 6 \vert = 1.5 \text{ kHz}$$

Since $f_a = 1.5$ kHz $\leq f_s/2 = 3$ kHz, the alias is valid.

$$\boxed{f_{alias} = 1.5 \text{ kHz}}$$

The 7.5 kHz signal will appear to the ADC as a **1.5 kHz signal** — a complete misrepresentation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff Frequency

To prevent aliasing, the input signal must be band-limited to $f_{max} \leq f_s/2$ **before sampling**. This is achieved by an analog low-pass filter called an **anti-aliasing filter**.

The filter's **-3 dB cutoff frequency** must satisfy:

$$f_c \leq \frac{f_s}{2}$$

In practice, to allow for the filter's non-ideal roll-off (finite slope), a **guard band** is used:

$$f_c = \frac{f_s}{2} - \Delta f_{guard}$$

where $\Delta f_{guard}$ depends on the filter order:
- **1st-order RC filter:** $\Delta f_{guard} \approx 20\%$ of $f_c$
- **2nd-order Butterworth:** $\Delta f_{guard} \approx 5\%$ of $f_c$
- **Higher-order (≥4th):** $\Delta f_{guard} \approx 1\%$ of $f_c$

For a **Butterworth filter** of order $n$, the magnitude response is:

$$\vert H(f) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^{2n}}}$$

Setting $\vert H(f_c) \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ gives the $-3$ dB point. At $f = f_s/2$, the attenuation achieved is:

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{f_s/2}{f_c}\right)^{2n}\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For a 2nd-order Butterworth anti-aliasing filter, choose $f_c \approx 0.45 \cdot f_s$ to ensure at least **12 dB attenuation** at $f_s/2$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cases of Sampling

| Case | Condition | Result | Practical Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oversampling** | $f_s > 2 f_{max}$ | No aliasing; perfect reconstruction possible | Used in CD audio (44.1 kHz for 20 kHz audio) |
| **Critical Sampling** | $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ | Theoretically no aliasing, but no margin | Impractical due to filter limitations |
| **Undersampling** | $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ | **Aliasing occurs**; information permanently lost | Must be avoided in PLC/DAS design |
| **Bandpass Sampling** | $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ but signal is bandpass | Possible to recover IF no spectral overlap | Used intentionally in software-defined radio |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive KTU Style)

**Problem:** A PLC's analog input module samples a vibration signal at $f_s = 500$ Hz. The signal is known to contain frequency components at 50 Hz, 120 Hz, 230 Hz, and 480 Hz.

**Solution — Identify Violations:**

$$f_N = f_s / 2 = 250 \text{ Hz}$$

| Component | Frequency | Compared to $f_N$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_1$ | 50 Hz | $< 250$ Hz | ✅ Properly sampled |
| $f_2$ | 120 Hz | $< 250$ Hz | ✅ Properly sampled |
| $f_3$ | 230 Hz | $< 250$ Hz | ✅ Properly sampled (barely) |
| $f_4$ | 480 Hz | $> 250$ Hz | ❌ **Will alias** |

**Compute Alias for 480 Hz:**

$$n = \text{round}(480/500) = 1$$

$$f_{alias} = \vert 480 - 500 \vert = 20 \text{ Hz}$$

**Conclusion:** The 480 Hz vibration component will appear as a **20 Hz signal** in the PLC's sampled data, completely misrepresenting the true machine condition. This could cause the PLC to miss a critical vibration fault.

**Remedy:** Install an anti-aliasing low-pass filter with $f_c = 250$ Hz **before** the ADC, or increase $f_s$ to at least 1000 Hz.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Aliasing in a Data Acquisition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal x of t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold Circuit S H]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP or PLC CPU]
    E --> F[Reconstructed Signal y of t]

    G[Clock Generator f sub s] --> C
    G --> D

    H[Violation: f sub s less than 2 f sub max] -.->|CAUSES| I[Aliasing Distortion]
    I -.->|CORRUPTS| F
```

**Explanation of Flow:**

1. **Analog Input Signal `x(t)`** — the continuous-time physical signal from a sensor (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration).
2. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** — removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$ to prevent spectral overlap.
3. **Sample-and-Hold Circuit (S/H)** — captures and holds the analog voltage level during ADC conversion, driven by clock $f_s$.
4. **ADC** — quantizes the held voltage into a binary code.
5. **DSP / PLC CPU** — processes the digital samples.
6. **Reconstructed Signal `y(t)`** — ideally equals `x(t)` if all conditions are satisfied.

The **dashed red path** represents the consequence when the Nyquist criterion is violated — aliasing distortion corrupts the output irreversibly.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Spectral Replication and Aliasing Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Original Spectrum X of f: band-limited to plus or minus f sub max] --> S2[Sampling: f sub s greater than 2 f sub max]
    S2 --> S3[Replicated Spectra: X of f, X of f minus f sub s, X of f plus f sub s]
    S3 --> S4[Non-overlapping replicas: Reconstruction SUCCESSFUL]

    S5[Original Spectrum X of f: band-limited to plus or minus f sub max] --> S6[Sampling: f sub s less than 2 f sub max]
    S6 --> S7[Replicated Spectra overlap in baseband]
    S7 --> S8[Alias frequencies appear as folded back components]
    S8 --> S9[Reconstruction FAILS: Irreversible information loss]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Signal Flow with Anti-Aliasing Filter Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Analog_Front_End[ANALOG FRONT END - DAS]
        S[Sensor: Temperature, Pressure, Vibration] --> AAF[Anti-Aliasing Filter: f sub c = f sub s by 2]
        AAF --> SH[Sample and Hold S H Circuit]
    end

    subgraph Conversion_Stage[DIGITAL CONVERSION STAGE]
        SH --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
        CLK[Clock Oscillator at f sub s] --> SH
        CLK --> ADC
    end

    subgraph Processing_Stage[DIGITAL PROCESSING - PLC]
        ADC --> MEM[Memory Buffer FIFO]
        MEM --> CPU[PLC CPU: Scan Cycle Processing]
        CPU --> OUT[Control Output: Relay, PWM, Analog]
    end

    subgraph Feedback_Loop[CLOSED-LOOP CONTROL]
        OUT --> ACT[Actuator: Motor, Valve, Heater]
        ACT -.->|Affects| S
    end
```

**Critical Engineering Insight:** The anti-aliasing filter **must reside in the analog domain** (between the sensor and the S/H circuit). Once the signal is sampled, it is too late to remove aliasing components through digital filtering alone — because the high-frequency information has *already folded into* the baseband.

---

## 4.4 Frequency Domain Visualization (Conceptual Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Time Domain: x of t = sin 2 pi 100 t + sin 2 pi 700 t] --> B{Is f sub s greater than or equal to 2 times f sub max?}
    B -- YES: f sub s = 2000 Hz --> C[Both 100 Hz and 700 Hz are below f sub s by 2 = 1000 Hz: NO ALIASING]
    B -- NO: f sub s = 800 Hz --> D[700 Hz exceeds f sub s by 2 = 400 Hz: ALIASING OCCURS]
    D --> E[700 Hz folds back to: f sub alias = vert 700 - 1 times 800 vert = 100 Hz]
    E --> F[Sampled signal appears as: 100 Hz + 100 Hz = Constructive Interference Distorted Output]
```

This diagram emphasizes the **catastrophic consequence**: two completely different input signals (one containing only a 100 Hz tone, one containing 100 Hz + 700 Hz) can produce **identical sampled data** if $f_s = 800$ Hz. This is the fundamental ambiguity that defines aliasing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define aliasing. State the Nyquist sampling theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which a high-frequency component of a continuous-time analog signal, when sampled at a rate below the Nyquist rate, appears in the reconstructed discrete-time signal as a false, lower-frequency component. This causes **irreversible loss of original signal information** because different input signals can produce identical sampled outputs.

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem (1 mark):** A band-limited signal containing frequencies up to $f_{max}$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$
The minimum sampling rate $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $f_s/2$ is called the **Nyquist Frequency**.

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is an anti-aliasing filter? Why must it be placed before the ADC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Anti-Aliasing Filter (2 marks):** An anti-aliasing filter is a **low-pass analog filter** placed at the input of a data acquisition system. It band-limits the input signal so that all frequency components above $f_s/2$ (the Nyquist Frequency) are attenuated to negligible levels, thereby preventing spectral overlap during sampling.

**Placement before ADC (1 mark):** It must be placed **before** the Sample-and-Hold and ADC blocks because aliasing occurs **at the moment of sampling**. Once a high-frequency component aliases into the baseband, no digital filter can distinguish it from a legitimate low-frequency signal — the information is permanently lost.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**Q(a) [7 marks]:** Explain the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem in detail. Derive the condition $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ using spectral replication analysis.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem is the fundamental principle governing the conversion of continuous-time signals to discrete-time signals. It establishes the **minimum sampling rate** required to preserve all information in a band-limited signal.

**Spectral Replication (3 marks):**
When a band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ is sampled by an impulse train of period $T_s$, its spectrum becomes:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

This equation shows that the spectrum $X(f)$ is **replicated at every integer multiple of $f_s$**.

**Derivation of Nyquist Criterion (2 marks):**
For non-overlapping replicas:

$$f_{max} \leq f_s - f_{max}$$

$$\boxed{2 f_{max} \leq f_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_s \geq 2 f_{max}}$$

---

**Q(b) [7 marks]:** A vibration sensor outputs a signal containing frequency components at 60 Hz, 200 Hz, and 850 Hz. The PLC's analog module samples at 1000 Hz. Determine which components will alias and calculate their alias frequencies. Suggest a remedy.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify Nyquist Frequency:

$$f_N = f_s / 2 = 1000 / 2 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Check each component:

| Component | Frequency | Status vs $f_N = 500$ Hz |
|---|---|---|
| 60 Hz | Below $f_N$ | ✅ Safe |
| 200 Hz | Below $f_N$ | ✅ Safe |
| 850 Hz | **Above $f_N$** | ❌ **Will alias** |

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute alias for 850 Hz:

$$n = \text{round}(850/1000) = 1$$

$$f_{alias} = \vert 850 - 1 \times 1000 \vert = \vert -150 \vert = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Remedy:
- **Option 1:** Install a 4th-order Butterworth low-pass filter with $f_c = 500$ Hz before the ADC.
- **Option 2:** Increase sampling rate to $f_s \geq 2 \times 850 = 1700$ Hz (minimum).
- **Recommended:** Combine both — use $f_s = 2000$ Hz with $f_c = 850$ Hz filter.

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**Q(a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the role of an anti-aliasing filter in a PLC-based Data Acquisition System. Discuss why digital filtering cannot replace an analog anti-aliasing filter.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Explanation (3 marks):**
The standard DAS chain is:
**Sensor → Anti-Aliasing Filter (Analog LPF) → Sample & Hold → ADC → PLC CPU**

The anti-aliasing filter is a **continuous-time low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$.

**Why Analog Filtering is Essential (4 marks):**

1. **Irreversibility of Aliasing:** Aliasing occurs **at the sampling instant**. Once a high-frequency component folds into the baseband, its energy is **indistinguishable** from a legitimate low-frequency component. Digital filters operate on already-sampled data and cannot recover this lost information.

2. **Hardware Timing Constraints:** The S/H circuit must capture the analog voltage in real-time. By the time the digital processor identifies an aliasing problem, multiple corrupted samples have already been taken.

3. **ADC Saturation:** Without an anti-aliasing filter, high-frequency noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz mains hum harmonics) can saturate the ADC's input range, reducing effective resolution.

4. **Theorem Implication:** The Nyquist theorem assumes the input is **band-limited**. The anti-aliasing filter enforces this assumption in the physical analog domain.

---

**Q(b) [7 marks]:** An industrial PLC monitors a motor's current signal. The current contains a fundamental 50 Hz component and a switching harmonic at 4.8 kHz. The PLC samples the current at 1 kHz. Determine:
(i) Whether aliasing occurs.
(ii) The alias frequency of the harmonic.
(iii) The minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Aliasing Check (2 marks):**
$f_{max} = 4800$ Hz, $f_s = 1000$ Hz.
Nyquist requirement: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 9600$ Hz.
Since $1000 < 9600$, **aliasing WILL occur**.

**(ii) Alias Frequency (3 marks):**
For $f_{in} = 4800$ Hz and $f_s = 1000$ Hz:

$$n = \text{round}(4800/1000) = 5$$

$$f_{alias} = \vert 4800 - 5 \times 1000 \vert = \vert 4800 - 5000 \vert = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

The 4.8 kHz switching harmonic will appear as a **200 Hz component**, which could be misinterpreted as a motor fault signature.

**(iii) Minimum Sampling Rate (2 marks):**
$$f_{s,min} = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 4800 = 9600 \text{ Hz}$$
With a practical safety margin: $f_s \approx 10$ kHz.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency:** Many students write $f_N = f_s/2$. Wrong — that is the Nyquist *Frequency*. The Nyquist *Rate* is $2 f_{max}$.
> 2. **Skipping the guard band:** Quoting $f_c = f_s/2$ exactly is impractical. Always mention a guard band for real filters.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "before the S/H":** A 1-mark penalty is given if the anti-aliasing filter placement is not specified.
> 4. **Confusing "aliasing" with "quantization error":** These are entirely different phenomena. Aliasing = temporal/sampling artifact; Quantization = amplitude-resolution artifact.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Mixing Hz with samples/second carelessly. Always specify units explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST:**

- ⭐ **Aliasing** = false low-frequency appearance of high-frequency components after sampling.
- ⭐ **Nyquist Criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (mandatory condition).
- ⭐ **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_{max}$ (minimum required sampling rate).
- ⭐ **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_s/2$ (folding frequency).
- ⭐ **Alias Formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - n f_s \vert$, where $n = \text{round}(f_{in}/f_s)$.
- ⭐ **Anti-aliasing filter** is always **analog**, always placed **before the S/H and ADC**.
- ⭐ **Cutoff frequency:** $f_c \leq f_s/2$ (with practical guard band).
- ⭐ Aliasing causes **irreversible information loss** — no digital filter can fix it post-sampling.
- ⭐ **Oversampling** (sampling much faster than Nyquist) relaxes anti-aliasing filter design.
- ⭐ In **PLCs and DAS**, anti-aliasing filters are integrated inside analog input modules.
- ⭐ **Butterworth filter** is the standard choice due to maximally flat passband response.
- ⭐ KTU exam keywords to memorize: *band-limited, spectral overlap, Nyquist, sampling theorem, guard band, oversampling, undersampling, reconstruction, sample-and-hold*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample and Hold Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sample and Hold Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit** is an essential analog signal-conditioning sub-system used at the front-end of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) within a Data Acquisition System (DAS). It is a *time-discretizing* network that captures (samples) the instantaneous amplitude of a continuous-time analog input signal $v_{in}(t)$ and *retains* (holds) that voltage magnitude constant on an internal storage element (typically a high-grade capacitor) for a finite interval called the **Hold Time ($T_H$)**.

In formal KTU terminology, the S/H circuit acts as a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** data reconstructor, mathematically described by the transfer relationship:

$$v_{out}(t) = v_{in}(kT_s) \quad \text{for} \quad kT_s \le t < (k+1)T_s$$

where $T_s$ is the **Sampling Period** dictated by the system clock, and $k$ is an integer index.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sample and Hold circuits are classified as **front-end analog memory elements**. They are mandatory before any successive-approximation ADC (SAR-ADC) or flash ADC, because conversion requires a *stationary* input for the entire conversion window $T_{conv}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Photographer & Frozen Frame" Model

Imagine you are photographing a **fast-moving racing car** with a standard camera. If the shutter is open, the image becomes a *blur* because the car moves during exposure. To get a **crisp photograph**, you need a shutter speed faster than the motion — that is, you capture a *single instant* and *freeze* it.

The **Sample and Hold circuit is precisely this "electronic shutter"**:

| Photography Concept | S/H Circuit Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Racing car (moving object) | Continuous-time analog input $v_{in}(t)$ |
| Camera shutter (open/close) | Electronic switch (FET / MOSFET) |
| Photographic film / sensor plate | **Hold Capacitor ($C_H$)** |
| Frozen photograph on print | Constant output voltage $V_{out}$ during hold |
| Time to take next photo | Sample interval $T_s = 1/f_s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** The S/H circuit "asks" the continuously-changing input signal *“What is your value right NOW?”* and then "remembers" that value perfectly still, giving the downstream ADC a non-moving target to digitize. Without it, the ADC would be digitizing a *moving target* — a recipe for massive conversion noise called **aperture error**.

---

## 1.3 Why is S/H Indispensable in a Data Acquisition System?

- **Aperture Error Mitigation:** Analog signals can vary by several mV during an ADC's conversion time. The S/H *freezes* the signal, eliminating aperture uncertainty $\Delta t_{ap}$.
- **Multiplexer De-Multiplexing:** In a multiplexed DAS, the S/H acts as a *per-channel analog memory*, retaining a channel's voltage while the multiplexer advances to the next.
- **Anti-Aliasing Support:** When paired with an anti-aliasing filter, the S/H provides a flat, step-like input to the ADC, simplifying filter design.
- **High-Frequency Capture:** Enables *equivalent-time sampling* for signals whose bandwidth exceeds the ADC's instantaneous sampling rate.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** If the input signal can change by more than **½ LSB** during the ADC conversion time, you **must** use a Sample and Hold circuit. The ½ LSB criterion gives us the relation: $T_{conv} \le \dfrac{1}{2^n \cdot f_{max}}$, where $n$ is the ADC resolution in bits.

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Quick-Reference Glossary)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Acquisition Time | $t_{AQ}$ | **0.5 μs – 50 μs** |
| Aperture Time (Delay) | $t_{ap}$ | **5 ns – 100 ns** |
| Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter) | $\Delta t_{ap}$ | **0.1 ns – 5 ns** |
| Hold Mode Droop | $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}$ | **0.1 μV/μs – 1 mV/μs** |
| Feedthrough Attenuation | FT | **–60 dB to –90 dB** |
| Sample-to-Hold Offset | $V_{SO}$ | **±0.5 mV – ±5 mV** |
| Hold Mode Settling Time | $t_{HS}$ | **0.5 μs – 5 μs** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Mode Operational Cycle

The S/H circuit operates in a strictly defined **two-state finite machine** cycle, governed by a digital control signal $S/H$:

### **MODE 1: SAMPLE (Track) MODE**
- The electronic switch (typically a JFET, MOSFET, or CMOS transmission gate) is **CLOSED**.
- The hold capacitor $C_H$ is *directly connected* to the input through a low ON-resistance $R_{ON}$.
- The output buffer amplifier (unity-gain) replicates $v_{in}(t)$ at $V_{out}$ with negligible delay.
- Capacitor charges/discharges to track the input: it is a **first-order RC low-pass follower**.

### **MODE 2: HOLD MODE**
- The switch is **OPENED** (turn-off transient $t_{off}$).
- The capacitor is *isolated* from the input — its voltage is now "frozen" at $V_C = v_{in}(t_{ap})$.
- The high-impedance buffer (op-amp with FET input) prevents discharge of $C_H$.
- The output $V_{out}$ remains *constant* for the entire hold interval $T_H$, allowing the ADC to digitize the value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Hold" is NOT Eternal:** Even in hold mode, $C_H$ slowly loses charge through (a) switch OFF-leakage $I_{leak}$, (b) op-amp input bias current $I_B$, and (c) PCB board leakage. This produces a finite **Hold-Mode Droop** that must be quantified.

---

## 2.2 The Three Critical Phases in a Complete S/H Cycle

A complete S/H cycle consists of three analytically distinct phases, each with its own governing equation:

### **Phase I — Acquisition (Charging) Phase**
During the sample interval, the capacitor must charge from its previous hold value to within ±½ LSB of the new input voltage $V_{in}$. The transient response is a classic first-order RC step:

$$V_C(t) = V_{in} - (V_{in} - V_{prev}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{acq}}$$

where the acquisition time-constant is:

$$\tau_{acq} = (R_{ON} + R_S) \cdot C_H$$

and $R_S$ is the source resistance of the input signal.

### **Phase II — Aperture (Switching) Phase**
A non-zero interval during which the switch transitions from ON to OFF. Defines:
- **Aperture Delay** $t_D$: time from S/H control edge to switch actually opening.
- **Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter)** $\Delta t_{ap}$: RMS variation in $t_D$ from sample to sample.

### **Phase III — Hold (Droop) Phase**
With switch open, leakage currents discharge $C_H$. The droop rate is:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_C(t) = V_{in}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Capacitor charging during sample | Volts |
| 2 | $\tau_{acq} = R_{ON} \cdot C_H$ | Acquisition time-constant | seconds |
| 3 | $t_{AQ} = \tau_{acq} \cdot \ln(2^{n+1})$ | Time to settle within ½ LSB (n-bit) | seconds |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt} = -\dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$ | Hold-mode voltage droop | V/s |
| 5 | $V_{error,max} = \dfrac{dV_o}{dt} \cdot T_{conv}$ | Max ADC error due to droop | Volts |
| 6 | $V_{aperture} = \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt} \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$ | Aperture-induced peak error | Volts |
| 7 | $f_{s,min} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist-Shannon lower limit | Hz |
| 8 | $T_{total} = t_{AQ} + t_{ap} + T_H + t_{HS}$ | Full S/H cycle budget | seconds |
| 9 | $S/H = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{FT}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Feedthrough attenuation in dB | dB |
| 10 | $Q = C_H \cdot V_{in}$ | Charge stored on hold capacitor | Coulombs |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Rule Applied:** In Formula 9 above, the absolute value expression was deliberately rewritten as $\left(\dfrac{V_{FT}}{V_{in}}\right)$ instead of using vertical bars, to preserve Markdown table integrity. The same convention applies throughout these notes.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of S/H |
|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT)** | Freezing bio-electric signals (ECG/EEG) for high-resolution digitisation |
| **Oscilloscopes (DSO)** | Equivalent-time sampling of GHz-bandwidth signals |
| **Audio CD/DVD Players** | Anti-aliasing front-end of 16/24-bit sigma-delta ADCs |
| **Automotive ECU** | Multiplexed sampling of 100+ sensor channels |
| **Radar & Sonar** | Coherent integration in phased-array receivers |
| **Process Control PLCs** | Front-end of SAR-ADCs in industrial PLC modules |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Acquisition Time for N-Bit Resolution

**Problem Statement:** Find the minimum sample-mode duration $t_{AQ}$ required for a S/H circuit to charge its hold capacitor to within ±½ LSB of the input voltage, given an N-bit ADC and acquisition time-constant $\tau_{acq}$.

**Step 1 — Express the instantaneous error during charging:**

Starting from the first-order step response, the difference between the capacitor voltage and the final value is:

$$e(t) = V_{in} - V_C(t) = (V_{in} - V_{prev}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{acq}}$$

**Step 2 — Set the worst-case initial condition:** The maximum error occurs when the previous sample was at the opposite rail ($V_{prev} = 0$, $V_{in} = V_{FS}$, the full-scale voltage). The error magnitude becomes:

$$\vert e(t) \vert = V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{acq}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ½ LSB settling criterion for an N-bit ADC:**

The quantization step (LSB size) is $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$. The ½ LSB tolerance is:

$$\vert e(t) \vert \le \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $t_{AQ}$:**

$$V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t_{AQ}/\tau_{acq}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}}$$

$$e^{-t_{AQ}/\tau_{acq}} = 2^{-(N+1)}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{t_{AQ}}{\tau_{acq}} = -(N+1) \cdot \ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{AQ} = (N+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot \tau_{acq} \approx 0.693 \cdot (N+1) \cdot \tau_{acq}\,}$$

**Engineering Insight:** For a 12-bit ADC with $\tau_{acq} = 100$ ns, we get $t_{AQ} = 13 \times 0.693 \times 100$ ns $\approx 901$ ns. For a 16-bit ADC the same circuit would need $t_{AQ} \approx 1.18$ μs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Hold-Mode Droop Rate

**Problem Statement:** Quantify the rate at which the held voltage droops during the hold interval, given a total leakage current $I_{leak}$ discharging the hold capacitor $C_H$.

**Step 1 — Fundamental capacitor charge equation:** The instantaneous charge stored on $C_H$ is:

$$Q(t) = C_H \cdot V_o(t)$$

**Step 2 — Current is the rate of charge flow:** By definition:

$$I_{leak} = -\frac{dQ}{dt} = -C_H \cdot \frac{dV_o}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the droop rate:**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$

**Step 4 — Total droop over hold interval $T_H$ (linear approximation, small $T_H$):**

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \left\vert \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert \cdot T_H = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot T_H}{C_H}$$

**Numerical Example:** With $I_{leak} = 1$ nA, $C_H = 100$ pF, $T_H = 10$ μs:

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \frac{(10^{-9}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6})}{100 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.1 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H} \quad ; \quad \Delta V_{droop} = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot T_H}{C_H}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Aperture Error for a Sinusoidal Input

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the peak aperture error when digitising a sinusoid $v_{in}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ with a S/H that has aperture uncertainty (jitter) $\Delta t_{ap}$.

**Step 1 — Derivative of the input signal:**

$$\frac{dv_{in}}{dt} = 2\pi f \cdot V_p \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Peak rate of change:** The maximum slope occurs at zero-crossings:

$$\left\vert \frac{dv_{in}}{dt} \right\vert_{max} = 2\pi f \cdot V_p$$

**Step 3 — Peak aperture error:** During the aperture uncertainty window $\Delta t_{ap}$, the worst-case voltage excursion is:

$$V_{aperture,max} = 2\pi f \cdot V_p \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $f = 1$ MHz, $V_p = 5$ V, $\Delta t_{ap} = 1$ ns:

$$V_{aperture,max} = 2\pi \cdot (10^6) \cdot 5 \cdot (10^{-9}) = 31.4 \text{ mV}$$

This corresponds to **6.4 LSBs of a 12-bit ADC** with a 10 V full-scale — clearly unacceptable for high-resolution work, justifying the use of low-jitter sample clocks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Complete S/H Cycle Time Budget

The total cycle time of a S/H in a multiplexed DAS must satisfy the inequality:

$$T_s \ge t_{AQ} + t_{HS} + T_{conv,ADC}$$

where each term has a specific physical meaning:

| Term | Symbol | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Acquisition time | $t_{AQ}$ | Capacitor charging window |
| Hold settling time | $t_{HS}$ | Buffer amplifier settling after switch open |
| ADC conversion time | $T_{conv,ADC}$ | Internal ADC clock cycles |
| **Total** | $T_s$ | Sampling period (≤ 1/f_s) |

For a typical SAR-ADC-based DAS with a 12-bit ADC running at 100 kS/s, the budget is approximately:

$$T_s = 1/100{,}000 = 10 \text{ μs} = t_{AQ}(0.9) + t_{HS}(0.5) + T_{conv}(8.6)$$

(in microseconds).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic S/H Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Analog Input Vin] --> swA1[Electronic Switch MOSFET or JFET]
    ctrlA[Digital Sample or Hold Control] --> swA1
    swA1 --> capA1[Hold Capacitor CH]
    capA1 --> bufA1[High Impedance Buffer OpAmp]
    bufA1 --> outA[Analog Output Vout to ADC]
    capA1 --> gndA[Ground Reference]
    ctrlA -.controls timing.-> bufA1
    classDef inStyle fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef swStyle fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef capStyle fill:#385723,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bufStyle fill:#7f6000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrlStyle fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outStyle fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    class inA,outA inStyle,outStyle
    class swA1 swStyle
    class capA1 capStyle
    class bufA1 bufStyle
    class ctrlA ctrlStyle
```

---

## 4.2 Operational Timeline / State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SAMPLE
    SAMPLE --> SWITCHING: Control Signal S or H Goes LOW at t equals tD
    SWITCHING --> HOLD: Aperture Time tap Elapses
    HOLD --> SAMPLE: Next Sample Edge Arrives at Ts minus tHS
    note right of SAMPLE: Capacitor CH charges to Vin via RON
    note right of SWITCHING: Switch turns OFF with jitter Delta tap
    note right of HOLD: Capacitor isolated Voltage frozen Droop occurs
```

---

## 4.3 Closed-Loop S/H with Op-Amp Buffer (Most Common Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inB[Input Vin to Non Inverting Terminal of OpAmp] --> opB1[OpAmp configured as Unity Gain Follower]
    ctrlB[Digital S or H Control] --> drvB1[Level Shifter and Driver]
    drvB1 --> fetB1[N Channel MOSFET Switch]
    inB --> fetB1
    fetB1 --> capB1[Hold Capacitor CH Connected to Virtual Ground]
    capB1 --> opB1
    opB1 --> outB[Buffered Output Vout]
    classDef inStyle2 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef opStyle2 fill:#7f6000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef capStyle2 fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrlStyle2 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef fetStyle2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    class inB,outB inStyle2
    class opB1 opStyle2
    class capB1 capStyle2
    class ctrlB,drvB1 ctrlStyle2
    class fetB1 fetStyle2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Topology Insight:** Placing $C_H$ inside the op-amp's feedback loop (at the *virtual ground*) eliminates the *signal-dependent* charge injection from the MOSFET — this is the **Integrator-Type S/H** and is used in the LF398 / AD582 monolithic S/H ICs.

---

## 4.4 Acquisition vs Hold Timing Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tA[Time Axis] --> tA1[Sample Window tAQ]
    tA1 --> tA2[Aperture Window tap]
    tA2 --> tA3[Hold Window TH]
    tA3 --> tA4[Settling tHS]
    tA4 --> tA5[Next Sample Edge]
    vinA[Vin continuous sinusoid] -.passes through.-> tA
    ctrlA2[Control S or H digital pulse] -.gates.-> tA2
    voutA[Vout step reconstruction ZOH] -.appears during.-> tA3
    classDef windowStyle fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef sigStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    class tA1,tA2,tA3,tA4,tA5 windowStyle
    class vinA,ctrlA2,voutA sigStyle
```

---

## 4.5 Signal-Flow Summary (End-to-End DAS Front-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensA[Physical Sensor] --> sigA1[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    sigA1 --> sigA2[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    sigA2 --> sigA3[S or H Circuit]
    sigA3 --> sigA4[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    sigA4 --> sigA5[Microcontroller or PLC CPU]
    clkA[Master Clock] -.synchronises.-> sigA3
    clkA -.synchronises.-> sigA4
    classDef stageStyle fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef logicStyle fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    class sensA,sigA1,sigA2,sigA3,sigA4 stageStyle
    class sigA5,clkA logicStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Sample and Hold circuit. List any two important specifications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Sample and Hold (S/H) circuit is an analog device that *samples* the instantaneous value of a continuously varying input signal and *holds* (freezes) that value constant for a specified duration. It is used as a front-end stage before an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) in a Data Acquisition System to prevent signal variation during the conversion period.

**Two Important Specifications:**

1. **Acquisition Time ($t_{AQ}$):** The time required for the S/H to acquire a new input value and settle within a specified error band (typically ±½ LSB) after the sample command is asserted. Typical values range from **0.5 μs to 50 μs**.

2. **Hold-Mode Droop Rate ($\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}$):** The rate at which the held output voltage decays during the hold interval due to leakage currents. Typical values range from **0.1 μV/μs to 1 mV/μs**, depending on capacitor size and switch leakage.

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each specification, ½ mark for the numerical range / units.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the term "aperture time" of a Sample and Hold circuit. Why is it critical in high-frequency applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$):** Aperture time is the small but finite interval required for the electronic switch (typically a MOSFET) to *transition* from the closed (sample) state to the open (hold) state after receiving the hold command. It represents the *delay* between the issuance of the control signal edge and the actual disconnection of the hold capacitor from the input.

**Criticality in High-Frequency Applications:**

For a high-frequency sinusoid $v_{in}(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the maximum rate of voltage change is $2\pi f \cdot V_p$. During the aperture window, the input may change by an *uncontrolled* amount, leading to an *aperture error* of magnitude:

$$V_{error} = 2\pi f \cdot V_p \cdot t_{ap}$$

For example, at $f = 10$ MHz, $V_p = 5$ V, $t_{ap} = 50$ ns, the error is $V_{error} \approx 15.7$ mV — equivalent to nearly 6.5 LSBs of a 10-bit ADC, which is unacceptably large. Hence, for high-frequency signals, a S/H with *small* and *well-defined* aperture time is mandatory.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for explanation with mathematical reasoning.)*

---

## 📚 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, each Part-B question carries 14 marks with a Module-Internal Choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Draw the functional block diagram of a Sample and Hold circuit and explain the operation in both *Sample* and *Hold* modes. Discuss the role of the hold capacitor and the buffer amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **acquisition time** of a S/H circuit settling to within ½ LSB for an N-bit ADC, starting from the first-order RC step response. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** The functional blocks are:

1. **Input Stage / Source Resistance ($R_S$):** Provides the input signal.
2. **Electronic Switch (MOSFET/JFET, $R_{ON}$):** Connects/disconnects the input to the hold capacitor.
3. **Hold Capacitor ($C_H$):** Stores the sampled voltage.
4. **High-Input-Impedance Buffer (Op-Amp, $Z_{in} \to \infty$):** Prevents loading of $C_H$ and provides low output impedance to drive the ADC.

**Sample Mode Operation (2 Marks):** When the digital control signal is *HIGH* (S), the MOSFET switch is ON with small ON-resistance $R_{ON}$. The capacitor $C_H$ charges through the series combination $(R_S + R_{ON}) \cdot C_H$ time-constant. The output buffer replicates the input, and $V_{out} = V_{in}$ with negligible delay. The circuit behaves as a *unity-gain follower*.

**Hold Mode Operation (2 Marks):** When the control signal goes *LOW* (H), the MOSFET is turned OFF within a finite aperture time $t_{ap}$. The capacitor $C_H$ is now isolated from the input. The buffer amplifier (with its FET-input op-amp providing $\sim 10^{12}\ \Omega$ input impedance) presents a negligible discharge path, so the voltage across $C_H$ remains constant. The output is a *frozen* DC level equal to the value of $v_{in}$ at the moment of switching.

**Role of $C_H$:** Stores the sampled charge $Q = C_H \cdot V_{in}$ during hold. Should be large enough to minimize droop but small enough to allow fast acquisition.

**Role of Buffer Amplifier:** Provides (i) high input impedance (to prevent $C_H$ discharge) and (ii) low output impedance (to drive the ADC input capacitance without loading effects).

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Starting Equation (2 Marks):** During sample mode, the capacitor charges from a previous value $V_{prev}$ toward $V_{in}$ according to the first-order step response:

$$V_C(t) = V_{in} - (V_{in} - V_{prev}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{acq}}$$

where $\tau_{acq} = (R_{ON} + R_S) \cdot C_H$.

**Worst-Case Error Magnitude (1 Mark):** With $V_{prev} = 0$ and $V_{in} = V_{FS}$ (worst case):

$$\vert V_{in} - V_C(t) \vert = V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{acq}}$$

**½ LSB Settling Criterion (2 Marks):** For an N-bit ADC, ½ LSB = $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}}$. Setting the error ≤ ½ LSB:

$$V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t_{AQ}/\tau_{acq}} \le \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}}$$

$$e^{-t_{AQ}/\tau_{acq}} \le 2^{-(N+1)}$$

**Solving (2 Marks):** Taking natural log:

$$-\frac{t_{AQ}}{\tau_{acq}} \le -(N+1) \cdot \ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{AQ} \ge (N+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot \tau_{acq} \approx 0.693 \cdot (N+1) \cdot \tau_{acq}\,}$$

**Result & Interpretation:** For a 12-bit ADC with $\tau_{acq} = 100$ ns:

$$t_{AQ} = 0.693 \times 13 \times 100 \text{ ns} \approx 901 \text{ ns}$$

This must be **strictly less** than the available sample window $T_s$ minus the ADC conversion time, otherwise the S/H will *not* settle and conversion accuracy will be lost.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain the term **Hold-Mode Droop**. Derive the expression for the droop rate and the maximum droop voltage over the hold interval $T_H$ for a hold capacitor $C_H$ and total leakage current $I_{leak}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A S/H circuit is used in front of a 12-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS} = 10$ V. The total leakage current during hold mode is $I_{leak} = 5$ nA and the hold capacitor is $C_H = 1$ nF. The ADC conversion time is $T_{conv} = 10$ μs. **(i)** Calculate the droop rate. **(ii)** Find the maximum droop voltage during one conversion cycle. **(iii)** Determine whether this droop is acceptable for a 12-bit ADC. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):** Hold-mode droop is the *unwanted decrease* in the held output voltage $V_{out}$ during the hold interval, caused by discharge of $C_H$ through parasitic leakage paths: (i) MOSFET OFF-state leakage, (ii) op-amp input bias current, and (iii) PCB surface leakage. It limits the maximum allowable hold duration for a given accuracy.

**Derivation (4 Marks):** From the capacitor charge-current relationship:

$$I_{leak} = -\frac{dQ}{dt} = -C_H \cdot \frac{dV_o}{dt}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$

Integrating over the hold interval $[0, T_H]$ (assuming constant $I_{leak}$):

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \left\vert \frac{I_{leak}}{C_H} \right\vert \cdot T_H$$

**Significance (1 Mark):** Droop effectively *adds a negative slope* to the held staircase. If the droop is excessive, the ADC's last bit decisions will be wrong. The design rule is $\Delta V_{droop} \le \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N+1}}$.

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:** $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $I_{leak} = 5$ nA, $C_H = 1$ nF, $T_{conv} = 10$ μs, $N = 12$ bits.

**(i) Droop Rate (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H} = -\frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = -5 \text{ V/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,\left\vert \frac{dV_o}{dt} \right\vert = 5 \text{ V/s} = 5 \text{ μV/μs}\,}$$

**(ii) Maximum Droop Voltage (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta V_{droop} = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot T_{conv}}{C_H} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta V_{droop} = 50 \text{ μV}\,}$$

**(iii) Acceptability Check (3 Marks):**

½ LSB of a 12-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 10$ V:

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2 \times 4096} = \frac{10}{8192} \approx 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

Since $\Delta V_{droop} = 50$ μV $\ll$ 1.22 mV (½ LSB), the droop is **well within tolerance** and **acceptable** for 12-bit accuracy. The droop consumes only $\dfrac{50\ \mu V}{1.22\ mV} \approx 4.1\%$ of the available error budget, leaving ample headroom for other error sources.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Acquisition Time with Hold Time:** Students frequently swap the definitions. Memorize: *Acquisition = charging time in SAMPLE mode*; *Hold = duration for which the value is preserved.*
> 2. **Forgetting the $(N+1)$ factor:** The acquisition-time formula is $t_{AQ} = (N+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot \tau$, **not** $N \cdot \ln(2) \cdot \tau$. The "off-by-one" mistake can cost 1 full mark.
> 3. **Using $|$ inside LaTeX tables:** Markdown tables interpret a single `|` as a column separator. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars inside tables.
> 4. **Ignoring droop sign:** Droop is *negative* (capacitor discharges). Reporting it as a positive number without the sign convention may cost a mark in analytical problems.
> 5. **Confusing aperture time with aperture uncertainty:** Aperture time is a *fixed* delay; aperture uncertainty (jitter) is the *random* variation — they are *not* the same.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Definition:** S/H circuit captures and freezes an analog voltage for ADC conversion.
- [x] **Two Modes:** SAMPLE (switch closed, $C_H$ tracks input) and HOLD (switch open, $C_H$ isolated).
- [x] **Three Phases:** Acquisition → Aperture → Hold Droop.
- [x] **Key Block:** Input → Switch (MOSFET) → Hold Capacitor $C_H$ → Op-Amp Buffer → Output.
- [x] **Acquisition Time Formula:** $t_{AQ} = (N+1) \cdot \ln(2) \cdot R_{ON} \cdot C_H \approx 0.693 (N+1)\tau$.
- [x] **Droop Rate:** $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt} = -\dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$.
- [x] **Droop Voltage:** $\Delta V = I_{leak} \cdot T_H / C_H$.
- [x] **Aperture Error:** $V_{aperture} = 2\pi f \cdot V_p \cdot \Delta t_{ap}$.
- [x] **½ LSB Criterion:** Droop and aperture error both must be ≤ $V_{FS}/2^{N+1}$.
- [x] **Common ICs:** LF398, AD582, AD684, SHC5320 (monolithic S/H ICs).
- [x] **Topologies:** Open-loop, closed-loop (integrator-type), switched-capacitor.
- [x] **Design Trade-off:** Larger $C_H$ → less droop but slower acquisition. Choose $C_H \sim 100$ pF – 10 nF.
- [x] **Sampling Theorem Compliance:** Sampling frequency $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist).
- [x] **Applications:** SAR/Flash ADC front-ends, DAS multiplexers, oscilloscope equivalent-time sampling, DSO peak detectors, PLL feedback paths.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Implementation of Sampling and Digitizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Practical Implementation of Sampling and Digitizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal into a discrete-time signal by measuring its instantaneous amplitude at uniform intervals governed by a clock. **Digitizing** (or Quantization) is the subsequent process of mapping each sampled amplitude to a finite set of discrete numerical codes using an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). Together, the **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** and the **ADC** form the practical hardware front-end of any Data Acquisition System (DAS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Block (Board-Examiner phrasing):**
> *"A Data Acquisition System performs sampling (discretization in time) using a Sample-and-Hold amplifier, followed by digitizing (discretization in amplitude) using an ADC. The combination of these two operations converts a real-world analog signal into a binary word suitable for microprocessor-based processing."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are watching a **merry-go-round (carousel)** spinning slowly and trying to determine its exact speed using a **single-camera taking photographs**:

| DAS Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Analog Signal** | The continuously moving carousel horse |
| **Sampling (taking snapshot)** | Camera shutter snapping a still photo |
| **Sample Rate (f_s)** | How many photos you take per second |
| **Sample-and-Hold** | The camera must keep the shutter open just long enough to "freeze" the motion |
| **Aperture Time** | The brief instant the shutter is actually open |
| **Quantization (Bit Depth)** | Number of distinct colors your camera can record (8-bit = 256 colors, 16-bit = 65,536 colors) |
| **ADC Resolution (N bits)** | More bits → smoother, more accurate photo |
| **Aliasing** | If you photograph too slowly, the wheel looks like it's spinning backwards! |

> [!NOTE]
> The **faster** the shutter clicks (higher sampling rate) and the **more colors** the camera can record (higher bit depth), the more faithfully the digital photo represents the real carousel. This is the *entire* engineering trade-off in a DAS.

### 1.3 Key Hardware Sub-Systems in Practical Implementation

1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** (Low-Pass, pre-sampling) – limits input bandwidth to f_s/2.
2. **Multiplexer (MUX)** – shares one ADC across many channels (time-division).
3. **Sample-and-Hold Amplifier (SHA / S/H)** – freezes analog voltage during conversion.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** – quantizes the held voltage into binary code.
5. **Reconstruction Filter / DAC** – for output or waveform reconstruction (ZOH).
6. **Microcontroller/Processor Interface** – reads digital word, controls timing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Practical implementation focuses on the *non-ideal* behaviour of the S/H (aperture time, jitter, droop) and the *finite resolution* of the ADC (quantization error, SNR). These imperfections—not ideal theory—determine real system performance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit – Step-by-Step Operation

A practical S/H consists of an **input buffer amplifier**, an **electronic switch** (typically a MOSFET or JFET), a **hold capacitor (C_H)**, and an **output buffer amplifier** (high input impedance to prevent C_H discharge).

**Operational Phases (Bulleted Logic):**

1. **Track (Sample) Mode:** Switch is **CLOSED**.
   - C_H charges to track the input voltage v_in(t).
   - Output v_out follows v_in.
   - Limited only by the RC time constant: τ = R_on × C_H.
2. **Hold Mode:** Switch is **OPENED**.
   - The capacitor C_H "freezes" the voltage at the value present at the moment of switching.
   - The high-impedance output buffer prevents discharge.
3. **Aperture Delay (t_ad):** The small time interval between the *command* to hold and the *actual* opening of the switch.
4. **Aperture Uncertainty / Jitter (t_au):** The randomness in t_ad from sample to sample—**the most critical parameter for high-frequency signals**.
5. **Acquisition Time (t_acq):** Time required (in track mode) for the output to settle within a specified accuracy band of the new input voltage.
6. **Hold Mode Settling Time (t_hs):** Time for the circuit to reach a stable held value after the switch opens.
7. **Droop Rate (dV/dt):** Unwanted slow discharge of C_H during hold due to leakage currents (MOSFET off-leakage, buffer input bias current).
8. **Hold Step / Pedestal Error:** A small voltage step introduced by switch-capacitive coupling (charge injection) when the switch opens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Jitter Matters:** The maximum slew rate that can be captured without significant amplitude error is governed by:
> Slew Rate ≤ ±(Quantization step) / (2 × t_au)
> At high frequencies, **aperture jitter—not bit resolution—limits the ENOB (Effective Number of Bits).**

### 2.2 ADC Architectures – Practical Comparison for KTU

| ADC Type | Resolution (typical) | Conversion Time | Key Feature | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash (Parallel)** | 6–8 bits | ~ ns (1 clock) | Fastest, expensive (2^N − 1 comparators) | High-speed oscilloscopes, video |
| **Successive Approximation (SAR)** | 8–18 bits | N clock cycles | Excellent speed/resolution trade-off | **Most common in DAS** (default answer) |
| **Dual-Slope Integrating** | 12–22 bits | ms range | Excellent noise rejection, slow | Digital voltmeters, industrial meters |
| **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ)** | 16–24 bits | High oversampling, decimation | High resolution, built-in filtering | Audio, precision measurement, PLC analog cards |
| **Pipelined** | 10–16 bits | Few clock cycles per stage | High throughput, moderate resolution | Communication, CCD imaging |

### 2.3 Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC – Working Principle

This is the **single most important ADC type for KTU 2024 boards**, since it is the default in most PLC and industrial DAS modules.

**Step-by-step Logic (Bulleted):**

1. On the start-of-conversion (SOC) command, the SAR sets the MSB = 1, all other bits = 0.
2. This trial code is fed to an internal **DAC**, producing analog voltage V_DAC = V_ref × (trial code / 2^N).
3. The comparator checks: **Is V_in > V_DAC?**
   - If **YES** → keep MSB = 1.
   - If **NO** → reset MSB = 0.
4. Move to the next bit (MSB−1), set it to 1, and repeat the test.
5. After **N trials**, the trial code = the final digital output.
6. **Conversion time = N × T_clock** (deterministic and predictable → ideal for PLC scan cycles).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for any KTU 2024 question on sampling & digitizing.** All values use SI units unless stated.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quantization step (LSB size) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ | Voltage represented by 1 LSB |
| 2 | Max Quantization Error | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | ±½ LSB (mid-rise quantizer) |
| 3 | RMS Quantization Noise | $e_{rms} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N \sqrt{12}}$ | For uniformly distributed noise |
| 4 | **Ideal SNR (dB) for full-scale sine** | $\mathrm{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \ \mathrm{dB}$ | **Most important KTU formula** |
| 5 | Dynamic Range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}(2^N) = 6.02\,N \ \mathrm{dB}$ | Ratio of max to min detectable signal |
| 6 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum rate to avoid aliasing |
| 7 | Aperture Frequency Limit | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\, t_{au}}$ | Max input freq for given jitter |
| 8 | Hold Capacitor Droop | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$ | V/s droop during hold |
| 9 | Acquisition Time (10-bit, settling) | $t_{acq} \approx (N+1)\cdot \tau = 11 \cdot R_{on} C_H$ | Time to charge C_H within ½ LSB |
| 10 | Throughput (SAR ADC) | $f_{sample} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot T_{clk}}$ | Samples per second |
| 11 | Effective Bits from measured SNR | $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Real-world bit resolution |
| 12 | Reconstruction (ZOH) Frequency Response | $H_{ZOH}(f) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi f / f_s)}{\pi f / f_s} \cdot e^{-j\pi f / f_s}$ | Sinc-shaped droop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The 6.02N + 1.76 dB formula is used by IC designers at Analog Devices, Texas Instruments, and Siemens (for their SIMATIC S7 analog input modules) to specify ADC ENOB. Every extra bit of resolution costs roughly 4× the silicon area and doubles power consumption.

### 2.5 Practical Reconstruction – Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

After processing, the digital signal must be converted back to analog using a DAC. The most common practical method is the **Zero-Order Hold**, which simply holds the last digital value constant until the next sample arrives, producing a **staircase waveform**. A low-pass **smoothing filter** is then applied to remove the staircase ripple.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The 6.02N + 1.76 dB SNR Formula

**Assumption:** The input is a full-scale sine wave, and the quantizer is ideal with uniformly distributed quantization error.

**Step 1 — Define the input signal.**

Let the full-scale input be a sine wave of amplitude $V_{FS}/2$:

$$v_{in}(t) = \frac{V_{FS}}{2} \sin(2\pi f t)$$

The signal power (delivered to a 1 Ω load, or normalized) is:

$$P_s = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_{in}^2(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{V_{FS}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization error as a sawtooth/random variable.**

For an N-bit ideal ADC, the step size is:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

The quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in the interval $[-\Delta/2,\ +\Delta/2]$.

**Step 3 — Compute mean-square (RMS²) quantization noise power.**

For a uniform distribution, the mean square is:

$$P_n = \overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$P_n = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[\left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3\right] = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{8} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Δ in terms of V_FS and N.**

$$P_n = \frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$

**Step 5 — Form the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (linear).**

$$\mathrm{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{V_{FS}^2/8}{V_{FS}^2/(12 \cdot 4^N)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^N}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 4^N$$

Rewrite using powers of 2:

$$\mathrm{SNR} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels (10 log₁₀).**

$$\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2N})$$

Since $10 \log_{10}(2) = 10 \times 0.30103 = 3.0103$:

$$\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 2N \cdot 10 \log_{10}(2)$$

$$\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 1.7609 + 2N \times 3.0103 \approx 1.76 + 6.02\,N$$

$$\boxed{\ \mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76\ \text{dB}\ }$$

**Engineering Insight:** Each additional bit of resolution improves the SNR by **6.02 dB**—a four-fold power reduction in quantization noise. This is why high-precision ADCs (24-bit sigma-delta in PLC analog cards) cost substantially more.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Selecting ADC Resolution

**Problem (Typical KTU 2024 style):** A temperature sensor outputs 0–5 V representing 0–500 °C. The system requires a quantization error of less than ±0.5 °C. What is the minimum number of ADC bits required?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert temperature resolution to voltage resolution:

$$\Delta V \leq \frac{0.5\ ^\circ C}{500\ ^\circ C} \times 5\ \mathrm{V} = 0.005\ \mathrm{V}$$

Step 2 — The maximum quantization error is ±Δ/2, so step size Δ ≤ 0.01 V.

Step 3 — Apply $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$:

$$2^N \geq \frac{V_{FS}}{\Delta} = \frac{5}{0.01} = 500$$

Step 4 — Solve for N:

$$N \geq \log_2(500) = 8.966$$

Step 5 — Round up to the next integer:

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 9\ \text{bits}}$$

A practical designer would select a **10-bit or 12-bit** ADC for margin.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Aperture Jitter Limit

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC samples a 100 kHz sine wave with full-scale amplitude 10 V p-p. Find the maximum allowable aperture jitter if the SNR due to jitter must remain above 70 dB.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Quantization step for 12-bit, 10 V FS:

$$\Delta = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = 2.441\ \mathrm{mV}$$

Step 2 — Maximum slew rate of a 100 kHz, 10 V p-p (5 V amplitude) sine wave:

$$\left.\frac{dv}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f \cdot A = 2\pi \times 10^5 \times 5 = 3.1416 \times 10^6\ \mathrm{V/s}$$

Step 3 — The amplitude error due to jitter $t_{au}$ over a step Δ/2 is:

$$\Delta V_{error} = \left.\frac{dv}{dt}\right|_{max} \cdot t_{au} \leq \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

Step 4 — Solve for $t_{au}$:

$$t_{au} \leq \frac{\Delta}{2 \cdot \left.\frac{dv}{dt}\right|_{max}} = \frac{2.441 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{6}} \approx 3.88 \times 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{au} \leq 0.388\ \mathrm{ns}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At 100 kHz, a 12-bit ADC needs jitter < 0.4 ns. A 16-bit ADC at the same frequency would demand jitter < 0.01 ns—forcing the use of expensive track-and-hold ICs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: S/H vs Track-and-Hold

| Parameter | Sample-and-Hold (S/H) | Track-and-Hold (T/H) |
|---|---|---|
| Default state | Hold | Track |
| Switch position during hold | Open | Open |
| Switch position during sample | Closed | Closed |
| Typical use | When input is changing during conversion | When input is DC-stable for the acquisition window |
| Aperture error | Critical (jitter matters most) | Less critical (can place T/H after buffer) |
| Droop control | High-impedance buffer required | Same |
| KTU default assumption | **S/H** (switch is closed during track, open during hold) | Mentioned only for contrast |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Practical Sample-and-Hold Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    vin["Input Signal v_in"] --> bufIn["Input Buffer Amplifier"]
    bufIn --> sw["Analog Switch MOSFET JFET"]
    sw --> cap["Hold Capacitor C_H"]
    cap --> bufOut["Output Buffer High Z"]
    bufOut --> vout["Held Output v_out"]
    ctrl["Control Logic Clock"] --> sw
    leak["Leakage Current I_leak"] -.-> cap
```

**Reading the diagram:** During the **TRACK** phase, the control logic closes the switch, allowing the buffer to charge C_H to v_in. During the **HOLD** phase, the switch opens, and C_H retains the frozen voltage. The output buffer presents a high impedance to prevent droop. The dashed leakage arrow indicates the unwanted (parasitic) discharge path that limits the hold duration.

---

### 4.2 SAR ADC — Successive Approximation Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["Start of Conversion SOC Pulse"] --> msbSet["SAR sets MSB 1, others 0"]
    msbSet --> dacTrial["Internal DAC converts trial code to V_DAC"]
    dacTrial --> compare{"Comparator: V_in greater than V_DAC"}
    compare -- Yes --> keepBit["Keep current bit as 1"]
    compare -- No --> clearBit["Clear current bit to 0"]
    keepBit --> nextBit["Move to next lower bit, set to 1"]
    clearBit --> nextBit
    nextBit --> checkLast{"All N bits tested"}
    checkLast -- No --> dacTrial
    checkLast -- Yes --> output["Latch final N bit digital output"]
    output --> eoc["End of Conversion EOC flag"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each clock cycle performs one binary search step. The MSB is decided first (test 50% of FS), then the next bit (test 25% of FS), and so on. After N cycles, the digital word is complete. This is why SAR is called a "binary search" ADC.

---

### 4.3 Complete Data Acquisition System Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    sensor["Analog Sensor Transducer"] --> aaf["Anti Aliasing LPF f_c = f_s over 2"]
    aaf --> mux["Analog Multiplexer MUX"]
    mux --> sha["Sample and Hold SHA"]
    sha --> adc["ADC N bit SAR or Sigma Delta"]
    adc --> iface["Digital Interface SPI or Parallel"]
    iface --> cpu["Microcontroller or PLC CPU"]
    cpu --> dacOut["DAC Reconstruction"]
    dacOut --> smooth["Smoothing Filter LPF"]
    smooth --> analogOut["Analog Output to Actuator"]
    cpu --> display["HMI or Display"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical *end-to-end* signal chain in a PLC-based data acquisition card (e.g., Siemens SM 1231, Allen-Bradley 5069-IY4). The **anti-aliasing filter** must come *before* sampling; the **smoothing filter** must come *after* the reconstruction DAC. Violating this order causes aliasing or staircase ripple in the output.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the following terms with respect to a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit: (i) Aperture time, (ii) Aperture uncertainty (jitter), (iii) Acquisition time.**

**Model Answer (Board-style, 3 points):**

1. **Aperture Time:** The small but finite time interval during which the analog switch is in the process of opening, i.e., the period when the hold capacitor transitions from tracking the input to holding a fixed value. It is the time uncertainty around the exact sampling instant.
2. **Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter, $t_{au}$):** The sample-to-sample variation in the aperture delay. Caused by clock noise, switch-driver transients, and comparator metastability. It is the dominant error source for high-frequency, high-resolution ADCs.
3. **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$):** The time required, after switching back to TRACK mode, for the hold capacitor to charge to the new input voltage and settle within a defined error band (typically ±½ LSB or ±0.1 %). Depends on the RC time constant of the switch resistance and hold capacitor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**List the four major sources of error in a practical ADC and write the formula relating SNR to the number of bits for an ideal N-bit converter.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major sources of error in a practical ADC are:

1. **Quantization Error** (inherent ±½ LSB).
2. **Aperture Uncertainty / Jitter** (sampling-clock dependent).
3. **Non-Linearity** (Integral Non-Linearity INL and Differential Non-Linearity DNL).
4. **Thermal / kTC Noise** and comparator noise (random).

**Formula:**

$$\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76\ \mathrm{dB}$$

where $N$ is the number of bits of the ideal ADC and the input is a full-scale sine wave.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the block diagram of a practical Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit. With neat timing diagrams, describe its **Track** and **Hold** modes of operation. List any four practical specifications used to characterize a S/H circuit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC with a unipolar input range of 0 to +10 V is connected to a sensor output. The reference voltage is 10 V. Find:
  (i) The quantization step size (LSB value).
  (ii) The maximum quantization error in volts.
  (iii) The RMS quantization noise voltage.
  (iv) The ideal Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in dB for a full-scale sine input.
  (v) The dynamic range in dB.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A (a)**

**Block Diagram (textual, exam-friendly):**

A practical S/H circuit consists of:
1. **Input buffer amplifier** (low output impedance to charge C_H quickly).
2. **Electronic analog switch** (JFET or MOSFET).
3. **Hold capacitor (C_H)** (typically 100 pF to 10 nF).
4. **Output buffer amplifier** (high input impedance JFET op-amp, unity gain).

**Operation — Track Mode:**
- The control logic keeps the switch **closed**.
- The capacitor C_H charges through the on-resistance $R_{on}$ of the switch.
- The output buffer reproduces the input: $v_{out}(t) = v_{in}(t)$.
- Time constant: $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$.
- After ≥ 5τ, the output settles within < 1 % of the input.

**Operation — Hold Mode:**
- The control logic opens the switch at the sampling instant.
- The capacitor holds the last tracked voltage.
- The output buffer (high-Z input) prevents discharge: $v_{out}(t) = v_{in}(t_{sample})$.
- Droop occurs due to leakage current $I_{leak}$: $dV/dt = I_{leak}/C_H$.

**Four Practical Specifications:**
1. Acquisition time $t_{acq}$.
2. Aperture delay $t_{ad}$ and aperture jitter $t_{au}$.
3. Hold-mode droop rate (V/s).
4. Hold-step / pedestal error (mV).
5. Feed-through (in TRACK mode, fraction of input that leaks to output in HOLD mode).
6. Hold-mode settling time.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $N = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $V_{ref} = 10$ V.

**(i) Quantization Step (LSB):**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta = 2.4414\ \mathrm{mV}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$e_q = \pm\frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm\frac{2.4414}{2}$$

$$\boxed{e_q = \pm 1.2207\ \mathrm{mV}}$$

**(iii) RMS Quantization Noise:**

$$e_{rms} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{2.4414\ \mathrm{mV}}{3.4641}$$

$$\boxed{e_{rms} = 0.7048\ \mathrm{mV}}$$

**(iv) Ideal SNR (dB):**

$$\mathrm{SNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76$$

$$\boxed{\mathrm{SNR} = 74.00\ \mathrm{dB}}$$

**(v) Dynamic Range:**

$$DR = 20 \log_{10}(2^{12}) = 20 \times 12 \times 0.30103 = 6.02 \times 12$$

$$\boxed{DR = 72.24\ \mathrm{dB}}$$

---

#### **Question B — Alternative Choice for Internal Option [KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Show, using a timing diagram, how a 4-bit SAR ADC converts an analog input of 7.5 V (with $V_{ref} = 10$ V) to its digital equivalent, step by step.
