**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression $\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76\ \mathrm{dB}$ for an ideal N-bit ADC with a full-scale sinusoidal input. Hence, calculate the SNR for a 16-bit ADC. State the engineering significance of the 6.02 dB/bit factor.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B (a)**

**Block Diagram (functional, exam-friendly):**

The SAR ADC consists of:
1. **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** — holds the trial code.
2. **Internal N-bit DAC** — converts trial code to analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.
3. **High-speed Comparator** — compares $V_{in}$ with $V_{DAC}$.
4. **Control / Clock Logic** — drives the SAR and provides EOC.
5. **Output Latch** — holds the final N-bit word.

**Working — Binary Search Algorithm:**

On receiving the SOC (Start of Conversion) pulse, the SAR performs a binary search for the correct digital code. For each bit, it sets the bit to 1, lets the internal DAC produce the trial voltage, and the comparator decides whether to keep the bit (if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$) or reset it (if $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$).

**Worked Example — 4-bit SAR, $V_{in} = 7.5$ V, $V_{ref} = 10$ V:**

The quantization step is $\Delta = V_{ref}/2^4 = 10/16 = 0.625$ V.

| Clock Cycle | Bit Tested | Trial Code (binary) | $V_{DAC}$ (V) | Compare: $V_{in}$ vs $V_{DAC}$ | Decision | Final Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MSB (b3) | 1000 | 5.00 | 7.5 > 5.0 | Keep 1 | 1--- |
| 2 | b2 | 1100 | 7.50 | 7.5 = 7.5 | Keep 1 (≥) | 11-- |
| 3 | b1 | 1110 | 8.75 | 7.5 < 8.75 | Reset 0 | 110- |
| 4 | b0 | 1101 | 8.125 | 7.5 < 8.125 | Reset 0 | 1100 |

**Final 4-bit output:** $\boxed{1100_2 = 12_{10}}$

Verification: $12 \times 0.625 = 7.5$ V ✓

**Conversion time:** 4 clock cycles (deterministic).

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B (b) — Derivation & Calculation**

**Derivation** (full algebraic detail — see SECTION 3.1 for the step-by-step derivation, summarized here):

Starting from a full-scale sine wave input of amplitude $V_{FS}/2$:

$$P_s = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8},\quad P_n = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$

$$\mathrm{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}$$

$$\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 N \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02\,N$$

**Calculation for N = 16:**

$$\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76$$

$$\boxed{\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 98.08\ \mathrm{dB}}$$

**Engineering Significance of the 6.02 dB/bit factor:**
- Each additional bit **quadruples** the number of quantization levels and improves the SNR by ~6 dB.
- It sets the **theoretical ceiling** for dynamic range; any practical ADC has ENOB < N due to noise and linearity.
- It guides the **design trade-off** between resolution, sampling rate, and cost (a 24-bit sigma-delta achieves ~146 dB SNR but at low kHz rates).
- In **PLC analog modules** (e.g., ±10 V input at 16 bits → 0.305 mV LSB), the 6.02 dB/bit rule helps validate manufacturer ENOB claims (e.g., a "16-bit" ADC with 78 dB SNR has an effective ENOB of $(78 - 1.76)/6.02 \approx 12.7$ bits).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse $2^N$ with $2^N - 1$**: The formula $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ is the *mid-rise* quantizer convention. If your syllabus specifies $V_{FS}/(2^N - 1)$ (mid-tread with no zero error), use that—**read the question carefully**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1.76$ dB constant** when writing the SNR formula will cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up 20 log vs 6.02 dB/bit** for dynamic range vs SNR—dynamic range uses $20 \log(2^N)$ (voltage ratio), SNR uses $6.02N + 1.76$ (power ratio for sine input).
> 4. **Not mentioning aperture jitter** in S/H questions loses 2 marks. Examiners specifically test this in 2024 Scheme questions.
> 5. **For SAR timing diagrams**: forgetting to mark the *Start-of-Conversion* (SOC) and *End-of-Conversion* (EOC) signals will cost 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Sampling** = discretization in **time**; **Quantization / Digitizing** = discretization in **amplitude**.
- [x] Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Violation → **aliasing** (irreversible distortion).
- [x] **S/H circuit** stages: Input buffer → analog switch → hold capacitor C_H → output buffer.
- [x] Track mode: switch closed, $v_{out} = v_{in}$. Hold mode: switch open, $v_{out}$ frozen.
- [x] **Aperture jitter ($t_{au}$)** limits ENOB at high input frequencies.
- [x] **Acquisition time** $t_{acq} \approx (N+1) \cdot R_{on} C_H$ for N-bit settling.
- [x] **Droop rate** $dV/dt = I_{leak}/C_H$ — choose low-leakage JFETs and film capacitors.
- [x] **SAR ADC** is the workhorse of PLC and industrial DAS; conversion = N clock cycles.
- [x] **Quantization step** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$; max error ±Δ/2; RMS noise Δ/√12.
- [x] **Master formula:** $\mathrm{SNR_{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$.
- [x] **Dynamic Range** = $20 \log_{10}(2^N) = 6.02\,N$ dB (no 1.76 constant).
- [x] **ENOB** from measured SNR: $ENOB = (SNR_{measured} - 1.76)/6.02$.
- [x] **Anti-aliasing filter** must be placed *before* the S/H (or before MUX in multichannel systems).
- [x] **ZOH reconstruction** produces sinc($f/f_s$) frequency droop — needs smoothing filter.
- [x] ADC types to remember: **Flash** (fast), **SAR** (balanced), **Dual-slope** (precise DC), **Sigma-Delta** (high res).
- [x] Always convert time/frequency to **SI units (seconds, Hz, V)** before plugging into formulas.
- [x] For 12-bit, 10 V ADC: $\Delta \approx 2.44$ mV, $e_{rms} \approx 0.705$ mV, $SNR \approx 74$ dB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition, Design and Need for Data Acquisition Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definition, Design and Need for Data Acquisition Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Data Acquisition System (DAS / DAQ)** is a precisely engineered, computer-based measurement framework that captures, conditions, digitizes, processes, stores, and visualizes real-world physical phenomena (analog signals) by converting them into a digital format that a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) or computer can interpret, analyze, and act upon.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEERT861**, a Data Acquisition System is formally defined as:

> An integrated assembly of sensors, signal conditioning circuits, multiplexing stages, Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC), communication interfaces, and software that systematically samples analog input signals from the physical world and transforms them into meaningful digital data points for industrial monitoring, control, archival, and analytical purposes.

**Core Terminology Breakdown:**

| Term | Standard Definition |
|------|---------------------|
| **Data** | Quantitative or qualitative variables representing physical parameters. |
| **Acquisition** | The systematic process of gathering, sampling, and digitizing. |
| **System** | An organized set of interoperable hardware + software components. |
| **Analog Signal** | A continuous, time-varying signal (e.g., 0–10 V, 4–20 mA). |
| **Digital Signal** | A discrete, quantized binary representation. |
| **Sample Rate** | The number of analog samples acquired per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **Samples per Second (S/s)**. |
| **Resolution** | The smallest detectable change in input signal, typically expressed in **bits (n-bit)**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 4, the student MUST be able to distinguish between a *Data Acquisition System* and a *Data Acquisition Card (DAQ card)*. A DAQ card is a single plug-in board (e.g., PCI-based), whereas a DAS is the entire distributed or rack-mounted measurement ecosystem.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **smart ICU (Intensive Care Unit) in a hospital** monitoring a critical patient. The patient is the **physical process (plant)**. Multiple sensors (ECG, blood pressure, pulse oximeter) act as the **transducers**. The signals are weak and noisy, so the **amplifier + filter (signal conditioning)** cleans and strengthens them. A central **nursing monitor (multiplexer + ADC)** rapidly switches between all the sensors, digitizes the readings, and displays them. The **doctor (controller/PLC)** reviews the digital data and issues commands (e.g., adjusting IV drip rate), which are converted back to physical action via **actuators**.

**In this analogy:**

- The **patient** = Physical process (temperature, pressure, flow, level).
- The **ECG/PPG sensors** = Transducers and sensors.
- The **filtering electronics** = Signal conditioning circuits.
- The **multiplexer** = Time-division switch.
- The **A/D converter** = The core digitizer.
- The **monitor screen** = HMI / SCADA display.
- The **IV pump** = Final control element (actuator).

This is *exactly* how an industrial DAS functions — converting raw physical reality into meaningful digital insight for automated decision-making.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of any DAS:**
> 1. **Acquire** the physical signal faithfully.
> 2. **Convert** it into a robust digital format.
> 3. **Deliver** it to a host (PLC, PC, or cloud) for control/analysis.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

A professionally engineered DAS is benchmarked against three fundamental metrics (per IEEE 1057 and IEC 61131 standards):

1. **Accuracy** — The closeness of the measured value to the true value. Expressed as a **percentage of Full-Scale Reading (%FSR)** or in engineering units.
2. **Precision (Repeatability)** — The degree to which repeated measurements yield the same value under identical conditions.
3. **Resolution** — The smallest incremental change the system can detect, governed by: $$\text{Resolution (V)} = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^n}$$ where $n$ is the number of bits of the ADC.

**Typical industrial standards a KTU student must know:**

- **Analog Input Ranges:** $\pm 10 \text{ V}$, $0\text{–}10 \text{ V}$, $4\text{–}20 \text{ mA}$ (industry standard for current loops).
- **Digital Communication Protocols:** Modbus RTU/TCP, PROFIBUS, EtherNet/IP, RS-232, RS-485.
- **Typical Sampling Rates:** From **$\approx 10 \text{ Hz}$** (slow process monitoring like temperature) up to **$1 \text{ MHz}$** (vibration/acoustic analysis).

> [!TIP]
> A KTU 2024 examiner *always* expects the student to state the **standard industrial current loop range of 4–20 mA** as the preferred signal transmission standard, because it is *live-zero* — meaning a 0 mA reading indicates a broken wire, not a zero process value.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Operational Architecture

A complete Data Acquisition System is architecturally decomposed into **five sequential functional stages**. Each stage is mandatory in the KTU 2024 module outcome CO4 evaluation.

### Stage 1 — Transduction (Sensors)
- The physical phenomenon (temperature, pressure, flow, strain, light intensity, pH, etc.) is converted into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or charge).
- **Why?** Because a computer/PLC cannot directly "feel" temperature or "see" pressure — it only understands electrical variables.
- **Examples:** Thermocouple (Seebeck effect), RTD (resistance change), Load cell (Wheatstone bridge), LVDT (inductance change).

### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning
- The raw transducer output is almost always **too weak, noisy, non-linear, or contaminated with offset**.
- Conditioning includes:
  - **Amplification** (using Instrumentation Amplifiers like INA128).
  - **Filtering** (Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter).
  - **Isolation** (galvanic isolation using optocouplers/transformers for safety and noise rejection).
  - **Linearization** (applying polynomial or lookup-table correction for thermocouples).
  - **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** for thermocouples.
- **Why?** A thermocouple outputs only **microvolts ($\mu$V) per °C** — without amplification, the ADC would see only noise.

### Stage 3 — Multiplexing (MUX)
- A single, expensive high-precision ADC is shared among **N input channels** using an analog multiplexer (e.g., CD4051, ADG708).
- The MUX sequentially routes one channel at a time to the ADC.
- **How?** Through time-division switching governed by the controller's logic.
- **Why?** To reduce cost, hardware complexity, and calibration overhead. A 16:1 MUX lets one ADC serve 16 sensors.

### Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
- The conditioned analog signal is quantized into discrete binary codes.
- Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADCs are most common in industrial PLCs (e.g., 12-bit, 16-bit).
- Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\text{-}\Delta$) ADCs are used in precision weighing and strain-gauge systems (24-bit).
- The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** governs the minimum sample rate.

### Stage 5 — Host Processing, Display & Storage
- The digital codes are transmitted to a PLC CPU, PC, or embedded controller.
- Software interprets the data: scaling, engineering-unit conversion (EU), alarming, trending, and data logging.
- Output: HMI screens, SCADA dashboards, SQL database, or cloud upload (MQTT/OPC-UA in Industry 4.0).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — every formula here is a high-probability KTU Part A or Part B question stem.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **ADC Resolution (Voltage)** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^n}$ | Volts; $n$ = bits |
| 2 | **Quantization Error (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Maximum ±Q/2 error |
| 3 | **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 4 | **Nyquist Minimum Sample Rate** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 5 | **Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff** | $f_c \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Strictly less than Nyquist freq. |
| 6 | **Conversion Time (SAR ADC)** | $T_{\text{conv}} = n \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | Seconds; $T_{\text{clk}}$ = clock period |
| 7 | **Total Acquisition Time (Multiplexed)** | $T_{\text{acq}} = N \cdot T_{\text{conv}} + T_{\text{settling}}$ | N = number of channels |
| 8 | **Throughput Rate** | $\text{THR} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{acq}}}$ | Samples/second |
| 9 | **Current-Loop to Voltage Conversion** | $V_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{loop}} \times R_{\text{shunt}}$ | $R_{\text{shunt}}$ typically 250 Ω |
| 10 | **Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)** | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{measured}} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Real-world ADC quality metric |
| 11 | **Thermocouple Sensitivity (K-type)** | $S \approx 41 \ \mu\text{V}/°\text{C}$ | Reference: NIST tables |
| 12 | **CMRR of Instrumentation Amp** | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | $A_d$ = differential, $A_c$ = common-mode gain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in any LaTeX table — **never use** the vertical pipe `|` symbol, as it will break the markdown table.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

A DAS is the *sensory nervous system* of modern industry. Its utility spans:

- **Power Plants:** Monitoring boiler temperature (°C), steam pressure (bar), turbine RPM, and emissions (ppm NOx).
- **Automotive Test Benches:** Capturing engine vibration (kHz range) and exhaust gas composition.
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Ensuring cleanroom temperature, humidity, and differential pressure remain within FDA-validated bands.
- **Aerospace:** Flight-test telemetry (thousands of strain-gauge channels at >100 kS/s aggregate).
- **Smart Cities:** Air quality (PM2.5 in $\mu\text{g/m}^3$), noise pollution (dB), and traffic flow.

> [!TIP]
> A KTU examiner loves the phrase **"The DAS is the bridge between the physical (analog) world and the cyber (digital) world of Industry 4.0"** — using this in a 14-mark answer instantly demonstrates CO4 mastery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Resolution and Quantization Error

**Problem Statement:**
A 12-bit ADC is used in a DAS with a unipolar input range of $0$ to $10 \text{ V}$. Calculate the (a) voltage resolution, (b) maximum quantization error, and (c) the digital output code when the analog input is $V_{\text{in}} = 3.725 \text{ V}$.

**Given:**

- Number of bits, $n = 12$
- Full-Scale Range, $V_{\text{FSR}} = 10 \text{ V} - 0 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Resolution Formula**

We know that a unipolar $n$-bit ADC partitions its full-scale range into $2^n$ equal quantum steps. The size of each step is the resolution.

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^n}$$

Substitute the known values into the denominator and numerator:

$$\Delta V = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{12}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Power of 2**

We compute $2^{12}$ explicitly. $2^{10} = 1024$, and $2^{12} = 2^{10} \times 2^2 = 1024 \times 4$.

$$2^{12} = 4096$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Resolution**

Substitute back to obtain the resolution in volts:

$$\Delta V = \frac{10}{4096} \text{ V}$$

Perform the long division to get the decimal form:

$$\Delta V = 0.00244140625 \text{ V} \approx 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Quantization Error**

By the definition of quantization, the maximum deviation of the true analog signal from the nearest digital code is **half the step size**:

$$Q_{\text{max}} = \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \frac{0.00244140625}{2} = 0.001220703 \text{ V} \approx 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Digital Output Code for $V_{\text{in}} = 3.725 \text{ V}$**

The digital code $D$ is found by dividing the input voltage by the resolution:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor$$

Substituting the values:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{3.725}{0.00244140625} \right\rfloor$$

Let us evaluate the numerator against the resolution step by step:

$$D = \left\lfloor 3.725 \times 4096 / 10 \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \left\lfloor 15258.176 / 10 \right\rfloor$$

Wait — recalculating cleanly:

$$3.725 \div 0.00244140625 = 1525.76$$

We must express this as an integer (rounding down or to nearest integer — for unipolar ADCs, the code is integer-rounding):

$$D_{\text{rounded}} = 1526$$

Convert this decimal code to hexadecimal for the binary representation. A 12-bit code in hex spans from $000_{\text{H}}$ to $\text{FFF}_{\text{H}}$.

$$1526 \div 16 = 95 \text{ remainder } 6$$
$$95 \div 16 = 5 \text{ remainder } 15 \; (F_H)$$
$$5 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 5$$

Therefore, $D = \mathbf{5F6_H}$ (binary: `0101 1111 0110`).

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Resolution $\Delta V$ | $2.44 \text{ mV}$ |
| Quantization Error $Q_{\text{max}}$ | $\pm 1.22 \text{ mV}$ |
| Digital Code for $3.725 \text{ V}$ | $5\text{F6}_{\text{H}}$ (1526 decimal) |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Sampling Theorem for Alias-Free Acquisition

**Problem Statement:**
A vibration sensor outputs a sinusoidal signal with a maximum frequency component of $f_{\text{max}} = 5 \text{ kHz}$. The DAS uses an anti-aliasing filter with a cutoff $f_c$ and samples at $f_s$. Determine the *minimum* sample rate and the *maximum allowable* filter cutoff to guarantee alias-free reconstruction.

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**

The Shannon Sampling Theorem states that to reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, the sample rate must be **strictly greater than twice the highest frequency component**:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$$

Substitute $f_{\text{max}} = 5000 \text{ Hz}$:

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Anti-Aliasing Filter Constraint**

The Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter (typically a 4th-order Butterworth) must attenuate all frequencies *above* $f_s / 2$ by at least the ADC's resolution margin (≥ 80 dB). The cutoff frequency $f_c$ is chosen slightly **below** $f_s / 2$ to provide a transition band:

$$f_c \leq \frac{f_s}{2}$$

A safe industrial design margin is $f_c = 0.4 \cdot f_s$:

$$f_c \leq 0.4 \times 10{,}000 = 4 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Filter Slope**

The roll-off needed beyond 4 kHz to suppress the 5 kHz signal is:

$$\text{Attenuation} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{4}\right) \approx 1.94 \text{ dB per octave (1st order)}$$

For an $N$-th order Butterworth filter, the attenuation at frequency ratio $r = f / f_c$ is:

$$A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^{2N}\right]$$

For $f = 5 \text{ kHz}$, $f_c = 4 \text{ kHz}$, $N = 4$:

$$A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + (1.25)^8\right] = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + 5.96\right] \approx 8.1 \text{ dB}$$

This is insufficient for a 12-bit system; hence an 8th-order filter is mandatory.

**Final Design Specification:**

| Parameter | Minimum Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Sample Rate $f_s$ | $10 \text{ kHz}$ |
| Anti-Alias Cutoff $f_c$ | $\leq 4 \text{ kHz}$ |
| Filter Order (≥ 80 dB rejection) | $\geq 8$ (Butterworth) |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

**Given:** ADC bits $n = 16$.

$$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 1.76 $$

$$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 $$

$$ \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB} $$

This matches the well-known **theoretical 98 dB ceiling for 16-bit audio CD-quality ADCs**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DAS vs. SCADA vs. PLC

| Feature | **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** | **SCADA** | **PLC** |
|---------|-----------------------------------|-----------|---------|
| Primary Function | Data collection, logging, analysis | Supervisory monitoring & control | Real-time discrete control |
| Sampling Speed | High (kHz to MHz) | Low–Medium (1–100 Hz) | Medium (scan time ms) |
| Data Storage | Large (GB–TB databases) | Tag-based historian | Ladder registers (KB) |
| Control Loop | Open or advisory | Open/closed supervisory | Closed-loop deterministic |
| Operator Interface | Engineering software (LabVIEW) | HMI / MMI screens | Pushbuttons / Lamps |
| Typical Use | R&D labs, test rigs | Power grid, water utilities | Discrete manufacturing |

> [!NOTE]
> A DAS *complements* a SCADA/PLC; it does not *replace* them. KTU 2024 module outcomes emphasize this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalized Single-Channel DAS Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical signal-flow chain for a single channel of a Data Acquisition System. Each block represents a mandatory functional stage in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHY["PHYSICAL PROCESS\nTemperature, Pressure, Flow"]
    SEN["TRANSDUCER / SENSOR\nThermocouple, RTD, Load Cell"]
    SC["SIGNAL CONDITIONING\nAmplify, Filter, Isolate, Linearize"]
    MUX["ANALOG MULTIPLEXER\nMUX N to 1"]
    ADC["ADC\nAnalog to Digital Converter"]
    CPU["HOST CONTROLLER\nPLC CPU / PC / DSP"]
    HMI["HMI / SCADA / DATABASE\nDisplay, Log, Alarm"]
    ACT["ACTUATOR\nValve, Motor, Heater"]

    PHY --> SEN
    SEN --> SC
    SC --> MUX
    MUX --> ADC
    ADC --> CPU
    CPU --> HMI
    CPU --> ACT
    ACT -.Feedback.-> PHY
```

## 4.2 Multi-Channel Distributed DAS Architecture

For large industrial plants (e.g., a 500-MW thermal power station), a single-rack DAS is insufficient. A **distributed DAS** uses remote I/O nodes communicating over fieldbus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FieldLayer["FIELD LAYER — Sensors and Actuators"]
        T1["T1 Boiler Temp"]
        T2["T2 Feedwater Temp"]
        P1["P1 Steam Pressure"]
        F1["F1 Coal Flow"]
        V1["V1 Control Valve"]
    end

    subgraph RemoteIO["REMOTE I/O — Signal Conditioning and MUX"]
        RIO1["RIO Node 1\n8-Channel Analog Input"]
        RIO2["RIO Node 2\n8-Channel Analog Input"]
        RIO3["RIO Node 3\nDigital I/O and Output"]
    end

    subgraph ControlLayer["CONTROL LAYER — DAS and PLC"]
        PLC["MASTER PLC CPU\nwith Embedded ADC"]
        DAQ["DAQ SERVER\nIndustrial PC"]
    end

    subgraph Supervisory["SUPERVISORY LAYER — HMI and Historian"]
        HMI1["HMI Operator Station 1"]
        HMI2["HMI Operator Station 2"]
        HIST["PROCESS HISTORIAN\nSQL Database"]
        CLOUD["CLOUD DASHBOARD\nIndustry 4.0 OPC UA"]
    end

    T1 --> RIO1
    T2 --> RIO1
    P1 --> RIO2
    F1 --> RIO2
    V1 --> RIO3

    RIO1 -->|Modbus TCP| PLC
    RIO2 -->|Modbus TCP| PLC
    RIO3 -->|Profibus DP| PLC

    PLC <-->|OPC UA / Ethernet| DAQ
    DAQ --> HIST
    DAQ --> HMI1
    DAQ --> HMI2
    DAQ --> CLOUD
```

## 4.3 Design Decision Tree for a New DAS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Define Measurement Need"] --> B["Identify Physical Variables"]
    B --> C["What is the Maximum Frequency fmax"]
    C --> D{"fmax greater than 1 kHz"}
    D -- YES --> E["Choose Sigma-Delta or Flash ADC\nSample Rate greater than 200 kS per s"]
    D -- NO --> F["Choose SAR ADC\nSample Rate greater than 100 S per s"]
    E --> G["Determine Required Resolution"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Accuracy better than 0.1 percent"}
    H -- YES --> I["Select 16 bit or 18 bit ADC"]
    H -- NO --> J["12 bit ADC is sufficient"]
    I --> K["Add Anti Aliasing Filter of Order 8"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Decide Channel Count and Multiplexer"]
    L --> M["Choose Communication Protocol\nModbus PROFIBUS EtherNet IP"]
    M --> N["Design Signal Conditioning\nINA128 with Isolation"]
    N --> O["END: Final BOM and Calibration Plan"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels use raw uppercase text and **no bold/italic markdown**, ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and Obsidian.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a Data Acquisition System. List its four major functional blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is a computer-based, integrated measurement framework that converts physical (analog) signals into digital data for processing, storage, and display.

**The four major functional blocks are:**

1. **Transducers / Sensors** — convert physical variables to electrical signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — amplifies, filters, isolates, and linearizes the raw signal.
3. **Multiplexer + ADC** — sequentially routes channels and converts analog voltage to digital codes.
4. **Host Computer / PLC** — performs scaling, engineering-unit conversion, display (HMI), alarming, and data logging.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why is a 4–20 mA current loop preferred over 0–10 V voltage transmission in industrial DAS?**

**Model Answer:**

The 4–20 mA current loop is preferred for four key reasons:

1. **Live-Zero (Fault Detection):** A reading of 0 mA unambiguously indicates a **broken wire or sensor failure**, not a true zero process value. In contrast, 0 V could mean either zero or fault.
2. **Noise Immunity:** Current signals are immune to voltage drops across long cable runs and induced EMI.
3. **Long-Distance Transmission:** A 4–20 mA signal can travel up to **1 km** without degradation, whereas 0–10 V degrades beyond ~30 m.
4. **Single-Wire Simplicity:** Only two wires carry both power and signal for loop-powered transmitters.

**Example Conversion:** A 250 Ω shunt resistor converts 4–20 mA → 1–5 V for ADC input.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Choice between two questions**, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either **Q1 (a+b)** or **Q2 (a+b)** must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the architecture of a generalized Data Acquisition System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the anti-aliasing filter in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (must be drawn in the answer sheet):**

```
[Physical Variable] → [Sensor] → [Signal Conditioner] → [MUX] → [Sample & Hold] → [ADC] → [PC/PLC] → [Display/Storage]
```

**1. Transducers:** Convert physical variables (temperature, pressure, flow) into electrical signals (mV, mA, V). Example: K-type thermocouple produces $\approx 41\ \mu\text{V/°C}$.

**2. Signal Conditioning:** Performs four operations:
- **Amplification** using an Instrumentation Amplifier (e.g., INA128, gain 100).
- **Filtering** to remove 50/60 Hz line noise.
- **Isolation** to break ground loops.
- **Linearization** for non-linear sensors.

**3. Multiplexer (MUX):** A 16:1 analog switch (e.g., CD4051) shares one ADC among many channels, drastically reducing cost.

**4. Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Freezes the analog signal during conversion to avoid aperture error. A hold capacitor $C_H$ is buffered by a unity-gain amplifier.

**5. ADC:** Performs the quantization. A 12-bit SAR ADC is standard.

**6. Host System:** Performs scaling, engineering-unit conversion, HMI display, alarming, and trending.

**Role of the Anti-Aliasing Filter:**

The anti-aliasing filter is a **low-pass analog filter placed BEFORE the ADC**. Its role is critical:

- **Problem it solves:** According to the Nyquist theorem, any frequency component $f > f_s/2$ in the input will "fold back" (alias) into the baseband as a false, lower frequency. This is irreversible — once aliased, the data is corrupt.
- **Solution:** A steep roll-off LPF (Butterworth, ≥ 4th order) attenuates all frequencies above $f_c \leq f_s/2$ by at least 60–80 dB.
- **Trade-off:** A very sharp filter (high order) introduces phase distortion; a Butterworth offers a maximally-flat magnitude response.
- **Practical example:** For $f_s = 10 \text{ kHz}$ and $f_c = 4 \text{ kHz}$, an 8th-order Butterworth rejects the 5 kHz component by >80 dB.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A 16-bit ADC with a $\pm 10 \text{ V}$ input range is used in a DAS. Calculate (i) the resolution in mV, (ii) the maximum quantization error, and (iii) the SQNR in dB.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 16$, $V_{\text{FSR}} = 20 \text{ V}$ (from $-10$ V to $+10$ V).

**(i) Resolution:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^n} = \frac{20}{2^{16}} = \frac{20}{65536}$$

$$\Delta V = 3.0517 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.305 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$Q_{\text{max}} = \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \frac{0.305}{2} = 0.1526 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) SQNR in dB:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76$$

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Computed Value |
|----------|----------------|
| Resolution $\Delta V$ | $0.305 \text{ mV}$ |
| Max Quantization Error | $\pm 0.1526 \text{ mV}$ |
| SQNR | $98.08 \text{ dB}$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the design considerations for a Data Acquisition System for a thermal power plant boiler. What sensors, signal conditioning, and sample rate are required?**

**Model Solution:**

**Design Objective:** Monitor boiler drum pressure (up to 200 bar), steam temperature (up to 540 °C), feed-water flow, and flue-gas oxygen (0–10% O₂).

**Step 1 — Sensor Selection:**

| Variable | Sensor | Output |
|----------|--------|--------|
| Drum Pressure | Strain-gauge pressure transducer (4-arm Wheatstone bridge) | 4–20 mA |
| Steam Temperature | K-type thermocouple in thermowell | ~22 mV @ 540 °C |
| Feed-water Flow | Magnetic flow meter | 4–20 mA |
| Flue-gas O₂ | Zirconia oxygen probe | 0–10 V |

**Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:**

- **Pressure transducer** outputs 4–20 mA → 250 Ω precision shunt → 1–5 V → INA128 amplifier (gain = 2) → 2–10 V to ADC.
- **Thermocouple** → INA128 with gain 250 → 0–5 V → CJC compensation circuit → software linearization (NIST polynomial).
- **Isolation:** Each channel uses an AD202 isolation amplifier for 1500 V galvanic isolation.
- **Filtering:** 4th-order Butterworth LPF at 10 Hz cutoff (boiler dynamics are slow).

**Step 3 — Sample Rate:**

Boiler dynamics are slow (thermal time constants in minutes). A sample rate of **1–10 Hz per channel** is sufficient. With 64 channels multiplexed, the aggregate throughput is:

$$f_{s,\text{total}} = 64 \times 10 = 640 \text{ S/s}$$

This is well within the capability of a 12-bit SAR ADC (100 kS/s).

**Step 4 — Communication and Archival:**

- **Fieldbus:** Modbus RTU over RS-485 (cost-effective) or PROFIBUS PA (for hazardous areas).
- **Historian:** OSIsoft PI or Wonderware InSQL for time-series storage with compression.
- **Compliance:** IEC 61508 SIL-2 for safety instrumentation.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the need for a Data Acquisition System in modern industry. Discuss any five applications.**

**Model Solution:**

A DAS is needed because modern industry demands **real-time visibility, traceability, and data-driven decision making** that is impossible to achieve with manual gauges and chart recorders.

**Five Critical Industrial Applications:**

1. **Power Generation (Energy Sector):**
   Continuous monitoring of boiler parameters, turbine vibrations, and grid frequency. Enables predictive maintenance, reducing unplanned downtime by 30–40%. Reference: IEEE C37.118 for phasor measurement units.

2. **Pharmaceutical Batch Recording (FDA 21 CFR Part 11):**
   Validated DAS records temperature, humidity, pH, and dissolved oxygen for every batch. Electronic signatures and audit trails ensure regulatory compliance.

3. **Automotive Crash Testing:**
   High-speed DAQ (≥ 1 MS/s) captures accelerometer and load-cell data at 10 kHz during a 100 ms crash event. Data drives occupant-safety algorithm validation.

4. **Oil & Gas Pipeline Monitoring:**
   Distributed DAS (using fiber-optic Rayleigh backscattering) detects third-party intrusion, leaks, and ground movement over hundreds of kilometers in real time.

5. **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):**
   Edge-DAS nodes feed machine-learning models for predictive quality control, OEE calculation, and digital-twin synchronization.

**Summary of NEED:**

- **Quality Assurance:** Traceable, calibration-friendly measurements.
- **Safety:** Early detection of out-of-limit conditions.
- **Efficiency:** Data-driven process optimization (Six Sigma, lean).
- **Compliance:** ISO 9001, FDA, environmental regulations.
- **Profitability:** Reduced scrap, energy, and warranty costs.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DAS with DAQ Card:** A DAQ card is ONE printed-circuit board; a DAS is the *entire* system (sensors → conditioning → MUX → ADC → host). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Always write $\text{V}$, $\text{mA}$, $\text{Hz}$, $\text{dB}$, or $\text{°C}$ explicitly. A numeric answer without units is awarded **zero** in Part A.
>
> 3. **Wrong Nyquist Inequality Direction:** Some students write $f_s < 2 f_{\text{max}}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct inequality is $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$, and the filter cutoff is $f_c \leq f_s / 2$.
>
> 4. **Skipping Block Diagram:** In 7-mark Part A answers, examiners allocate **at least 2 marks** for a clean, labeled block diagram. A text-only answer is penalized.
>
> 5. **Formula Substitution Without Evaluation:** Writing $\Delta V = V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^n$ is worth only partial credit. You must substitute and compute the **final numerical value**.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Bits and Levels:** A 12-bit ADC has $2^{12} = 4096$ *levels*, NOT 12 levels. This is a common 1-mark trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Definitions to Memorize
- **DAS** = Sensors + Signal Conditioning + MUX + ADC + Host.
- **DAQ Card** = A single plug-in PCB containing the MUX, S/H, and ADC stages only.
- **Live-Zero Standard** = 4 mA, not 0 mA.
- **Resolution** = $V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^n$.
- **Quantization Error** = ±½ LSB.

### ✅ Essential Formulas (must be memorized verbatim)
- $\Delta V = V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^n$
- $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 1.76$
- $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ (Nyquist)
- $Q_{\text{max}} = V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^{n+1}$

### ✅ Key Numbers to Remember
- $2^{10} = 1024$, $2^{12} = 4096$, $2^{16} = 65{,}536$.
- 16-bit SQNR ≈ 98 dB.
- K-type thermocouple ≈ 41 $\mu\text{V/°C}$.
- 4–20 mA through 250 Ω → 1–5 V.

### ✅ Block Diagram Order (memorize left → right)
**Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → MUX → S/H → ADC → CPU → HMI/Storage**

### ✅ Common KTU-Asked Comparisons
- **DAS vs. PLC** (data focus vs. control focus).
- **DAS vs. SCADA** (acquisition vs. supervision).
- **SAR vs. Sigma-Delta ADC** (speed vs. resolution).
- **Voltage vs. Current loop transmission** (short vs. long distance).

### ✅ Standards and Protocols (appear in 14-mark answers)
- **IEEE 1057** — ADC testing standard.
- **IEC 61131-3** — PLC programming languages.
- **ISO 9001 / 21 CFR Part 11** — Quality and pharma compliance.
- **Modbus, PROFIBUS, EtherNet/IP** — Industrial communication protocols.

### ✅ High-Yield Keywords for Exam Wording
Use these phrases in 14-mark answers to score above 90%:
- "The DAS acts as the **sensory nervous system** of the plant."
- "Anti-aliasing filter is a **non-negotiable** analog safeguard."
- "The 4–20 mA standard provides **live-zero fault detection**."
- "Nyquist sampling guarantees **alias-free reconstruction**."

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"SAFMAH"** → **S**ensor → **A**mplify → **F**ilter → **M**ultiplex → **A**DC → **H**ost. This single word covers the entire DAS chain in the correct order.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing ADC and DAC with Microprocessor/Multiplexer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interfacing ADC and DAC with Microprocessor/Multiplexer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** An electronic integrated circuit that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude **analog signal** (typically a voltage or current) into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude **digital code** (binary word) proportional to the input magnitude. The conversion involves three sub-processes: **Sampling**, **Holding**, **Quantization**, and **Encoding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC):** An electronic device that performs the inverse operation of an ADC. It accepts an **n-bit binary digital word** at its input and produces an equivalent **analog voltage or current** at its output whose magnitude is proportional to the digital code, governed by the reference voltage $V_{REF}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analog Multiplexer (MUX):** A semiconductor switching device that accepts **multiple analog input channels** (typically 4, 8, or 16) and routes **one selected channel** to a single output line, controlled by a binary address word. In a Data Acquisition System (DAS), it acts as a **channel expander** for a single ADC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Acquisition System (DAS):** A measurement system comprising sensors, signal conditioning, multiplexer, sample-and-hold amplifier, ADC, and a microprocessor/controller that systematically digitizes multiple real-world analog signals for storage, processing, and display.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **multi-bed hospital ICU** with hundreds of patients, each needing their temperature, blood pressure, and pulse checked.

- The **patients** are the **analog input channels** (temperature sensors, pressure transducers, flow meters).
- The **duty nurse** is the **Analog Multiplexer** — she cannot check all patients simultaneously, so she walks from bed-to-bed selecting one patient at a time based on a schedule.
- The **medical diagnostic machine** (blood analyzer) is the **ADC** — it converts the analog reading (e.g., 98.6 °F) into a digital display.
- The **doctor** is the **microprocessor** — it interprets the digital result, decides what to do, and stores it in the patient file.
- The **DAC** is the reverse process — when the doctor writes a prescription on the computer, the printer **converts digital text back into analog ink marks** on paper.

In short: **Sensor → Multiplexer (selection) → Sample & Hold → ADC (digitize) → Microprocessor → DAC (reconstruct)**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Resolution** of an $n$-bit ADC/DAC = **1 LSB** = $V_{REF}/2^{n}$
- **Quantization Error** = **± ½ LSB** (unavoidable rounding noise)
- **Conversion Time ($t_{conv}$)** for successive approximation ADC typically lies in the range **1 µs to 100 µs**
- **Settling Time** of a DAC (to reach ±½ LSB of final value) typically between **100 ns to 1 µs**
- **Standard Reference Voltage** $V_{REF} = \mathbf{+5.0 \, V}$ or **±10 V** for industrial systems
- **Typical Multiplexer ON-resistance ($R_{ON}$)** = **100 Ω to 500 Ω** (e.g., CD4051: ~125 Ω)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEERT861 Module 4):** The student is expected to understand the **hardware-level signal exchange** between a microprocessor bus (data, address, control) and converter chips, the role of **address decoding**, **handshaking using status flags (INTR, EOC, BUSY)**, and the use of **analog multiplexers** to build cost-effective multi-channel DAS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Steps — ADC Interfacing with Microprocessor

The interfacing of an ADC (e.g., ADC0808/0809, AD574A) with a microprocessor (8085/8086) follows a **systematic handshake protocol**:

1. **Address Decoding:** The microprocessor places the address of the ADC on the address bus. A decoder (e.g., 74LS138 — 3-to-8 line decoder) activates the chip select ($\overline{CS}$) pin of the ADC corresponding to that address.
2. **Channel Selection (for multi-channel ADCs):** The lower bits of the address bus (ADDa, ADDb, ADDc for ADC0808) select one of 8 analog input channels ($IN_0$ to $IN_7$).
3. **Start of Conversion (SOC):** The microprocessor issues a **WRITE** pulse (active-low) to the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** and **START** pins of the ADC, latching the channel address and initiating the conversion.
4. **Wait State / Polling:** The microprocessor either enters a wait loop (polling the EOC pin) or relies on an **interrupt-driven** mechanism via the **INTR (End-of-Conversion)** output pin of the ADC.
5. **Read Operation:** Once EOC goes HIGH, the microprocessor issues a **READ** pulse (active-low $\overline{RD}$), and the digitized 8-bit word appears on the data bus.
6. **Storage & Processing:** The data is stored in memory or processed by the CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why address decoding?** Because the microprocessor's address bus must uniquely identify *one* peripheral among many. Without decoding, **bus contention** (two devices driving the bus simultaneously) would damage the hardware.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps — DAC Interfacing with Microprocessor

1. The microprocessor loads the **8-bit digital code** into an accumulator or latches it onto the data bus.
2. A **decoder** decodes the I/O address and generates a chip select for the DAC (e.g., DAC0808).
3. The chip select and a **WRITE** signal together enable the DAC's internal latch, accepting the digital input.
4. The DAC produces an **analog output current** $I_{out}$ proportional to the digital word.
5. An external **operational amplifier (current-to-voltage converter)** converts $I_{out}$ into a usable analog voltage $V_{out}$.
6. The **settling time** of the op-amp + DAC combination determines how fast $V_{out}$ stabilizes.

## 2.3 Role of the Analog Multiplexer (MUX) in DAS

A single expensive ADC can digitize many analog channels using an analog MUX (e.g., **CD4051, CD4052, ADG708**). Operation:

- The MUX has **N analog inputs** ($X_0, X_1, \dots, X_{N-1}$) and **1 analog output** (common terminal).
- An **n-bit digital address** ($n = \log_2 N$) selects which input is connected to the output via internal CMOS analog switches.
- The **throughput rate** of the DAS must respect the **MUX switching time + ADC conversion time**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units / Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|
| ADC Resolution (LSB weight) | $\Delta V = V_{REF} \,/\, 2^{n}$ | Volts (V) |
| DAC Output Voltage | $V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^{n}}$ where $D$ is decimal equivalent | Volts (V) |
| Quantization Error | $E_{q} = \pm \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$ | Volts (V) |
| Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \, n + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) |
| Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | $f_{s} \geq 2 \, f_{max}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Multiplexer Channel Count | $N = 2^{n}$ where $n$ = number of address lines | Integer |
| MUX Settling Time | $t_{settle} = t_{MUX} + t_{S\&H} + t_{ADC}$ | Seconds (s) |
| ADC Throughput | $\text{Thr} = 1 \,/\, (t_{conv} + t_{acq})$ | Samples/Second (SPS) |
| Full-Scale Output (DAC) | $V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{2^{n} - 1}{2^{n}}$ | Volts (V) |
| Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Bits |
| Multiplexer ON-resistance | $R_{ON}$ | **100 Ω – 500 Ω** |
| Crosstalk (MUX) | Ratio of unwanted signal to selected signal | dB (typically −80 dB) |
| Differential Nonlinearity (DNL) | $\text{DNL} = \dfrac{V_{step,actual} - V_{step,ideal}}{\text{LSB}}$ | LSB (ideal = 0) |
| Integral Nonlinearity (INL) | $\text{INL} = \dfrac{V_{out,actual} - V_{out,ideal}}{\text{LSB}}$ | LSB (ideal = 0) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic Rule:** "**Voltage reference over 2 to the n**" — always remember that resolution is *one part in $2^n$* of the reference.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Process Control:** Multi-channel DAS monitors temperature, pressure, flow, and level in a chemical plant using a single ADC and an analog MUX.
- **Medical Instrumentation:** Patient monitors use 8-channel MUX + ADC to capture ECG, SpO₂, respiration, blood pressure.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Engine control units digitize crankshaft position, throttle position, oxygen sensor via MUX-ADC architecture.
- **Audio Equipment:** DACs are the heart of CD players, MP3 decoders, and digital amplifiers.
- **PLC Analog I/O Modules:** Directly use ADC + MUX + DAC architecture to interface with field sensors and actuators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Using a MUX with a single high-precision ADC is **cheaper and more accurate** than using one ADC per channel, because matching multiple ADCs is difficult. This is called the **"time-multiplexed single-ADC"** architecture and is the industry standard for PLCs and SCADA systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DAC Output Voltage for an 8-bit DAC0808

**Given:**
- DAC: DAC0808, $n = 8$ bits
- Reference Voltage: $V_{REF} = +5.0 \, \text{V}$
- Digital Input Code (binary): $D_{binary} = 11001010$
- Op-amp feedback resistor $R_f = 5 \, \text{k}\Omega$ (standard for DAC0808 demo circuit)

**Find:** Output analog voltage $V_{out}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert the binary digital code $D_{binary}$ to its decimal equivalent $D$.

$$D = (1 \times 2^{7}) + (1 \times 2^{6}) + (0 \times 2^{5}) + (0 \times 2^{4}) + (1 \times 2^{3}) + (0 \times 2^{2}) + (1 \times 2^{1}) + (0 \times 2^{0})$$

Computing each term:

$$D = 128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 0$$

$$D = 202 \quad (\text{in decimal})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the standard DAC output voltage formula:

$$V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{D}{2^{n}}$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{202}{2^{8}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator $2^{n}$:

$$2^{8} = 256$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{202}{256} = 0.7890625$$

**Step 5:** Compute final output voltage:

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \times 0.7890625$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 3.945 \, \text{V}}$$

**Step 6:** Cross-verify using 1 LSB weight:

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5.0}{256} = 0.01953 \, \text{V/LSB}$$

$$V_{out} = D \times \text{LSB} = 202 \times 0.01953 = 3.945 \, \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resolution and Quantization Error of a 12-bit ADC

**Given:** ADC with $n = 12$ bits, $V_{REF} = +10.0 \, \text{V}$.

**Find:** (a) Resolution in Volts (b) Quantization error (c) SQNR

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute total number of discrete levels:

$$L = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

**Step 2:** Compute Resolution (1 LSB):

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10.0}{4096}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 2.441 \, \text{mV}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Quantization Error:

$$E_q = \pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB} = \pm \frac{1}{2} \times 2.441 \, \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_q = \pm 1.221 \, \text{mV}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio):

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76$$

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR} = 74.00 \, \text{dB}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{REF}=10$ V can distinguish voltage changes as small as **2.441 mV**, with a worst-case quantization noise of **±1.221 mV**. This is more than sufficient for industrial sensor signals (4–20 mA loops, 0–10 V transducer outputs).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Sampling Frequency for a Band-limited Signal

**Given:** An analog signal has a maximum frequency component $f_{max} = 4 \, \text{kHz}$ (e.g., speech signal in telephony).

**Find:** Minimum sampling frequency $f_s$ (Nyquist rate).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 4 \, \text{kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{s,min} = 8 \, \text{kHz}}$$

**Step 3:** Practical engineering note: In real systems, we use a **guard band** and oversample at $f_s = 2.5 \text{ to } 5$ times $f_{max}$ to allow for realizable **anti-aliasing filters** (which cannot have infinite roll-off). So a typical telephony ADC samples at **8 kHz to 44.1 kHz**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Multi-Channel DAS Throughput Calculation

**Given:**
- 8-channel analog multiplexer (CD4051)
- MUX switching time: $t_{MUX} = 1 \, \mu\text{s}$
- Sample-and-Hold acquisition time: $t_{S\&H} = 2 \, \mu\text{s}$
- ADC conversion time: $t_{ADC} = 10 \, \mu\text{s}$

**Find:** Maximum total throughput in samples/second.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Total time per single conversion:

$$t_{total} = t_{MUX} + t_{S\&H} + t_{ADC}$$

$$t_{total} = 1 + 2 + 10$$

$$t_{total} = 13 \, \mu\text{s per channel}$$

**Step 2:** Sampling rate per channel:

$$f_{channel} = \frac{1}{t_{total}} = \frac{1}{13 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{channel} \approx 76.92 \, \text{kSPS (kilosamples/sec)}}$$

**Step 3:** Since the MUX cycles through 8 channels sequentially, the **aggregate system throughput** is:

$$f_{agg} = \frac{f_{channel}}{8} = \frac{76923}{8}$$

$$\boxed{f_{agg} \approx 9615 \, \text{SPS}}$$

This means each of the 8 channels is sampled at approximately **9.6 kHz** while sharing a single ADC.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Address Decoder Truth Table (3-to-8 Decoder for ADC0808)

For ADC0808, the address lines ADDa, ADDb, ADDc select the analog input channel. KTU students must master this:

| **ADDa (A0)** | **ADDb (A1)** | **ADDc (A2)** | **Selected Channel** | **I/O Address (Hex)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | $IN_0$ | `00H` |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | $IN_1$ | `01H` |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | $IN_2$ | `02H` |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | $IN_3$ | `03H` |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | $IN_4$ | `04H` |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | $IN_5$ | `05H` |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | $IN_6$ | `06H` |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | $IN_7$ | `07H` |

The higher-order address bits are fed to a 74LS138 decoder whose output $\overline{Y_n}$ provides the chip-select signal $\overline{CS}$ to the ADC.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sample ADC Interfacing Assembly Code (8085)

```assembly
; Read 8-bit data from ADC0808 at port address 08H
; Store result in memory location 2000H

        MVI  A, 00H       ; Channel select = IN_0
        OUT  08H          ; Apply ALE+START pulse to ADC
WAIT:   IN   09H          ; Read EOC status from status port
        ANI  01H          ; Mask all bits except D0 (EOC)
        JZ   WAIT         ; If EOC = 0, keep waiting
        IN   08H          ; EOC = 1, read digital data
        STA  2000H        ; Store in memory
        HLT               ; Stop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In modern systems, the wait loop is replaced by an **interrupt service routine (ISR)** triggered by the ADC's INTR pin, allowing the CPU to perform other tasks while conversion is in progress.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Multi-Channel Data Acquisition System (DAS)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional signal flow** of an industrial multi-channel DAS, which is the core architecture used in PLC analog input modules:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor 1\nTemperature] --> SC[Signal Conditioner\nAmplify and Filter]
    S2[Sensor 2\nPressure] --> SC
    S3[Sensor 3\nFlow Rate] --> SC
    S4[Sensor 4\nLevel] --> SC
    SC --> MUX[Analog Multiplexer\nCD4051 8 to 1]
    MUX --> SH[Sample and Hold\nAmplifier LF398]
    SH --> ADC[ADC0808\n8 bit Successive\nApproximation]
    ADC --> CPU[Microprocessor\n8085 or 8086]
    CPU --> DAC[DAC0808\n8 bit DAC]
    DAC --> OP[Operational Amplifier\nCurrent to Voltage]
    OP --> ACT[Actuator\nValve or Motor]
    CPU --> DISP[Display\nLCD 16x2]
    CPU --> MEM[Memory\nRAM and ROM]
```

**Signal Flow Explanation:**

- Eight (or more) sensors provide analog signals.
- The **Signal Conditioner** amplifies weak sensor outputs (e.g., thermocouple ~mV) to 0–5 V range.
- The **Analog MUX** selects one channel at a time.
- The **Sample-and-Hold** freezes the analog voltage during the ADC's conversion period, preventing aperture error.
- The **ADC** digitizes the analog voltage into an 8-bit binary code.
- The **Microprocessor** processes, stores, and displays the data.
- The **DAC** generates analog control signals for actuators (e.g., proportional valve position).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — ADC0808 Interfacing with 8085 Microprocessor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU8085[8085 CPU\nData Bus D0 to D7\nAddress Bus A0 to A15]
    DEMUX[74LS138\n3 to 8 Decoder]
    ADC1[ADC0808\nChannel IN0 to IN7]
    LATCH[74LS373\nAddress Latch]
    CTRL[Control Logic\nIOR and IOW]
    ALE[ALE Pin of ADC]
    START[START Pin of ADC]
    EOC[EOC Pin of ADC]
    OE[OE Pin of ADC]

    CPU8085 -->|A0 A1 A2| ADC1
    CPU8085 -->|A3 A4 A5| DEMUX
    CPU8085 -->|A8 to A15| LATCH
    DEMUX -->|CS_bar| ADC1
    CTRL -->|Pulse| ALE
    CTRL -->|Pulse| START
    ADC1 -->|End of Conv| EOC
    EOC -->|Interrupt| CPU8085
    CPU8085 -->|Read Pulse| OE
    ADC1 -->|Digital Output| CPU8085
```

**Key Connection Points:**

- Address lines **A0, A1, A2** of the 8085 → **ADDa, ADDb, ADDc** of ADC0808 (channel select).
- Address lines **A3, A4, A5** → **74LS138 decoder** inputs.
- Decoder output $\overline{Y_0}$ → $\overline{CS}$ (chip select) of ADC0808.
- **IOW** (I/O Write) → **ALE** and **START** pins (single-pulse mode).
- **IOR** (I/O Read) → **OE** (Output Enable) pin of ADC0808.
- **EOC** pin → connected to **RST 7.5** interrupt pin of 8085 (for interrupt-driven mode) OR polled via an I/O port.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — DAC0808 Interfacing with Microprocessor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MPU[Microprocessor\n8085 or 8086] -->|8 bit Data| LATCH1[74LS373\nLatch]
    LATCH1 -->|D0 to D7| DAC1[DAC0808\nDigital to Analog\nConverter]
    VREF[Reference Voltage\nplus 10 V] --> DAC1
    DAC1 -->|IOUT| OPAMP[Op Amp LM741\nCurrent to Voltage\nConverter]
    RFB[Feedback Resistor\nRf = 5 kilo Ohm] --> OPAMP
    OPAMP --> VOUT[Analog Output\n0 to plus 5 V or\nplus 10 V]
    DEC[74LS138\nDecoder] -->|CS_bar| LATCH1
    MPU -->|Address Lines| DEC
```

**Critical Pin Connections:**

- $V_{REF}(+)$ pin of DAC0808 → +10 V reference source.
- $V_{REF}(-)$ pin → Ground.
- $I_{out}$ pin → inverting input of op-amp.
- Op-amp non-inverting input → Ground.
- Feedback resistor $R_f = 5 \, \text{k}\Omega$ between op-amp output and inverting input.
- $\overline{CS}$ and $V_{LC}$ (latch control) → tied together, then to decoder output.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — ADC Handshaking Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: Microprocessor powers on
    Idle --> ChannelSelect: CPU writes channel address to MUX
    ChannelSelect --> WaitSettle: MUX switches, S and H acquires
    WaitSettle --> Conversion: SOC pulse sent to ADC
    Conversion --> WaitEOC: ADC performs successive approximation
    WaitEOC --> ReadData: EOC goes HIGH
    ReadData --> ProcessData: CPU reads 8 bit code from data bus
    ProcessData --> Idle: Store and loop to next channel
    ProcessData --> [*]: All channels scanned
```

**Reading the State Diagram:** Each state transition represents a hardware event (pulse, flag assertion, or bus transaction). Students must explain **why** a Sample-and-Hold is needed between MUX and ADC — because during the ADC's conversion time (10 µs), the MUX output could change, causing **aperture error**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Caution:** For high-frequency or rapidly changing signals, the MUX's switching transient (charge injection) can corrupt the sample. This is mitigated by inserting a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) amplifier** between the MUX and ADC, which holds the analog voltage constant during conversion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the function of an analog multiplexer in a Data Acquisition System. Why is it preferred to use a multiplexer with a single ADC instead of multiple ADCs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An analog multiplexer (MUX) is a semiconductor switching device that selects **one of several analog input channels** and routes it to a **single output line**, controlled by a binary address word. In a Data Acquisition System, the MUX allows a **single ADC** to digitize **multiple analog signals sequentially**, thereby reducing the cost, board space, and calibration complexity. Using one ADC per channel (1 mark) would require matching multiple ADCs (1 mark), which is practically impossible because each ADC has unique offset and gain errors. Hence, a MUX + single high-precision ADC is the **industry-standard architecture** for PLC analog input modules (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "Resolution" of an ADC. For a 10-bit ADC with $V_{REF} = 5.0 \, \text{V}$, calculate the resolution and quantization error.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Resolution is the **smallest change in input analog voltage** that an ADC can detect and represent as a change in the output digital code (1 mark). It is equal to **1 LSB (Least Significant Bit)**.

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.88 \, \text{mV} \quad (1 \, \text{mark})$$

Quantization Error (1 mark):

$$E_q = \pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB} = \pm 2.44 \, \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_q = \pm 2.44 \, \text{mV}}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are of equal difficulty and marks. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of an 8-bit ADC0808 with the 8085 microprocessor. Describe the handshaking signals, address decoding, and write an 8085 assembly language program to read analog data from channel $IN_3$.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Block Diagram and Hardware Interfacing

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.2** above. The 8085 microprocessor is connected to the ADC0808 via:

1. **Address lines $A_0, A_1, A_2$** → ADC's **ADDa, ADDb, ADDc** (channel selection inputs).
2. **Address lines $A_3$ to $A_7$** → 74LS138 decoder to generate the chip select $\overline{CS}$ for the ADC at a unique I/O address (e.g., `08H`).
3. **Data bus $D_0$ to $D_7$** → ADC's output lines (bi-directional).
4. **IOR** signal → ADC's **OE (Output Enable)** pin.
5. **IOW** signal → ADC's **ALE** and **START** pins (tied together).
6. **EOC** pin → connected to 8085's **RST 7.5** interrupt pin (for interrupt-driven I/O) OR polled via a status port.

**Handshaking Signals Explanation (2 marks):**

- **ALE (Address Latch Enable):** Active-high pulse from CPU to latch the channel address into ADC's internal address register.
- **START:** Active-high pulse to initiate analog-to-digital conversion; conversion begins on the **falling edge** of START.
- **EOC (End of Conversion):** Goes HIGH when conversion completes; used to signal the CPU that data is ready.
- **OE (Output Enable):** Active-high signal from CPU to tri-state the digital output onto the data bus.

**Address Decoding (2 marks):**

For ADC at port address `08H`:
- Binary of `08H` = `0000 1000`
- $A_7 A_6 A_5 A_4 A_3 = 00001$ → inputs to 74LS138 with $A=0, B=0, C=0$
- The decoder's output $\overline{Y_1}$ activates $\overline{CS}$ of ADC.
- Lower address bits $A_2 A_1 A_0 = 000$ → initially select channel $IN_0$. To select $IN_3$, the CPU outputs the value `0000 1011` = `0BH` to port `08H`.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Assembly Language Program

```assembly
; Read analog data from ADC0808 channel IN_3
; Store result at memory location 2050H
; Assume ADC data port = 08H, ADC status port = 09H

        MVI   A, 0BH      ; A = 0BH = 0000 1011
                           ; Bits A2 A1 A0 = 011 selects IN3
        OUT   08H         ; Send channel address + START pulse
                           ; (ALE latches IN3, START begins conversion)

WAIT:   IN    09H         ; Read status port (EOC bit = D0)
        ANI   01H         ; Mask all bits except D0
        JZ    WAIT        ; If EOC = 0, loop back and wait

        IN    08H         ; EOC = 1; read digital data from ADC
        STA   2050H       ; Store result in memory
        HLT               ; End of program
```

**Step-by-step Explanation (mark distribution):**

- (2 marks) Initializing accumulator with channel select code `0BH`.
- (2 marks) Sending the OUT pulse to start conversion.
- (2 marks) Polling loop using EOC status flag (WAIT routine).
- (1 mark) Reading and storing the digitized data.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of an 8-bit DAC0808 with the 8086 microprocessor operating in minimum mode. Describe the role of the current-to-voltage converting op-amp. Calculate the analog output voltage for an input digital code of `10110100`.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Block Diagram and Operational Details

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.3**. Key interconnections:

1. 8086 data bus (lower 8 bits, $D_0$ to $D_7$) → DAC0808's digital inputs.
2. Address lines $A_1$ to $A_3$ → 74LS138 decoder; output $\overline{Y_2}$ → DAC's $\overline{CS}$ and $V_{LC}$ (latch control) tied together.
3. **IOR** (8086 read) and **IOW** (8086 write) signals generated by the 8288 bus controller in minimum mode.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

The 8086 writes an 8-bit digital code to the DAC port address (e.g., `0BH`). The decoder activates the DAC's chip select, latching the digital code internally. The DAC converts this code into a proportional **current $I_{out}$** at pin 4, given by:

$$I_{out} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_{14}} \cdot \frac{D}{256}$$

where $D$ is the decimal value of the digital input and $R_{14}$ is the internal reference resistor (typically 5 kΩ).

**Role of Op-Amp (2 marks):**

The DAC0808 produces a **current output**, not a voltage. An external op-amp (e.g., LM741) configured as a **current-to-voltage converter** (transimpedance amplifier) converts $I_{out}$ into $V_{out}$ using:

$$V_{out} = I_{out} \times R_f$$

where $R_f$ is the feedback resistor. This op-amp also **buffers** the DAC output, providing low output impedance to drive loads.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Output Voltage Calculation

**Given:**
- DAC: DAC0808, $n = 8$
- $V_{REF} = +5.0 \, \text{V}$
- Digital input $D_{binary} = 10110100$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert binary to decimal:

$$D = (1 \times 2^{7}) + (0 \times 2^{6}) + (1 \times 2^{5}) + (1 \times 2^{4}) + (0 \times 2^{3}) + (1 \times 2^{2}) + (0 \times 2^{1}) + (0 \times 2^{0})$$

$$D = 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 180$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate 1 LSB weight:

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5.0}{256} = 19.53125 \, \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate analog output voltage:

$$V_{out} = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{D}{2^{n}} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{180}{256}$$

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \times 0.703125$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final result:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 3.516 \, \text{V}}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Verification using LSB:

$$V_{out} = 180 \times 19.53125 \, \text{mV} = 3515.625 \, \text{mV} \approx 3.516 \, \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Resolution with Quantization Error:** Resolution = 1 LSB; Quantization Error = ±½ LSB. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the Sample-and-Hold circuit:** Many students omit S/H in the DAS block diagram. This loses 2 marks because it is essential for high-accuracy systems.
> 3. **Wrong conversion of binary to decimal:** Always show the positional weight calculation explicitly (e.g., $1 \times 2^7 + 0 \times 2^6 + \dots$). Don't skip steps.
> 4. **Wrong formula for SQNR:** The correct formula is $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76 \, \text{dB}$. Some students mistakenly use $6.02n$ alone, losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Not drawing the op-amp with DAC:** DAC0808 produces current output — without the I-to-V op-amp, the circuit is incomplete. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 6. **Confusing 8085 I/O signals with 8086 I/O signals:** In 8085, I/O uses `IN` and `OUT` instructions with 8-bit port address; in 8086, I/O uses `IN` and `OUT` with 8-bit or 16-bit port address, and uses `M/IO` control line. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 7. **Address decoding errors:** Forgetting to show the 74LS138 decoder in the block diagram. Always include it for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ADC** converts analog → digital; **DAC** converts digital → analog.
- ✅ **Resolution** = $V_{REF} / 2^{n}$ (1 LSB); **Quantization Error** = ±½ LSB.
- ✅ **SQNR** = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB; **ENOB** = (SNR − 1.76) / 6.02.
- ✅ **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$.
- ✅ **ADC0808** is an 8-bit ADC with **8 internal analog input channels** (multiplexed internally).
- ✅ **DAC0808** is an 8-bit **current-output** DAC → requires external op-amp.
- ✅ **Analog Multiplexer (e.g., CD4051)** allows **one ADC** to serve **multiple analog channels** → cost-effective DAS.
- ✅ **Address decoding** using **74LS138** is mandatory to avoid bus contention.
- ✅ **Handshaking signals of ADC0808:** ALE, START, EOC, OE.
- ✅ **Throughput of multi-channel DAS:** $f_{agg} = 1 / (N \cdot t_{total})$.
- ✅ **Sample-and-Hold circuit** is required between MUX and ADC to avoid **aperture error**.
- ✅ **Settling time** of DAC = time to reach ±½ LSB of final value.
- ✅ **Full-scale output** of DAC = $V_{REF} \cdot (2^n - 1) / 2^n$.
- ✅ For **8085**, I/O port address is **8-bit** (00H–FFH); for **8086**, it is also 8-bit for IN/OUT, but 16-bit for DX-register based I/O.
- ✅ **Typical industrial architectures**: Multi-channel sensor → Signal conditioner → MUX → S/H → ADC → MPU → DAC → Actuator.
- ✅ **MUX ON-resistance ($R_{ON}$)** must be considered when designing high-accuracy systems — typically compensated by **buffer amplifier** after MUX.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering exam questions, always **(i) draw a labeled block diagram first**, **(ii) write the relevant formula**, **(iii) substitute values step-by-step**, and **(iv) box the final answer**. This structured approach fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplexed Channel Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multiplexed Channel Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Data Acquisition Systems (DAS)** and **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)**, **Multiplexed Channel Operation** refers to the systematic technique of sequentially connecting multiple analog/digital input or output signals to a **single shared processing resource** (such as an Analog-to-Digital Converter, ADC, or a single communication line) through an electronic switching device known as a **Multiplexer (MUX)**. The opposite device, a **Demultiplexer (DEMUX)**, reconstructs the signals back into parallel form at the receiver end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Multiplexing in DAS is fundamentally a **resource optimization strategy** that enables one expensive, high-precision ADC to service N number of low-cost input channels sequentially, thereby drastically reducing the per-channel hardware cost of the acquisition system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **single security guard (the ADC)** standing at the entrance of a large corporate office building with **50 separate rooms (input channels)**. The guard cannot physically be in all 50 rooms at once. Instead, every 5 seconds, the guard walks into the next room, checks the temperature, smoke level, and occupancy, and then moves on. This rotation is **multiplexing** — one resource serving many channels by *time-sharing*. The guard's notebook is the **shared ADC**, and the walk-through sequence is the **scan cycle**.

### 1.3 Physical & Standard Constants Used

- **Standard ADC Resolution:** **8-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit** (typical industrial PLC ADCs are **12-bit** or **16-bit**).
- **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Frequency Lower Bound:** **fs ≥ 2 × fmax**.
- **Typical Industrial Scan Time per Channel:** **1 µs to 100 µs** depending on ADC architecture (Successive Approximation Register, SAR, is the dominant industrial choice).
- **Common Multiplexer ICs:** **CD4051 (8:1)**, **CD4052 (dual 4:1)**, **CD4067 (16:1)**, **ADG726 (dual 16:1)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multiplexing Matters in PLC/DAS:** In a typical process plant, a PLC may monitor **256 or 512 analog inputs** (temperature, pressure, flow, level). Buying 256 separate ADC chips is economically impossible. Multiplexing solves this by sharing one ADC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Channel Selection (Address Latching)**
The PLC CPU or DAS controller writes a binary address word (e.g., `0000 0101` = Channel 5) onto the **selector lines (S0, S1, S2)** of the analog multiplexer IC.

**Step 2: Switching / Settling**
The multiplexer's internal CMOS transmission gates close on the addressed channel within the **break-before-make time (tBBM)**, typically **50 ns to 200 ns**. The signal then propagates through any anti-aliasing filter.

**Step 3: Acquisition / Sampling**
The shared ADC samples the analog voltage. A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) amplifier** may be used at the output of the MUX to *freeze* the signal during the ADC's conversion interval, preventing errors from channel-to-channel switching transients.

**Step 4: Conversion**
The ADC performs quantization. For a 12-bit ADC with a **10 V full-scale reference (Vref)**, the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** size is:

$$\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}$$

**Step 5: Storage and Sequential Repeat**
The digital word is read by the controller, stored in a memory-mapped register, and the address is incremented to the next channel.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}$ | Volts | $n$ = ADC resolution in bits |
| 2 | Total Scan Time for N channels | $T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot t_{\text{ch}}$ | seconds | $t_{\text{ch}}$ = per-channel acquisition+conversion time |
| 3 | Effective Sampling Rate per Channel | $f_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot t_{\text{ch}}}$ | Hz (samples/sec) | Must satisfy Nyquist for each signal |
| 4 | Nyquist Minimum Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$ | Hz | $f_{\text{max}}$ = highest signal frequency component |
| 5 | Aperture Time Uncertainty Error | $\Delta V = \dfrac{dV}{dt} \cdot t_{\text{ap}}$ | Volts | Critical for high-frequency signals |
| 6 | Multiplexer Throughput | $T_{\text{MUX}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{\text{switch}}}$ | channels/sec | Limited by ON-resistance × capacitance RC |
| 7 | Settling Time (1 LSB accuracy) | $t_{\text{settle}} \approx \tau \cdot \ln(2^n)$ | seconds | $\tau = R_{\text{ON}} \cdot C_{\text{load}}$ |
| 8 | Number of Channels Decodable | $N = 2^m$ | dimensionless | $m$ = number of selector lines |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiplexed channel operation is the **backbone of every SCADA, DCS, and PLC rack** in the world. From the **Boeing 777 aircraft's Integrated Modular Avionics (IMA)** to a **Kerala State Dairy Development milk collection PLC** that monitors 40 chillers simultaneously, multiplexing makes large-scale data acquisition **economically viable**. Without it, the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) and Industry 4.0 sensor arrays would be prohibitively expensive.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks about "how to reduce cost of a DAS," the answer **always** starts with **"by using a multiplexer in front of a single ADC."** This is a guaranteed 2-mark short note for KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Scan Time of a Multiplexed DAS

**Problem Setup:** A 16-channel multiplexed DAS uses a single **12-bit SAR ADC**. Each channel has a per-channel acquisition-conversion time of **t_ch = 25 µs**. Determine:
(a) Total scan time for one complete sweep
(b) Effective sampling rate per channel
(c) The maximum input signal frequency that can be digitized without aliasing.

**Given Data:**
- Number of channels, **N = 16**
- Per-channel time, **t_ch = 25 µs = 25 × 10⁻⁶ s**
- ADC resolution, **n = 12 bits**
- Nyquist criterion, **f_s ≥ 2 × f_max**

---

**Part (a) — Total Scan Time Derivation:**

The scan time is the sum of the per-channel times across all N channels, because the multiplexer visits each channel sequentially in one sweep:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{\text{ch},\,i} \\
&= N \cdot t_{\text{ch}} \quad \text{(assuming uniform acquisition time per channel)} \\
&= 16 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \\
&= 400 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \\
&= 400\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** Each channel is sampled once every 400 µs. The total scan cycle is 400 microseconds for all 16 inputs combined.

---

**Part (b) — Effective Sampling Rate per Channel:**

The effective sampling rate per individual channel equals the inverse of the total scan time, since one sample per channel is captured per scan:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{400 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}} \\
&= \frac{10^{6}}{400}\,\text{Hz} \\
&= 2500\,\text{samples/sec/channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** Even though the shared ADC is converting at 40,000 conversions/sec overall, each *individual* channel only sees a sample at 2.5 kHz — the per-channel throughput is divided by N.

---

**Part (c) — Maximum Input Signal Frequency (Nyquist Limit):**

Applying the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem to the *per-channel* effective rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{max}} &\le \frac{f_{\text{eff}}}{2} \\
&= \frac{2500}{2} \\
&= 1250\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** Any analog signal containing frequency components **above 1.25 kHz** will violate the Nyquist criterion and produce **aliasing artifacts** in this multiplexed system.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ADC Quantization (LSB) Size

**Given:** V_ref = **10 V**, n = **12 bits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB} &= \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n} \\
&= \frac{10\,\text{V}}{2^{12}} \\
&= \frac{10}{4096}\,\text{V} \\
&\approx 2.4414\,\text{mV per LSB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** The smallest analog voltage difference the ADC can resolve is approximately **2.44 mV**. Any input change smaller than this will be invisible to the digital output.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Multiplexed vs. Simultaneous-Sampling DAS

| Parameter | Multiplexed DAS (Single ADC) | Simultaneous-Sampling DAS (Multi-ADC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost per Channel** | Low | High |
| **ADC Count** | 1 (shared) | N (one per channel) |
| **Inter-Channel Skew** | High (sequential delay) | Zero (parallel sampling) |
| **Throughput per Channel** | Low (f_eff = f_ADC / N) | High (equal to f_ADC) |
| **Suitable Signal Bandwidth** | DC to ~kHz range | DC to MHz range |
| **Anti-Aliasing Filter** | One shared filter, placed after MUX | One dedicated filter per channel |
| **Board Space / PCB Area** | Minimal | Large |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (single ADC active) | High (N ADCs active) |
| **Typical Application** | Temperature/Pressure/Level monitoring (slow signals) | Vibration analysis, Power system transient capture (fast signals) |
| **KTU Exam Buzzword** | "Economical multi-channel DAS" | "High-fidelity, low-skew DAS" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A common 14-mark question compares these two architectures and asks the student to *justify* the choice. The standard answer template is: **"Choose multiplexed for slow, cost-sensitive, high-channel-count process control; choose simultaneous for fast transient or phase-critical measurements."**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multiplexed DAS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["CH1 Sensor"] --> muxA
    srcB["CH2 Sensor"] --> muxA
    srcC["CH3 Sensor"] --> muxA
    srcD["CHN Sensor"] --> muxA
    muxA["Analog MUX 8:1 or 16:1"] --> filterA
    filterA["Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter"] --> shA
    shA["Sample and Hold Amplifier"] --> adcA
    adcA["Shared SAR ADC 12-bit / 16-bit"] --> ctlA
    ctlA["PLC CPU or DAS Controller"]
    ctlA --> addrBus["Address Lines S0 S1 S2"]
    addrBus --> muxA
    ctlA --> memMap["Memory Mapped Result Register"]
    memMap --> scada["SCADA / HMI Display"]
```
