
**Block-Level Interpretation:**

- **CH1 ... CHN Sensors:** Field devices (RTD, thermocouple, 4–20 mA transmitter, strain gauge).
- **Analog MUX:** The CD4051/CD4067 IC that sequentially selects one of N inputs.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Critical RC or active low-pass filter. **Must precede the sampler** by the sampling theorem's requirement.
- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Freezes the rapidly switching channel voltage so the ADC sees a stable DC during conversion.
- **SAR ADC:** The shared converter — only one exists in the entire system.
- **Controller:** Provides the binary select address AND clocks the ADC.
- **Memory-Mapped Register:** Stores the converted digital word, addressable by the CPU.
- **SCADA/HMI:** The supervisory layer where the digital value is scaled into engineering units (e.g., °C, bar).

---

### 4.2 Multiplexer Scanning Sequence Timing Diagram (Conceptual Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as PLC CPU
    participant MUX as Analog Multiplexer
    participant S_H as Sample and Hold
    participant ADC as SAR ADC
    participant MEM as Memory Register

    Note over CPU: Set Address = 000 (Channel 1)
    CPU->>MUX: Address 000 on S2 S1 S0
    MUX->>S_H: Pass CH1 analog voltage
    Note over S_H: Settling time t_settle ~ 2 microseconds
    S_H->>S_H: Hold CH1 voltage constant
    S_H->>ADC: Apply held voltage
    Note over ADC: Conversion takes 25 microseconds
    ADC->>MEM: Store 12-bit digital code for CH1

    Note over CPU: Set Address = 001 (Channel 2)
    CPU->>MUX: Address 001 on S2 S1 S0
    MUX->>S_H: Pass CH2 analog voltage
    S_H->>ADC: Apply held voltage
    ADC->>MEM: Store 12-bit digital code for CH2

    Note over CPU: Repeat for all N channels sequentially
    Note over CPU: After channel N, restart from Channel 1
```

**Timing Interpretation:**

This sequence illustrates the **cyclic, round-robin nature** of multiplexed channel operation. The CPU orchestrates the channel selection, and the per-channel cycle of `select → settle → hold → convert → store` repeats N times before the entire frame restarts.

---

### 4.3 Selector Logic Truth Table for a 4-Channel (2-Line) MUX

| S1 | S0 | Selected Channel | Decimal Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | CH0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | CH1 | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | CH2 | 2 |
| 1 | 1 | CH3 | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization Rule:** For an **m-line** selector input, the multiplexer can address **N = 2ᵐ** channels. KTU frequently tests this — e.g., "How many selector lines are required for a 32-channel MUX?" → $m = \log_2 32 = 5$ lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define multiplexed channel operation in a Data Acquisition System. Mention any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Multiplexed channel operation is a technique in which **multiple analog input channels are sequentially connected to a single shared Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** through an analog multiplexer (MUX). The multiplexer is controlled by binary address lines from the controller, and only one channel is connected to the ADC at any given instant.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Cost Reduction:** Only one (expensive, high-precision) ADC is required to service N input channels, drastically reducing the per-channel hardware cost.
2. **Compact PCB Design:** The shared ADC + single anti-aliasing filter minimizes board space and power consumption.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem and explain its relevance in a multiplexed DAS.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem** states that an analog signal containing frequency components up to a maximum frequency **f_max** must be sampled at a rate **f_s** that is **at least twice** the maximum frequency:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}
$$

**Relevance in Multiplexed DAS:** In a multiplexed system, the per-channel sampling rate is reduced by a factor of N (number of channels). Therefore, the effective per-channel rate **f_eff = f_ADC / N** must still satisfy the Nyquist criterion. If not, **aliasing** will occur, causing high-frequency components to appear as false lower-frequency signals in the digitized output.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a multiplexed Data Acquisition System. Identify each block and state its function.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A multiplexed DAS uses a single 12-bit ADC with a conversion time of **20 µs per channel**. The system monitors **32 channels**. Calculate:
(i) Total scan time for one complete sweep.
(ii) Effective sampling rate per channel.
(iii) The highest input signal frequency that can be accurately digitized without aliasing.

---

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete Mermaid architecture. The student must reproduce a labeled block diagram with the following seven essential blocks:

| # | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Multiple Sensors / Transducers (CH1 to CHN) | Convert physical quantities to electrical signals |
| 2 | Analog Multiplexer (MUX) | Selects one of N inputs sequentially based on address lines |
| 3 | Signal Conditioning Amplifier | Amplifies low-level sensor outputs to ADC input range |
| 4 | Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter | Bandlimits signal to ≤ f_s/2 to prevent aliasing |
| 5 | Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Amplifier | Holds channel voltage steady during ADC conversion |
| 6 | Shared ADC (12-bit SAR) | Converts analog sample to digital code |
| 7 | Controller (PLC CPU / Microcontroller) | Generates MUX address, clocks ADC, stores result |

Each block must be **named, drawn, and its function stated explicitly** to score full marks.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** n = 12 bits, t_ch = 20 µs, N = 32 channels.

**(i) Total Scan Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= N \cdot t_{\text{ch}} \\
&= 32 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \\
&= 640 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \\
&= 640\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Effective Sampling Rate per Channel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{640 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 1562.5\,\text{samples/sec/channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Unaliased Signal Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{max}} &= \frac{f_{\text{eff}}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1562.5}{2} \\
&= 781.25\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:** T_scan = **640 µs**, f_eff = **1562.5 Hz**, f_max = **781.25 Hz**.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare multiplexed and simultaneous-sampling DAS architectures. Under what application conditions would you prefer each?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the number of channels that can be addressed by an analog multiplexer with *m* selector lines. If a process plant DAS requires monitoring **64 temperature channels**, determine:
(i) The number of selector lines required.
(ii) The total number of multiplexer ICs needed if each MUX IC is an 8:1 type.

---

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.3 (Analytical Comparison Matrix)** of these notes. The student must produce a tabular comparison covering **at least six parameters**: cost per channel, ADC count, inter-channel skew (time delay between samples), throughput, complexity, and suitable application domain.

**Application Selection Rule:**
- **Multiplexed DAS** is preferred when the number of channels is **large (N > 16)**, the signals are **slowly varying (DC to low kHz)**, **cost is a primary constraint**, and **inter-channel skew is tolerable**. Example: **Chemical plant temperature/level monitoring**.
- **Simultaneous-Sampling DAS** is preferred when signals are **fast transients**, **phase relationships between channels must be preserved**, and **high bandwidth (>100 kHz) is required**. Example: **Power system fault recording, vibration modal analysis**.

---

**(b) Derivation and Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Derivation of N = 2ᵐ:**

An analog multiplexer with *m* selector lines accepts binary addresses ranging from `0...0` to `1...1`. The total number of distinct binary combinations (and hence distinct selectable channels) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{channels}} &= 2^m
\end{aligned}
$$

where $m$ is the number of binary selector inputs. Each combination uniquely addresses one input line out of the $2^m$ available.

**Given:** N = 64 channels.

**(i) Number of Selector Lines:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
64 &= 2^m \\
\log_2(64) &= m \\
m &= 6\,\text{selector lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of 8:1 MUX ICs Required:**

Each 8:1 MUX handles 8 channels. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of ICs} &= \frac{N}{8} \\
&= \frac{64}{8} \\
&= 8\,\text{multiplexer ICs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:** m = **6 selector lines**, Total 8:1 MUX ICs = **8 ICs**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Multiplexed-Channel Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing f_ADC (overall ADC rate) with f_eff (per-channel rate):** Many students write `f_s = 1/t_ch` instead of `f_eff = 1/(N·t_ch)`. This is a **2-mark error** in calculation-based questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the "settling time"** between channel switches in timing diagrams. The MUX output takes ~1–5 µs to settle to a new voltage; omitting this loses **1 mark** in timing-sketch questions.
> 3. **Drawing the anti-aliasing filter AFTER the ADC instead of BEFORE the sampler.** The filter must bandlimit the analog signal *before* sampling — placing it after the ADC is meaningless. This is a **favourite KTU trap** worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Not labeling the selector lines (S0, S1, S2)** in the block diagram. KTU examiners deduct marks for un-labeled address inputs.
> 5. **Writing "multiplexer" when "demultiplexer" is needed** in DAC reconstruction systems — pay attention to direction of signal flow.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Multiplexing = Time-sharing of one ADC across N input channels** via an electronically addressed MUX switch.
- [x] **Channel count formula:** $N = 2^m$ where $m$ = number of binary selector lines.
- [x] **Total scan time:** $T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot t_{\text{ch}}$ — **not** $t_{\text{ch}}$ alone.
- [x] **Per-channel sampling rate:** $f_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot t_{\text{ch}}}$ — this is the **effective** sampling rate seen by each individual channel.
- [x] **Nyquist limit:** $f_{\text{max}} \leq \dfrac{f_{\text{eff}}}{2}$ — multiply by **0.5**, not by any other factor.
- [x] **ADC LSB size:** $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}$ — memorize this; it appears in **every** DAS numerical.
- [x] **Anti-aliasing filter MUST precede the sampler** (i.e., placed between MUX and S/H or between sensor and MUX).
- [x] **Sample-and-Hold is mandatory** in multiplexed systems to prevent channel-to-channel switching transients from corrupting ADC conversion.
- [x] **Multiplexed DAS is cheap but slow; simultaneous-sampling DAS is fast but expensive** — remember this trade-off for 7-mark comparison questions.
- [x] **Typical industrial multiplexer ICs:** CD4051 (8:1), CD4067 (16:1), ADG726 (dual 16:1).
- [x] **Settling time** of MUX output must be considered in high-precision applications (1 LSB accuracy takes ~$\tau \ln(2^n)$).
- [x] **Aliasing** occurs if input signal bandwidth exceeds $f_{\text{eff}}/2$ — always state this when discussing limitations.
- [x] **One multiplexer per signal direction** — separate MUX for inputs, DEMUX for outputs (in PLC analog I/O modules).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microprocessor/PC Based Acquisition Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT861 - PLC AND DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microprocessor/PC Based Acquisition Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microprocessor/PC Based Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is an electronic system that uses a **microprocessor** (or a **Personal Computer** as the host) as the central processing and control unit to acquire, condition, convert, process, store, and display real-world analog and digital signals from sensors and transducers.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this topic sits at the intersection of **embedded systems**, **instrumentation**, and **computer interfacing**, where the intelligence of the system is migrated from hard-wired logic to software-driven programmable architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Smart Health Monitor**
> Imagine a smart ECG machine in a hospital. The patient's heart produces tiny electrical signals (analog). A microprocessor-based DAS acts like a **multilingual translator + secretary**:
> - **Sensors** = Stethoscope (captures raw signal)
> - **Signal Conditioning** = Amplifier (makes the whisper loud enough to hear)
> - **ADC** = A scribe converting whispers into written words (analog → digital)
> - **Microprocessor/PC** = The doctor analyzing the words and making a diagnosis
> - **Display/Storage** = Medical record file

Without the microprocessor, every function would require a separate hardware circuit. With it, **one chip + software does the work of hundreds of components**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Microprocessor/PC Based DAS

| Metric | Typical Range / Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **ADC Resolution** | **8-bit to 24-bit** | Determines smallest detectable signal change |
| **Sampling Rate** | **1 kS/s to 1 GS/s** | Samples per second capability |
| **CPU Clock Frequency** | **1 MHz to 5 GHz** | Processing throughput |
| **Bus Width** | **8/16/32/64-bit** | Data transfer granularity |
| **Memory (RAM)** | **4 GB to 64 GB** | Temporary data buffering |
| **Storage** | **500 GB to 4 TB SSD** | Long-term archival |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes that the *intelligence* of a modern DAS is not in the hardware alone, but in the **firmware/driver-software layer** running on the microprocessor or PC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Hierarchy of a Microprocessor-Based DAS

A microprocessor-based DAS is organized in **five functional layers**. Each layer is essential and must be understood in sequence.

**Layer 1 — Sensor / Transducer Interface**
- Converts physical quantities (temperature, pressure, strain, flow) into electrical signals.
- Output types: **Voltage (mV to V)**, **Current (4–20 mA)**, **Resistance (RTD)**, **Frequency (Hz)**.
- Critical concept: *Many sensors output non-standard levels requiring normalization before ADC*.

**Layer 2 — Signal Conditioning Circuit**
- Performs **Amplification**, **Filtering**, **Isolation**, **Linearization**, and **Multiplexing**.
- Most common configuration uses the **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** topology because of its high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** typically > **100 dB**.

**Layer 3 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**
- Converts conditioned analog signals to digital words.
- Key parameters: **Resolution (n bits)**, **Sampling Rate (f_s)**, **Input Range (V_in)**, **Quantization Error**.

**Layer 4 — Microprocessor / PC Processing Unit**
- Executes control algorithms, computes engineering units, manages memory, handles communication.
- In **microprocessor-based** systems: 8085/8086/ARM Cortex-M.
- In **PC-based** systems: x86/x64 CPU running OS (Windows/Linux/RTOS).

**Layer 5 — Output / Communication Interface**
- Displays (LCD/VGA/HDMI), Storage (HDD/SSD/Flash), Networking (Ethernet/RS-485/USB/CAN).

### 2.2 Why a Microprocessor is the "Brain" — The Core "How"

> [!NOTE]
> The microprocessor replaces **discrete logic** (AND/OR gates, flip-flops) with **sequential software instructions**. This brings three KTU-examined advantages:
> 1. **Flexibility**: Behavior can be changed by rewriting code without altering hardware.
> 2. **Scalability**: Same hardware can handle more channels by software multiplexing.
> 3. **Self-Diagnostics**: Built-in test (BIT) routines can detect sensor/ADC faults.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU University Exams. Memorize the variables and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Voltage resolution (Least Significant Bit) | Volts; $V_{FS}$ = Full-scale range, $n$ = ADC bits |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Quantization step (when $V_{FS}$ is true peak-to-peak) | Volts |
| 3 | $e_q = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$ | Maximum quantization error | Volts |
| 4 | $f_{Nyquist} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 5 | $SNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio of ideal ADC | dB |
| 6 | $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Effective Number of Bits | bits |
| 7 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Reconstructed digital code to voltage | Volts |
| 8 | $T_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Conversion time per sample | seconds |
| 9 | $V_{out,IA} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)}{1}$ | Instrumentation amplifier output (3-IA topology) | Volts |
| 10 | $t_{acq} = (n+1) \cdot T_{clk}$ | SAR ADC acquisition time | seconds |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microprocessor/PC-based DAS is the **backbone of modern Industry 4.0**. Applications include:
- **SCADA systems** in power plants
- **Environmental monitoring stations** (weather, pollution)
- **Automotive diagnostics** (OBD-II systems)
- **Biomedical instrumentation** (ECG, EEG, EMG monitors)
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** of bridges and buildings
- **Test & Measurement** in R&D laboratories

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolution and Quantization Error of an ADC

**Statement:** A 12-bit ADC operates with a full-scale input range of **0 V to 10 V**. Find the voltage represented by 1 LSB, the maximum quantization error, and the theoretical SNR.

**Given:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- $V_{FS} = 10$ V

**Step 1: Voltage resolution (1 LSB)**

The number of discrete digital codes an $n$-bit ADC can produce is $2^n$.

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{LSB} = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.4414 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum quantization error**

The worst-case quantization error is half of one LSB, because any analog value can fall midway between two adjacent codes.

$$
e_q = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \dfrac{2.4414 \text{ mV}}{2} = 1.2207 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3: Theoretical SNR**

For an ideal ADC, the signal-to-noise ratio (in dB) is governed solely by the resolution.

$$
SNR = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation**

A 12-bit ADC with 10 V FS can detect voltage changes as small as **2.44 mV**, with maximum uncertainty of **±1.22 mV**. This is the dynamic range floor — for higher precision, increase $n$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Sampling Theorem Application

**Statement:** A vibration sensor outputs a sinusoidal signal whose highest frequency component is **5 kHz**. Determine the **minimum sampling frequency** that guarantees faithful reconstruction, and compute the maximum allowable sampling interval.

**Given:**
- $f_{max} = 5 \text{ kHz} = 5000 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 1: Apply the Nyquist criterion**

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 5000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the maximum sampling period**

$$
T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{1}{10{,}000} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3: Engineering safety margin (KTU standard practice)**

In real systems, the sampling rate is chosen as **2.5× to 5×** the Nyquist rate to accommodate anti-aliasing filter roll-off and to provide a transition band.

$$
f_{s, design} = 4 \cdot f_{max} = 4 \cdot 5000 = 20 \text{ kHz}
$$

This is the rate the KTU answer key would accept as "engineered design."

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Digital Code to Engineering Unit Conversion

**Statement:** A pressure transducer outputs **4–20 mA** corresponding to **0–10 bar**. A 16-bit ADC reads a digital code of **N = 45,000**. Compute the measured pressure in bar.

**Step 1: Convert ADC digital code to voltage**

For a unipolar ADC with reference $V_{ref} = 5$ V and $n = 16$ bits:

$$
V_{in} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{45{,}000 \cdot 5}{65{,}536} \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{in} = \dfrac{225{,}000}{65{,}536} = 3.4332 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Convert ADC voltage to loop current**

The current-sense resistor is typically $R_{sense} = 250 \, \Omega$, so:

$$
I_{loop} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_{sense}} = \dfrac{3.4332}{250} = 0.013733 \text{ A} = 13.733 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3: Linearize to engineering units (pressure)**

For a 4–20 mA → 0–10 bar transducer, the linear transfer function is:

$$
P = \dfrac{(I - 4)}{(20 - 4)} \cdot 10 = \dfrac{(13.733 - 4)}{16} \cdot 10
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{9.733}{16} \cdot 10 = 0.6083 \cdot 10 = 6.083 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 4: Verification**

- 4 mA → 0 bar ✓
- 20 mA → 10 bar ✓
- 13.733 mA is approximately **61%** of span → ~6.08 bar ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) from Measured SNR

**Statement:** A 14-bit ADC is tested. Its measured SNR is **78 dB**. Compute the **ENOB** and comment on its deviation from the ideal.

**Step 1: Apply the ENOB formula**

$$
ENOB = \dfrac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02} = \dfrac{78 - 1.76}{6.02} = \dfrac{76.24}{6.02}
$$

$$
ENOB = 12.66 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Compute ideal SNR for the 14-bit ADC**

$$
SNR_{ideal} = 6.02 \cdot 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

The ADC performs approximately **1.34 bits below ideal** — this is due to thermal noise, clock jitter, and integral non-linearity. KTU examiners expect students to state that **real ADCs always perform below their nominal bit count**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generalized Microprocessor-Based DAS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phys[Physical Quantity] --> sens[Sensor / Transducer]
    sens --> mux[Analog Multiplexer MUX]
    mux --> ia[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    ia --> filt[Anti Aliasing Filter LPF]
    filt --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    adc --> mpu[Microprocessor 8086 / ARM]
    mpu --> mem[Memory RAM ROM]
    mpu --> dio[Digital IO Ports]
    mpu --> disp[Display LCD VGA]
    mpu --> comm[Communication UART USB Ethernet]
    mpu --> store[Storage HDD SSD Flash]
    pwr[Power Supply SMPS Linear] -.-> sens
    pwr -.-> ia
    pwr -.-> adc
    pwr -.-> mpu
    clk[Crystal Clock Oscillator] -.-> mpu
    clk -.-> adc
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right):**
1. Physical quantity → sensor (transduction).
2. Sensor → MUX (channel selection; one ADC serves many sensors).
3. MUX → IA (amplifies low-level signals, rejects common-mode noise).
4. IA → LPF (anti-aliasing, removes frequencies above Nyquist limit).
5. LPF → ADC (analog → digital conversion).
6. ADC → Microprocessor (digital processing, scaling, logging).
7. Microprocessor branches to memory, display, communication, and storage.
8. Power supply and clock oscillator provide *global infrastructure* to all active blocks.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — PC-Based Data Acquisition System (Plug-in Card Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph field[FIELD SIDE]
        S1[Thermocouple TC]
        S2[Strain Gauge SG]
        S3[Flow Meter FM]
        S4[Pressure Transmitter PT]
    end
    subgraph sigcon[SIGNAL CONDITIONING PANEL SCXI SCC]
        SC1[Cold Junction Comp]
        SC2[Wheatstone Bridge]
        SC3[F to V Converter]
        SC4[4 to 20 mA Receiver]
    end
    subgraph daq[DASDAQ PLUG IN CARD]
        MUX2[Analog Multiplexer]
        PGAA[Programmable Gain Amp]
        ADC2[ADC 16 bit]
        FIFO[FIFO Buffer]
        CTRL[DMA Controller]
        PCIB[PCI PCIe Bus Interface]
    end
    subgraph pc[PC HOST COMPUTER]
        CPU[x86 x64 CPU]
        DRAM[DDR RAM]
        OS[Operating System]
        DRV[Device Driver]
        APP[Application Software LabVIEW MATLAB]
        HDD[Hard Disk SSD]
        MON[Monitor]
    end
    S1 --> SC1
    S2 --> SC2
    S3 --> SC3
    S4 --> SC4
    SC1 --> MUX2
    SC2 --> MUX2
    SC3 --> MUX2
    SC4 --> MUX2
    MUX2 --> PGAA
    PGAA --> ADC2
    ADC2 --> FIFO
    FIFO --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PCIB
    PCIB --> CPU
    CPU --> DRAM
    CPU --> OS
    OS --> DRV
    DRV --> APP
    APP --> HDD
    APP --> MON
```

**Architecture Explanation (KTU-Exam-Relevant):**
- **Field Side**: Real-world sensors. TC (mV), SG (mV), FM (Hz), PT (mA).
- **Signal Conditioning**: Specialized per sensor type — CJC for thermocouples, bridge completion for strain gauges, F-to-V for flow meters, current-loop receiver for 4–20 mA.
- **DAQ Plug-in Card (PCI/PCIe)**: Sits inside the PC. The MUX → PGA → ADC → FIFO → DMA → PCIe pipeline ensures **high-throughput, CPU-offloaded acquisition**.
- **PC Host**: Provides massive memory, OS-level multitasking, GUI, and third-party software (LabVIEW, MATLAB, Python) for visualization and analysis.

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Acquisition Cycle Timing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([System Power On]) --> init[Initialize CPU Peripherals]
    init --> chsel[Select Channel via MUX]
    chsel --> setgain[Set PGA Gain]
    setgain --> wait[Wait for Settling Time ts]
    wait --> startconv[Issue SOC to ADC]
    startconv --> conv[ADC Conversion in Progress]
    conv --> eoc{EOC Flag Set}
    eoc -- no --> conv
    eoc -- yes --> read[Read Digital Code via Bus]
    read --> scale[Convert to Engineering Unit]
    scale --> store[Store in RAM Buffer]
    store --> chk{All Channels Done}
    chk -- no --> chsel
    chk -- yes --> disp[Update Display Send to Host]
    disp --> chk2{Continuous Mode}
    chk2 -- yes --> chsel
    chk2 -- no --> stop([Stop Acquisition])
```

This timing diagram illustrates the **sequential polling architecture** — the most common structure in low-to-medium speed DAS. KTU examiners frequently ask for such flowcharts under the "block diagram" question.

---

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Microprocessor vs PC-Based DAS

| Parameter | Microprocessor-Based DAS | PC-Based DAS |
|---|---|---|
| **Processor** | 8085/8086/ARM Cortex | x86/x64 Desktop CPU |
| **Memory** | KB to a few MB | GB scale (DDR) |
| **Storage** | Limited Flash (MB) | HDD/SSD (TB) |
| **Display** | 16x2 LCD / 7" TFT | Full HD/4K Monitor |
| **Sampling Rate** | Up to ~1 MS/s (typical) | Up to ~10 GS/s (high-end) |
| **Software** | Firmware (C/Assembly) | OS + Drivers + Apps |
| **Cost** | Low to Medium | Medium to High |
| **Portability** | Excellent (embedded) | Limited (bench/lab) |
| **Multitasking** | Limited (RTOS optional) | Full preemptive multitasking |
| **Typical Use** | Field instruments, IoT edge | Lab automation, R&D, SCADA |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Define a Data Acquisition System. List the basic functional blocks of a microprocessor-based DAS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is a collection of hardware and software elements that work together to measure, condition, convert, and record real-world physical quantities in a form suitable for storage, display, and analysis.

The **basic functional blocks** of a microprocessor-based DAS are:
1. **Sensors / Transducers** — convert physical signals to electrical.
2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — amplification, filtering, isolation.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — time-shares one ADC across multiple channels.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts analog to digital code.
5. **Microprocessor (CPU)** — central intelligence, executes control software.
6. **Memory** — RAM (temporary) and ROM/Flash (firmware).
7. **Display & Output Interface** — LCD, indicators, communication ports.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 4+ correct blocks.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**What is the role of an Instrumentation Amplifier in a microprocessor-based DAS? Why is it preferred over a simple op-amp difference amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** is the first active stage after the sensor in a DAS. Its roles are:
1. **Amplifies small differential signals** (typically mV-range) from sensors like thermocouples and strain gauges to a level suitable for the ADC.
2. **Rejects common-mode noise** (e.g., 50 Hz mains interference) — quantified by its high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR > 100 dB)**.
3. **Provides high input impedance** to avoid loading the sensor.

It is **preferred over a simple op-amp difference amplifier** because:
- The IA has **symmetric high-impedance inputs** (no loading imbalance).
- Gain is set by **one external resistor** without disturbing the matched resistor network (which would degrade CMRR).
- It offers **superior CMRR, lower offset, and lower noise** — critical for low-level sensor signals.

*(1 mark for roles + 2 marks for justification with one CMRR/value.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** by drawing block diagrams without *arrow direction* and *power/clock annotations*.
> - Marks are also lost for **confusing** sampling rate $f_s$ with signal frequency $f$ or using the wrong Nyquist factor (2× vs 4×).
> - Always **state the units** explicitly in your final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
> - In derivation problems, do **not skip algebraic steps**; even minor jumps invite partial marking penalties.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **microprocessor-based Data Acquisition System**. Discuss the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 16-bit ADC has a full-scale input range of **±10 V**. Compute:
   (i) The voltage represented by **1 LSB**.
   (ii) The **maximum quantization error**.
   (iii) The **theoretical SNR** in dB.
   (iv) The **ENOB** if the measured SNR is **92 dB**, and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The architecture of a microprocessor-based DAS consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Sensors/Transducers** (e.g., thermocouple, RTD, LVDT): Convert physical quantities (temperature, displacement, pressure) into electrical signals (mV, V, mA, or $\Omega$).

2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit**: Includes:
   - **Amplification** (Instrumentation Amplifier) to boost low-level signals.
   - **Filtering** (low-pass anti-aliasing filter) to limit bandwidth.
   - **Isolation** (opto-coupler or transformer) to break ground loops.
   - **Linearization** to correct non-linear sensor characteristics.

3. **Analog Multiplexer (MUX)**: Selects one of $N$ input channels sequentially under microprocessor control. This allows one ADC to serve many channels, reducing cost.

4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit**: Freezes the analog voltage during conversion to avoid aperture error.

5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**: Performs the A-to-D conversion using successive approximation, flash, or sigma-delta techniques. Output is a parallel/serial digital word.

6. **Microprocessor (CPU)**: The brain — fetches instructions from ROM, reads the ADC output, performs scaling/linearization, manages memory, and updates outputs.

7. **Memory**:
   - **ROM/Flash**: Stores firmware.
   - **RAM**: Buffers acquired data.
   - **EEPROM**: Stores calibration constants.

8. **Display & Output**: LCD/LED displays, keyboard for operator interface, communication ports (UART, USB) for host PC link.

9. **Clock & Power Supply**: Crystal oscillator provides timing; regulated SMPS provides $\pm 5$ V, $\pm 12$ V, +3.3 V rails.

The **microprocessor** orchestrates the entire sequence: select channel → trigger S/H → start ADC → wait for EOC → read code → store → repeat.

*(Block diagram 3 marks + explanation of 5+ blocks 4 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $n = 16$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10$ V (unipolar interpretation; for $\pm 10$ V bipolar, full range is 20 V but per-bit resolution is computed using the same $V_{FS}/2^n$).

**(i) Voltage per LSB (1 mark):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10}{2^{16}} = \dfrac{10}{65{,}536} = 1.5259 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 152.59 \text{ µV}
$$

**(ii) Maximum quantization error (2 marks):**

$$
e_q = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \dfrac{152.59 \text{ µV}}{2} = 76.29 \text{ µV}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical SNR (2 marks):**

$$
SNR_{ideal} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iv) ENOB (2 marks):**

$$
ENOB = \dfrac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02} = \dfrac{92 - 1.76}{6.02} = \dfrac{90.24}{6.02} = 14.99 \approx 15 \text{ bits}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The ADC performs approximately **1 bit below its nominal 16-bit rating**, which is excellent — this loss is attributable to clock jitter and thermal noise. Such performance is typical of high-quality 16-bit sigma-delta ADCs used in precision instrumentation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **microprocessor-based** and **PC-based** Data Acquisition Systems. Discuss the role of:
   - Signal conditioning
   - ADC resolution
   - Software/firmware layer
   - Communication interfaces
   in each architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vibration sensor outputs a sinusoidal signal with maximum frequency $f_{max} = 8$ kHz. The DAS uses an ADC with $n = 14$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V. Determine:
   (i) The minimum sampling rate as per the Nyquist criterion.
   (ii) The recommended sampling rate with a 4× safety factor.
   (iii) The voltage resolution (1 LSB) and the maximum quantization error.
   (iv) The maximum quantization error as a **percentage of full scale**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Aspect | Microprocessor-Based DAS | PC-Based DAS |
|---|---|---|
| **Processor** | 8085/8086/ARM Cortex-M | x86/x64 Desktop CPU |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Onboard dedicated analog front-end | External SCXI/SCC chassis + plug-in card front-end |
| **ADC Resolution** | Typically 12–16 bit | Typically 16–24 bit (high-end cards) |
| **Software Layer** | Bare-metal firmware (C/Assembly) + optional RTOS | Full OS (Windows/Linux) + Device Driver + Application (LabVIEW/MATLAB) |
| **Communication** | UART, SPI, I²C, CAN | USB, Ethernet, PCIe, GPIB |
| **Memory** | KB–MB | GB scale |
| **Storage** | Flash (MB) | HDD/SSD (TB) |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| **Portability** | Excellent (field-deployable) | Limited (lab/bench) |
| **Real-time Determinism** | High (deterministic) | Lower (OS scheduling latency) |

**Key role discussions:**
- *Signal conditioning*: In both, it is the sensor-protection and noise-rejection stage. PC-based systems often externalize it to a separate chassis (e.g., NI SCXI) for higher channel counts and better isolation.
- *ADC resolution*: PC-based cards push to 24-bit for high-dynamic-range applications (audio, vibration); microprocessor boards typically stay at 12–16 bit due to cost.
- *Software*: PC systems benefit from rich libraries, GUI builders, and cloud connectivity; microprocessor systems win in real-time determinism and low power.
- *Communication*: PC uses standard PC buses (PCIe); microprocessor uses low-pin-count serial buses.

*(Comparison table 4 marks + role discussion 3 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $f_{max} = 8$ kHz, $n = 14$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V.

**(i) Minimum sampling rate — Nyquist (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_{s,min} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 8000 = 16 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Recommended sampling rate with 4× safety factor (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_{s,design} = 4 \cdot f_{max} = 4 \cdot 8000 = 32 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(iii) Voltage resolution and quantization error (2 marks):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5}{16{,}384} = 3.0518 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 305.18 \text{ µV}
$$

$$
e_q = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} = 152.59 \text{ µV}
$$

**(iv) Percentage of full scale (2 marks):**

$$
\% e_q = \dfrac{e_q}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \dfrac{152.59 \text{ µV}}{5 \text{ V}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% e_q = 3.0518 \times 10^{-3} \% \approx 0.00305 \%
$$

**Final Comment:** Such a low quantization error percentage confirms that a 14-bit ADC is well suited for vibration analysis up to 8 kHz.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Microprocessor/PC Based Acquisition Systems**

- ✅ **DAS = Sensor + Signal Conditioning + MUX + ADC + Microprocessor/PC + Display + Storage**.
- ✅ **Microprocessor-based** = embedded, deterministic, low-power, real-time firmware.
- ✅ **PC-based** = plug-in DAQ card (PCI/PCIe/USB) + host CPU + OS + application software.
- ✅ **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** is mandatory for low-level (mV) sensor signals — provides high CMRR (>100 dB) and high input impedance.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** must be a low-pass filter with cutoff at or below the Nyquist frequency.
- ✅ **ADC Resolution formula**: $V_{LSB} = V_{FS} / 2^n$ — a 12-bit ADC with 10 V FS gives **2.44 mV/LSB**.
- ✅ **Maximum quantization error** is $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- ✅ **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; in practice, use $4 f_{max}$ for safety.
- ✅ **Theoretical SNR (ideal ADC)**: $6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- ✅ **ENOB formula**: $(SNR_{measured} - 1.76) / 6.02$ — always less than nominal $n$ for real ADCs.
- ✅ **MUX** allows one expensive ADC to serve many cheap sensors — common KTU block-diagram feature.
- ✅ **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** prevents aperture error during conversion.
- ✅ **Current loop (4–20 mA)** is industry-standard for noise-immune field transmission.
- ✅ **Linearization equation** for 4–20 mA: $P = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times \text{span}$ — memorize this form.
- ✅ **PC-based DAS** advantages: massive storage, GUI, multitasking, third-party software.
- ✅ **Disadvantage of PC-based DAS**: OS-induced latency reduces real-time determinism.
- ✅ Always **state units** in numerical answers — KTU valuation penalizes missing units.
- ✅ Always **annotate arrows** in block diagrams — direction, signal type, and power rails.
